topics.title: Concept of Structure and Function of DNA; Identification of the Genetic Material - Classical Experiments (Griffith, Avery, McLeod, Hershey and Chase); Central Dogma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Structure and Function of DNA; Identification of the Genetic Material - Classical Experiments (Griffith, Avery, McLeod, Hershey and Chase); Central Dogma

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Concept and Definition of DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid)

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA) is a double-helical, antiparallel, polynucleotide macromolecule composed of deoxyribonucleotide monomers. Each monomer consists of a **deoxyribose sugar**, a **phosphate group** (forming the sugar-phosphate backbone), and one of four **nitrogenous bases** — two purines (Adenine, Guanine) and two pyrimidines (Cytosine, Thymine). DNA functions as the primary genetic material in all cellular organisms and many viruses, encoding the instructions required for the development, functioning, growth, and reproduction of all known living organisms and many viruses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DNA is the *genetic material* responsible for **heredity**, **information storage**, and **expression of traits** through the Central Dogma flow: DNA → RNA → Protein.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" of Life

Think of a cell as a massive, fully automated factory. The DNA is the **master architectural blueprint locked inside the manager's safe**. Every machine (protein), every worker (enzyme), and every product the factory makes is described on a specific page of that blueprint. The blueprint is copied (replication) when the factory divides, specific pages are photocopied as working instructions (transcription → mRNA), and those copies are read by ribosomes (the factory's assembly line) to build proteins (translation).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Helix diameter | **2 nm (20 Å)** |
| Distance per base pair | **0.34 nm (3.4 Å)** |
| Base pairs per complete turn (B-DNA) | **10.5 bp/turn** |
| Pitch of helix (one complete turn) | **3.4 nm (34 Å)** |
| Number of hydrogen bonds A=T | **2** |
| Number of hydrogen bonds G≡C | **3** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The Watson-Crick B-form of DNA is a **right-handed double helix**. The two strands run **antiparallel** (one in 5′→3′, the other in 3′→5′ direction) and are held together by **complementary base pairing** and **π-stacking interactions**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of DNA — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Nucleotide (The Monomer):**
A nucleotide is the repeating unit of DNA and contains three components:
1. A **deoxyribose pentose sugar** (5-carbon sugar lacking a hydroxyl group at the 2′ position — this distinguishes DNA from RNA).
2. A **phosphate group** attached to the 5′ carbon of the sugar.
3. A **nitrogenous base** attached to the 1′ carbon of the sugar via a β-glycosidic bond.

**Step 2 — Phosphodiester Bond (The Backbone Link):**
The 3′-OH of one sugar bonds with the 5′-phosphate of the next nucleotide via a **phosphodiester bond**, forming a single polynucleotide strand with a **5′→3′ directionality**.

**Step 3 — Complementary Base Pairing (Watson-Crick Rules):**
The two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between complementary bases:
- **Adenine (A)** pairs with **Thymine (T)** via **2 hydrogen bonds**.
- **Guanine (G)** pairs with **Cytosine (C)** via **3 hydrogen bonds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for engineering/bioinformatics?** The complementarity rule (A=T, G≡C) is the foundation of **PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction)**, **DNA sequencing**, **DNA microarrays**, **CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing**, and forensic DNA fingerprinting.

**Step 4 — Antiparallel Strand Orientation:**
One strand runs 5′→3′, while its complement runs 3′→5′. This antiparallel orientation is *essential* for the geometry of the major and minor grooves and for the action of DNA polymerase during replication.

**Step 5 — Double Helix Folding:**
The two antiparallel strands twist around a common axis to form a **right-handed double helix**. The sugar-phosphate backbones lie on the outside, while the bases project inward, stacking via hydrophobic and van der Waals interactions (π-stacking) that stabilize the helix vertically.

### 2.2 Functions of DNA

1. **Storage of Genetic Information:** DNA stores hereditary information in the sequence of its bases (the "genetic code").
2. **Replication:** DNA produces identical copies of itself during cell division (mitosis/meiosis), ensuring genetic continuity.
3. **Transcription:** DNA acts as a template for the synthesis of messenger RNA (mRNA), transfer RNA (tRNA), and ribosomal RNA (rRNA).
4. **Translation (Indirect):** Through mRNA, DNA directs the synthesis of proteins that determine cellular structure and function.
5. **Mutation & Evolution:** Changes in the DNA sequence (mutations) provide the raw material for evolution and genetic variation.
6. **Regulation of Gene Expression:** Non-coding regions (promoters, enhancers, silencers) regulate when and how genes are expressed.

### 2.3 Chargaff's Rule — Quantitative Foundation

Erwin Chargaff (1950) discovered a fundamental stoichiometric relationship:

$$\% A = \% T \quad \text{and} \quad \% G = \% C$$

Equivalently, the molar ratios satisfy:

$$\frac{A+G}{T+C} = 1 \quad \text{(purines = pyrimidines)}$$

This rule was *critical empirical evidence* that later allowed Watson and Crick to deduce the base-pairing principle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression / Rule | Variable Meaning | Engineering / Biological Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Chargaff's Rule | $\%A = \%T$ and $\%G = \%C$ | Base composition in dsDNA | Forensic analysis, sequencing QC |
| Melting Temperature | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41(\%GC) - \frac{675}{N}$ | $N$ = length of shortest strand; $[Na^+]$ = salt molarity | Primer design in PCR |
| Helix Geometry | $\text{Pitch} = n \times \text{rise/bp}$ | $n$ = bp per turn (10.5 in B-DNA); rise = 0.34 nm | DNA nanotechnology, structural biology |
| Base Pair Energy | $\Delta G_{A:T} \approx -2.0$ kcal/mol; $\Delta G_{G:C} \approx -3.4$ kcal/mol | Free energy of pairing | Predicting duplex stability |
| Molar Extinction | $\varepsilon_{260nm} \approx 6600$ L/(mol·cm) per ssDNA base | Beer's Law: $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | Quantifying DNA in solution (Nanodrop) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CRISPR-Cas9:** Exploits Watson-Crick complementarity to edit specific DNA sequences.
- **DNA Fingerprinting (VNTR/STR analysis):** Used in forensic science and paternity testing.
- **PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction):** Diagnostic tool for diseases (COVID-19 RT-PCR, TB, HIV viral load testing).
- **Gene Therapy:** Corrects defective genes in monogenic disorders like Cystic Fibrosis and SCID.
- **Bioinformatics:** Tools like BLAST use base-pair complementarity principles for sequence alignment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Classical Experiment 1 — Griffith's Transformation Experiment (1928)

**Background:** Frederick Griffith worked with *Streptococcus pneumoniae*, a bacterium causing pneumonia. Two strains were used:
- **S-strain (Smooth):** Has a polysaccharide capsule → **virulent** (causes death in mice).
- **R-strain (Rough):** Lacks a capsule → **avirulent** (harmless).

**Experimental Setup and Observations (Step-by-Step):**

| Tube | Injection into Mouse | Result | Inference |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Live **S-strain** (virulent) | **Mouse dies**; live S-strain recovered | S-strain is virulent |
| 2 | Live **R-strain** (avirulent) | Mouse **lives**; live R-strain recovered | R-strain is non-virulent |
| 3 | **Heat-killed S-strain** | Mouse **lives**; no bacteria recovered | Heat destroys virulence |
| 4 | **Heat-killed S-strain + Live R-strain** | **Mouse dies**; live S-strain recovered | A "transforming principle" passed from dead S to live R |

**Conclusion (Analytical Derivation):**

$$\text{Dead S-strain (heat-killed)} \xrightarrow{\text{releases "transforming principle"}} \text{Live R-strain} \xrightarrow{\text{uptake + integration}} \text{Live S-strain (virulent)}$$

Griffith concluded that some chemical "transforming principle" from dead S-strain was taken up by live R-strain, permanently transforming it into virulent S-strain. However, he did **not** identify the chemical nature of this principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this experiment is historic:** Griffith provided the *first evidence* that genetic information can be transferred between cells (bacterial transformation), but the **chemical identity of the genetic material remained unknown** until the next experiment.

### 3.2 Classical Experiment 2 — Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty Experiment (1944)

**Objective:** To identify the chemical nature of Griffith's "transforming principle."

**Methodology (Systematic Biochemical Fractionation):**

They cultured large quantities of S-strain, heat-killed them, and extracted the cellular contents. They then **chemically separated** the extract into different macromolecular fractions:

1. **Protein fraction** — treated with **protease** (digests proteins).
2. **RNA fraction** — treated with **RNase** (digests RNA).
3. **DNA fraction** — treated with **DNase** (digests DNA).
4. **Polysaccharide fraction** — treated with appropriate enzymes.
5. **Control** — untreated extract.

Each fraction was added to a culture of live R-strain and tested for transformation.

**Step-by-Step Observations and Inference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Protein fraction + protease} &\longrightarrow \text{Transformation STILL occurs} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Protein is NOT the principle} \\
\text{RNA fraction + RNase} &\longrightarrow \text{Transformation STILL occurs} \quad \Rightarrow \text{RNA is NOT the principle} \\
\text{DNA fraction + DNase} &\longrightarrow \text{Transformation BLOCKED} \quad \Rightarrow \text{DNA IS the principle} \\
\text{Polysaccharide fraction} &\longrightarrow \text{No transformation} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Polysaccharides are NOT the principle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
Only when the **DNA fraction was degraded by DNase** did transformation cease. This established **DNA as the transforming principle** and therefore the genetic material.

### 3.3 Classical Experiment 3 — Hershey-Chase Experiment (1952)

**Background:** Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase used **bacteriophage T2**, a virus that infects *Escherichia coli*. The phage consists only of:
- A **protein coat (capsid)** — composed of sulfur-containing amino acids (cysteine, methionine) but **no phosphorus**.
- **DNA core** — contains **phosphorus** in its phosphate backbone but **no sulfur**.

This **differential chemical labeling** was the key experimental trick.

**Methodology:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Culture A:} &\quad \text{T2 phages grown on }^{32}P\text{-labeled medium (radioactive P labels DNA)} \\
&\quad \text{— DNA labeled with }^{32}P \\
\text{Culture B:} &\quad \text{T2 phages grown on }^{35}S\text{-labeled medium (radioactive S labels protein)} \\
&\quad \text{— Protein coat labeled with }^{35}S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Each labeled phage was allowed to infect *E. coli* cells.
**Step 2:** After infection, the mixture was **agitated in a blender** to shear off the empty phage coats from the bacterial surface.
**Step 3:** The blend was **centrifuged** to separate:
- **Supernatant (light):** empty phage coats
- **Pellet (heavy):** infected bacterial cells

**Observations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
^{32}P\text{-labeled (DNA) experiment:} &\quad \text{Radioactivity found INSIDE the bacteria (pellet)} \\
^{35}S\text{-labeled (protein) experiment:} &\quad \text{Radioactivity found OUTSIDE in phage coats (supernatant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mathematical/Logical Conclusion:**

$$
\text{Protein enters?} \rightarrow \text{NO (}^{35}S\text{ in supernatant)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Protein NOT genetic material}
$$
$$
\text{DNA enters?} \rightarrow \text{YES (}^{32}P\text{ in pellet)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{DNA IS genetic material}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** Hershey and Chase provided **conclusive, irrefutable proof** that **DNA**, not protein, is the genetic material of bacteriophage T2. This finding was generalized to all cellular organisms.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Three Classical Experiments

| Experiment | Year | Model Organism | Method | Genetic Material Identified? |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Griffith | 1928 | *Streptococcus pneumoniae* | In vivo transformation in mice | **No** — proposed a "transforming principle" |
| Avery, MacLeod, McCarty | 1944 | *Streptococcus pneumoniae* | In vitro biochemical fractionation + enzyme digestion | **Yes** — identified it as DNA |
| Hershey-Chase | 1952 | Bacteriophage T2 / *E. coli* | Radioisotopic labeling (³²P / ³⁵S) | **Yes** — definitively proved DNA |

### 3.5 Central Dogma — Francis Crick (1958)

The Central Dogma describes the directional flow of genetic information within a biological system.

**Statement (Crick, 1958):**
"Once information has passed from nucleic acid to protein, it cannot go back. Information cannot flow from protein to nucleic acid."

**The Three Sequential Processes:**

$$
\text{DNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Transcription}} \text{mRNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Translation}} \text{Protein}
$$

**Reverse Transcription (Special Exception):**
In retroviruses (e.g., HIV), the flow is reversed by the enzyme **reverse transcriptase**:

$$
\text{RNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Reverse Transcription}} \text{DNA}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Replication** is also part of the genetic information flow but is a *self-copying* process for DNA itself, not part of the linear DNA→RNA→Protein flow of the original Central Dogma.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Structure of DNA

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[NUCLEOTIDE - Monomer Unit] --> B[Deoxyribose Sugar]
    A --> C[Phosphate Group]
    A --> D[Nitrogenous Base]
    D --> E[Purines: Adenine and Guanine]
    D --> F[Pyrimidines: Cytosine and Thymine]
    B --> G[5 prime Carbon]
    B --> H[3 prime Carbon]
    B --> I[1 prime Carbon]
    G --> J[Phosphodiester Bond]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Polynucleotide Single Strand 5 prime to 3 prime]
    K --> L[Watson-Crick Base Pairing]
    K --> M[Antiparallel Complementary Strand 3 prime to 5 prime]
    L --> N[Double Helix B form DNA]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Chromatin Fiber with Histones]
    O --> P[Metaphase Chromosome]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Central Dogma Flow of Genetic Information

```mermaid
graph LR
    REP[DNA Replication] --> DNA[DNA]
    DNA -->|Transcription| RNA[mRNA]
    RNA -->|Translation| PRO[Protein]
    PRO -.->|Cannot flow back| DNA
    RNA --> RT[Reverse Transcription by Reverse Transcriptase]
    RT -.->|In Retroviruses only| DNA
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Hershey-Chase Experiment Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    PHAGE[T2 Bacteriophage] --> CAPSID[Protein Coat contains Sulfur 35S]
    PHAGE --> CORE[DNA Core contains Phosphorus 32P]
    CAPSID -.->|Labeled with 35S| EXP1[Experiment B: 35S Protein Label]
    CORE -->|Labeled with 32P| EXP2[Experiment A: 32P DNA Label]
    EXP1 --> INF1[Infection of E coli]
    EXP2 --> INF2[Infection of E coli]
    INF1 --> BLEND1[Blender Agitation]
    INF2 --> BLEND2[Blender Agitation]
    BLEND1 --> CENT1[Centrifugation]
    BLEND2 --> CENT2[Centrifugation]
    CENT1 --> SUP1[Supernatant: 35S detected here]
    CENT1 --> PEL1[Pellet: No radioactivity in bacteria]
    CENT2 --> SUP2[Supernatant: No radioactivity]
    CENT2 --> PEL2[Pellet: 32P detected inside bacteria]
    PEL2 --> CONCL[Conclusion: DNA is the Genetic Material]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Griffith's Transformation Experiment Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    SS[Live S strain Virulent Polysaccharide capsule] -->|Injected alone| M1[Mouse Dies]
    RS[Live R strain Avirulent No capsule] -->|Injected alone| M2[Mouse Lives]
    HK[Heat Killed S strain] -->|Injected alone| M3[Mouse Lives]
    HK --> MIX[Mixed with Live R strain]
    RS --> MIX
    MIX --> M4[Mouse Dies]
    M4 --> REC[Live S strain recovered from dead mouse]
    REC --> INFER[Transforming Principle from dead S converts live R to virulent S]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define the Central Dogma of Molecular Biology. Name the three processes involved in it.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Central Dogma, proposed by **Francis Crick in 1958**, states that genetic information flows in a unidirectional manner: **DNA → RNA → Protein**. The three sequential processes are:

1. **Replication** — DNA copies itself.
2. **Transcription** — DNA serves as a template to synthesize mRNA.
3. **Translation** — mRNA directs the synthesis of proteins on ribosomes.

The dogma also specifies that information, once translated into protein, **cannot reverse-flow back to nucleic acid**. (Mark distribution: Definition – 1 mark, Three processes – 1.5 marks, Unidirectionality statement – 0.5 mark.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Distinguish between the findings of Griffith's experiment and Hershey-Chase experiment.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Griffith (1928) | Hershey-Chase (1952) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Identified a "transforming principle" but did not chemically identify it | Chemically identified DNA as the genetic material |
| Used *Streptococcus pneumoniae* in mice | Used bacteriophage T2 and *E. coli* |
| Indirect evidence via bacterial transformation | Direct proof using radioactive ³²P (DNA) and ³⁵S (protein) labeling |
| Conclusion: Some "principle" causes transformation | Conclusion: DNA is the genetic material |

(Mark distribution: Comparison table – 2 marks, Conclusion statement – 1 mark.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe in detail Griffith's experiment on bacterial transformation. What conclusion did he draw, and what was the limitation of his study? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Hershey-Chase experiment with a neat labeled diagram. How did it conclusively prove that DNA is the genetic material? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Griffith's Experiment (7 Marks):**
Frederick Griffith (1928) used two strains of *Streptococcus pneumoniae* — S-strain (smooth, virulent, with polysaccharide capsule) and R-strain (rough, avirulent, no capsule). He performed four sets of experiments in mice:
1. Live S-strain → mouse dies → S-strain re-isolated.
2. Live R-strain → mouse lives → R-strain re-isolated.
3. Heat-killed S-strain → mouse lives → no bacteria recovered.
4. Heat-killed S-strain + Live R-strain → **mouse dies** → **live S-strain recovered**.

**Conclusion:** A "transforming principle" was released from heat-killed S-strain and was taken up by live R-strain, transforming it into virulent S-strain.

**Limitation:** Griffith could not identify the **chemical nature** of the transforming principle. (Distribution: Experimental design – 3 marks, Four observations – 2 marks, Conclusion and limitation – 2 marks.)

**(b) Hershey-Chase Experiment (7 Marks):**
Hershey and Chase (1952) used **bacteriophage T2** and *E. coli*. The phage has a protein coat (containing sulfur, no phosphorus) and DNA core (containing phosphorus, no sulfur).

- **³²P labeling:** Labels DNA → after infection, radioactivity found **inside bacteria (pellet)** → DNA enters the cell.
- **³⁵S labeling:** Labels protein → after infection, radioactivity found **outside (supernatant)** → protein does not enter.

After blender agitation and centrifugation, the ³²P (DNA) entered the bacteria while ³⁵S (protein coat) stayed outside. The progeny phages produced contained ³²P, proving DNA — not protein — is the genetic material. (Distribution: Principle – 2 marks, ³²P experiment – 2 marks, ³⁵S experiment – 2 marks, Conclusion – 1 mark.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the Watson-Crick model of DNA double helix. Mention any six salient features. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed account of the Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty experiment. How did it establish DNA as the genetic material? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Watson-Crick Model of DNA (7 Marks):**
James Watson and Francis Crick (1953) proposed the double helix model based on X-ray diffraction data (Rosalind Franklin) and Chargaff's rules.

**Six Salient Features:**
1. DNA is a **right-handed double helix**.
2. Two polynucleotide strands run **antiparallel** (5′→3′ and 3′→5′).
3. **Sugar-phosphate backbone** is on the outside; **bases** project inward.
4. Bases pair via **Watson-Crick complementarity**: A=T (2 H-bonds), G≡C (3 H-bonds).
5. **Chargaff's rule** is explained: %A = %T and %G = %C.
6. The helix has a **pitch of 3.4 nm** with **10.5 base pairs per turn**.
(Distribution: Historical context – 1 mark, Six features – 6 marks = 1 mark each.)

**(b) Avery, MacLeod, McCarty Experiment (7 Marks):**
Oswald Avery, Colin MacLeod, and Maclyn McCarty (1944) chemically fractionated heat-killed S-strain extracts into proteins, RNA, DNA, and polysaccharides. Each fraction was treated with a specific enzyme (protease, RNase, DNase) and tested for transformation ability on R-strain.

**Key Observation:** Transformation was abolished **only when DNA was degraded by DNase**. This proved DNA — and not protein, RNA, or polysaccharide — was the transforming principle.

This was the **first direct chemical evidence** that DNA carries genetic information. (Distribution: Methodology – 3 marks, Observations – 2 marks, Conclusion – 2 marks.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Griffith with Avery:** Many students incorrectly state that Griffith "proved DNA is the genetic material." Griffith only proposed a "transforming principle" without identifying its chemistry. **Always specify that Avery-MacLeod-McCarty and Hershey-Chase** provided the conclusive proof.
> 2. **Confusing radioactive labels:** Students often write ³²P for protein and ³⁵S for DNA. **Correct:** Phosphorus (³²P) is in DNA's phosphate backbone; Sulfur (³⁵S) is in protein's cysteine/methionine residues.
> 3. **Central Dogma directionality:** Some students forget to mention that the flow is **unidirectional** and that information cannot flow from protein back to nucleic acid.
> 4. **Watson-Crick helix handedness:** B-DNA is **right-handed**; stating it as left-handed costs marks immediately.
> 5. **Skipping the "antiparallel" keyword:** Examiners specifically test for antiparallel strand orientation. Always mention **5′→3′** and **3′→5′** explicitly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DNA structure:** Double helix, antiparallel, complementary base pairing, right-handed, B-form major.
- **Watson-Crick base pairing:** A=T (2 H-bonds); G≡C (3 H-bonds).
- **Helix dimensions:** 2 nm diameter; 0.34 nm rise per bp; 10.5 bp/turn; 3.4 nm pitch.
- **Chargaff's Rule:** %A = %T and %G = %C (purines = pyrimidines).
- **Griffith (1928):** Proposed transforming principle; no chemical identification.
- **Avery-MacLeod-McCarty (1944):** Used enzyme digestion; **DNase abolished transformation → DNA is the principle.**
- **Hershey-Chase (1952):** ³²P (DNA) inside bacteria; ³⁵S (protein) outside → **DNA is the genetic material.**
- **Central Dogma (Crick, 1958):** DNA → RNA → Protein; **unidirectional** (no protein → nucleic acid).
- **Three processes:** Replication, Transcription, Translation.
- **Special exception:** Reverse transcriptase in retroviruses (RNA → DNA).
- **High-yield keywords to memorize:** Phosphodiester bond, β-glycosidic bond, antiparallel, π-stacking, semiconservative, complementary, right-handed helix, transformation, bacteriophage, reverse transcriptase.
- **Difference between DNA and RNA** (often asked): DNA has deoxyribose + thymine; RNA has ribose + uracil; DNA is usually double-stranded, RNA is usually single-stranded.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gene Organization and Distribution
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Gene Organization and Distribution — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Gene:**
A gene is a fundamental physical and functional unit of heredity, consisting of a specific DNA sequence that is transcribed into RNA and ultimately translated (or directly functional as RNA) to produce a phenotypic trait or regulatory function.

**Classical Gene Concept (Mendelian/Traditional):**
The classical gene is defined as a discrete heritable factor that:
- Occupies a specific chromosomal locus
- Segregates according to Mendelian laws
- Mutates to give discrete phenotypic effects
- Shows clear-cut recombination with neighboring genes

**Modern Molecular Gene Concept:**
At the molecular level, a gene is defined as:
> "A complete chromosomal segment responsible for making a functional product — be it a polypeptide chain or a functional RNA molecule (rRNA, tRNA, snRNA)."

This modern definition was articulated by **Benzer (1957, 1959)** through his work on *rII* locus in T4 bacteriophage and consolidated by the discovery of **split genes (introns)** by **Roberts and Sharp (1977, Nobel Prize 1993)**.

**Gene Organization:**
The arrangement, packaging, density, clustering, and ordering of genes along a chromosome (or genome) — including whether genes exist in single copies, tandem repeats, or dispersed multigene families.

**Gene Distribution:**
The non-random positioning of coding and non-coding DNA across the genome, including how genes are partitioned between chromosomes, euchromatin, heterochromatin, and organellar genomes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **vast library (the genome)**:

| Library Component | Genome Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Library building | Cell nucleus |
| Books | Chromosomes |
| Chapters in a book | Genes |
| Sentences in a chapter | Exons (meaningful parts) |
| Page numbers / filler text | Introns (intervening, non-coding) |
| Reference notes at the back | Regulatory elements, pseudogenes |
| Copies of a best-seller book | Tandem gene clusters / gene families |

**Key Insights from the Analogy:**
- Not every chapter (gene) is read the same number of times — some genes are highly expressed, others are silent (like rare books).
- Some chapters have filler (introns) that gets edited out before reading; this is "splicing."
- The library sometimes has multiple copies of the same chapter — this represents **gene families**.
- Some "chapters" are unreadable or damaged — these are **pseudogenes**.
- The library has both "main collection" shelves (euchromatin — gene-rich, transcriptionally active) and "storage rooms" (heterochromatin — gene-poor, silent).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics in Gene Organization Studies**
>
> - **1 kilobase pair (kb)** = $10^3$ base pairs $\approx 333 \text{ nm}$ of B-DNA
> - **1 megabase pair (Mb)** = $10^6$ bp $\approx 0.33 \text{ mm}$
> - **Human diploid cell DNA content** = ~**6.6 picograms (pg)** $\approx$ **6.0 × 10⁹ bp**
> - **Avogadro's constant** = $6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
> - **Mean molecular weight per base pair (dsDNA)** = **~660 g/mol**
> - **1 pg DNA** $\approx$ **0.965 × 10⁹ bp** (conventionally **1 pg ≈ 10⁹ bp**)
> - **Mean gene length (human)** ≈ **27,000 bp** (including introns); coding region only ~**1,350 bp**

---

## 1.4 Core Definitions for KTU Module 1

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Definitions (High-Yield for KTU Exam)**
>
> 1. **Exon:** A DNA segment of a gene that is retained in the mature mRNA after splicing. Exons encode the protein product.
> 2. **Intron:** A non-coding DNA sequence within a gene that is transcribed into pre-mRNA but excised by the spliceosome during RNA processing.
> 3. **C-value:** The total DNA content of a haploid (1n) genome, measured in picograms (pg) or base pairs (bp).
> 4. **C-value paradox:** The observation that genome size (C-value) does NOT correlate with organismal complexity or gene number (e.g., *Amphiuma* salamander has C-value ~30× greater than humans).
> 5. **Split gene:** A gene containing one or more introns interrupting the coding sequence.
> 6. **Operon:** A cluster of co-transcribed bacterial genes under the control of a single promoter and operator (Jacob & Monod, 1961).
> 7. **Gene family:** A group of evolutionarily related genes derived from duplication of an ancestral gene, often clustered or dispersed.
> 8. **Pseudogene:** A non-functional DNA segment that resembles a functional gene but contains disabling mutations (premature stop codons, frameshifts, or loss of regulatory elements).
> 9. **Repetitive DNA:** DNA sequences present in multiple copies in the genome — classified as **tandem repeats** (satellites, minisatellites, microsatellites) or **interspersed repeats** (LINEs, SINEs, retrotransposons).
> 10. **Euchromatin:** Lightly packed, gene-rich, transcriptionally active chromatin (replicates early in S phase).
> 11. **Heterochromatin:** Densely packed, gene-poor, transcriptionally silent chromatin (replicates late in S phase).
> 12. **Single-copy genes:** Genes present in one (or a few) copies per haploid genome — represent **~40–50%** of eukaryotic genomes.
> 13. **Overlapping genes:** Two or more genes that share the same DNA region but read in different frames or directions (common in viruses and mitochondrial genomes).
> 14. **Selenocysteine codon (UGA):** Re-purposed stop codon that codes for the 21st amino acid selenocysteine.
> 15. **Read-through / Stop codon read-through:** Translation that continues past the canonical stop codon due to specific context.

---

## 1.5 Gene Organization — Hierarchical Levels

Gene organization can be described at **four hierarchical levels**:

1. **Genome-level organization** — Total DNA content, chromosome number, distribution between organelles.
2. **Chromosome-level organization** — Gene density, banding patterns (G-bands, R-bands), chromatin states.
3. **Gene-level organization** — Open Reading Frame (ORF) architecture, intron/exon structure, regulatory elements.
4. **Nucleotide-level organization** — Codon usage, GC content, CpG islands.

---

## 1.6 Classical vs Modern Molecular Definition — KTU Exam Comparison Table

| Feature | Classical Gene | Modern Molecular Gene |
|---|---|---|
| Unit of function | Recombination, mutation, function | **Cistron** (complementation test) — Benzer |
| Definition basis | Phenotype | **Sequence-based** (transcribed region) |
| Inheritance | Mendelian | Often non-Mendelian (epigenetic, transgenerational) |
| Structure | Indivisible | **Split** (introns + exons) |
| Function | One gene = one trait | One gene = one polypeptide **OR** functional RNA |
| Overlap | Genes are discrete | **Overlapping genes** exist |
| Coding | Continuous | **Discontinuous** (mosaic) |
| Established by | Morgan, Sturtevant (1910s) | Benzer (1957), Jacob-Monod (1961) |

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gene Organization in Prokaryotes vs Eukaryotes

### 2.1.1 Prokaryotic Gene Organization

Prokaryotic genomes are characterized by **gene density** — virtually every base pair is coding or regulatory. Bacterial chromosomes typically have a **single origin of replication (oriC)** and are organized into **topologically associating domains (TADs)**.

**Hallmarks:**
- **Circular chromosome** (most bacteria; *Borrelia burgdorferi* and *Streptomyces* are exceptions with linear chromosomes)
- **No introns** in protein-coding genes (rare exceptions: archaeal rRNA and tRNA introns, group I introns in cyanobacteria)
- **Operon-based clustering** — multiple related genes co-transcribed as a polycistronic mRNA
- **Minimal intergenic spacing** — often overlap or have short promoters
- **Leader sequences** with attenuators (e.g., *trp* operon)
- **Compactness** — *E. coli* has ~4,400 genes in ~4.6 Mb → **~1 gene per 1 kb**
- **Overlapping ORFs** — different reading frames on opposite strands

**Example: The *lac* Operon (Jacob & Monod, 1961):**

$$\text{Structure: } \underbrace{P_{lac} - O_{lac}}_{\text{Control}} - \underbrace{lacZ - lacY - lacA}_{\text{Structural genes}}$$

- *lacZ* — $\beta$-galactosidase
- *lacY* — permease
- *lacA* — transacetylase

All three are transcribed as a single ~5,300 nt polycistronic mRNA.

---

### 2.1.2 Eukaryotic Gene Organization

Eukaryotic genomes are characterized by **discontinuous genes (split genes)** and large amounts of **non-coding DNA**.

**Hallmarks:**
- **Linear chromosomes** packaged into nucleosomes
- **Introns** — average 4–8 per gene in humans; can be huge (e.g., *DMD* dystrophin gene = 2.4 Mb with 79 exons; longest intron = 400 kb)
- **Long intergenic regions**
- **Enhancers, silencers, insulators** — distal regulatory elements
- **Operon-like clusters exist** but are rare — most eukaryotic genes are monocistronic
- **Multigene families** and pseudogenes
- **Repetitive DNA** dominates — in humans, **~69%** of the genome is repetitive
- **CpG islands** — unmethylated CG-rich promoters of housekeeping genes

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Prokaryote (E. coli) | Eukaryote (Human) |
|---|---|---|
| Genome size | 4.6 Mb | 3,200 Mb |
| Gene number | ~4,400 | ~20,000–25,000 |
| Gene density | ~1 per kb | ~1 per 100–150 kb |
| Introns in protein genes | Almost none | Average 4–8 per gene |
| % Coding DNA | ~90% | ~1.2% |
| Chromosomes | 1 (circular) | 46 (linear, 23 pairs) |
| mRNA | Polycistronic | Monocistronic |
| Operons | Yes | Rare |
| Repetitive DNA | Minimal | ~69% |

---

## 2.2 C-Value: Genome Size Measurement

### 2.2.1 Definition and Conversion Formula

**C-value (C)** = DNA content of a **haploid** nucleus (1n)

**Conversion between picograms (pg) and base pairs (bp):**

$$\boxed{1 \text{ pg DNA} \approx 0.965 \times 10^9 \text{ bp} \approx 10^9 \text{ bp}}$$

**Number of base pairs from C-value:**

$$\boxed{N_{bp} = C \text{ [pg]} \times 0.965 \times 10^9}$$

**Length of DNA (extended):**

Each base pair contributes $0.34 \text{ nm}$ to B-form DNA length.

$$\boxed{L \text{ [m]} = N_{bp} \times 3.4 \times 10^{-10}}$$

**Molecular weight of dsDNA:**

$$\boxed{M_W \text{ [Da]} = N_{bp} \times 660 \text{ g/mol}}$$

---

### 2.2.2 C-Value Paradox

The **C-value paradox** describes the puzzling observation that genome size **does NOT** correlate with organismal complexity or estimated gene number.

**Examples:**

| Organism | C-value (pg) | Estimated Genes | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| *E. coli* | 0.0046 | ~4,400 | Bacterium |
| *Arabidopsis thaliana* | 0.16 | ~27,000 | Plant |
| *Drosophila melanogaster* | 0.18 | ~14,000 | Insect |
| *Homo sapiens* | 3.5 | ~20,000–25,000 | Mammal |
| *Amphiuma* (salamander) | ~84 | Not sequenced fully | "Lower" vertebrate |
| *Paris japonica* (lily) | ~152 | Estimated ~50,000 | Plant |
| *Trillium* | ~30 | — | Plant |

**Resolution of the C-Value Paradox:**
- Non-coding DNA (introns, intergenic regions, repetitive DNA) accounts for most size variation
- Transposable elements and polyploidy inflate genome size
- **Gene number is NOT proportional to genome size** — this is now called the **G-value paradox**

---

## 2.3 Repetitive DNA — Classification

### 2.3.1 Tandem Repeats

Sequences arranged head-to-tail in clusters.

| Class | Repeat Unit | Length | Total per Genome | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Satellites** | Multi-kb | $10^5$ – $10^7$ bp | Mb-level | Centromeres, telomeres |
| **Minisatellites** | 10–100 bp | $10^2$ – $10^5$ bp | 0.1–1 Mb | Hypervariable VNTRs; DNA fingerprinting |
| **Microsatellites** | 1–6 bp | Up to 150 bp | ~10⁵ loci | SSR markers, genetic mapping |
| **Macrosatellites** | 100 bp – several kb | Variable | — | Some chromatin organization |

**Examples:**
- $\alpha$-satellite DNA at centromeres
- (CA)ₙ microsatellites used in forensic DNA fingerprinting
- Telomeric (TTAGGG)ₙ repeats at chromosome ends

### 2.3.2 Interspersed Repeats (Transposable Elements)

**SINEs (Short Interspersed Nuclear Elements):**
- 100–500 bp long
- Examples: **Alu** (~300 bp, ~1.1 million copies in humans = ~11% of genome)

**LINEs (Long Interspersed Nuclear Elements):**
- 4–6 kb long
- Example: **LINE-1 (L1)** (~100,000 active copies in humans = ~17% of genome)

**LTR Retrotransposons:**
- Endogenous retroviruses (HERVs in humans)

---

## 2.4 Gene Families

### 2.4.1 Types of Gene Families

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple families** | Few related genes in cluster | $\alpha$-globin (HBA1, HBA2) |
| **Complex families** | Multiple related genes across genome | $\beta$-globin cluster, Hox genes |
| **Dispersed families** | Single ancestor, scattered | Olfactory receptor genes |
| **Pseudogenes** | Disabled family members | ψ$\alpha$1, ψ$\beta$2 |
| **Processed pseudogenes** | Retrotransposed cDNA copies (no introns, with poly-A tail) | Many in mammals |

### 2.4.2 Classical Gene Families for KTU

**$\alpha$-Globin Cluster (Human, Chromosome 16):**
$$\zeta - \psi\zeta - \psi\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 - \alpha_1 - \theta$$

**$\beta$-Globin Cluster (Human, Chromosome 11):**
$$\epsilon - {}^G\gamma - {}^A\gamma - \psi\beta_1 - \delta - \beta$$

---

## 2.5 Pseudogenes — Detailed Classification

| Type | Origin | Hallmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Unprocessed (conventional)** | Tandem duplication + disabling mutations | Retains introns, flanking repeats |
| **Processed (retro-pseudogenes)** | Reverse transcription of mRNA → insertion | **No introns**, **3′ poly-A tail**, **direct repeats** |
| **Unitary pseudogenes** | Single-gene disabling (no duplication) | Unique in genome |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base pairs from C-value | $N_{bp} = C \times 0.965 \times 10^9$ | C in pg |
| 2 | C-value from bp | $C = \dfrac{N_{bp}}{0.965 \times 10^9}$ | pg |
| 3 | DNA length (extended) | $L = N_{bp} \times 0.34 \times 10^{-9}$ | m |
| 4 | DNA molecular weight | $M_W = N_{bp} \times 660$ | g/mol |
| 5 | Molecular weight of single nucleotide (avg) | $\overline{MW}_{nt} = 330$ | g/mol |
| 6 | Number of base pairs in 1 µm | $\dfrac{10^{-6}}{0.34 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 2941$ | bp/µm |
| 7 | Number of helical turns per kb | $\dfrac{1000}{10.5} \approx 95.2$ | turns (B-DNA) |
| 8 | Cot curve complexity formula | $C_0 t_{1/2}$ is proportional to genome complexity | Used in renaturation kinetics |
| 9 | Cot half value | $C_0 t_{1/2} \propto \text{genome complexity}$ | Larger genome → higher Cot½ |
| 10 | Renaturation rate | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k C^2$ | Second-order kinetics |
| 11 | Re-association rate constant | $k = \dfrac{1}{C_0 t_{1/2}}$ | L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 12 | Fraction of genome (single copy) | $f_{sc} \approx 40\text{–}50\%$ | Eukaryotic genomes |
| 13 | Fraction repetitive | $f_{rep} = 1 - f_{sc}$ | Eukaryotic |
| 14 | Gene density | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{genes}}{L_{genome} \text{ [Mb]}}$ | genes/Mb |
| 15 | Coding fraction | $f_{coding} = \dfrac{\sum L_{exons}}{L_{genome}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 16 | Number of alleles per locus | $n_{alleles}$ | Diploid = 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Renaturation kinetics ($C_0 t$ analysis) is a direct experimental method to determine **genome complexity** and fraction of repetitive DNA. Pioneered by **Roy Britten and Kohne (1968)**.

---

## 2.7 Cot Curve Analysis (Britten-Davidson Kinetics)

**C₀t curve** plots the fraction of denatured DNA that has re-annealed (fraction renatured) against $C_0 t$, where:
- $C_0$ = initial concentration of single-stranded DNA (mol/L)
- $t$ = time (seconds)

**Standard Interpretation:**
- **Fast-renaturing (low $C_0 t_{1/2}$)** = highly repetitive DNA (e.g., satellite, Alu)
- **Intermediate-renaturing** = moderately repetitive (e.g., LINE, ribosomal RNA genes)
- **Slow-renaturing (high $C_0 t_{1/2}$)** = single-copy unique sequences

**Pure component complexity (Equation):**

$$\boxed{X = \dfrac{C_0 t_{1/2}^{\text{component}}}{C_0 t_{1/2}^{\text{reference}}} \times X_{\text{reference}}}$$

A **polyploid genome** of complexity $X_{polyploid}$ in identical repeats gives:

$$C_0 t_{1/2}^{polyploid} = \dfrac{C_0 t_{1/2}^{unique}}{N_{copies}}$$

where $N_{copies}$ is the copy number of the repeat.

---

| **Medicine** | Diagnosis of trinucleotide repeat disorders (Huntington's, Fragile X, Myotonic dystrophy) |
| **Forensics** | STR (microsatellite) profiling for DNA fingerprinting |
| **Agriculture** | Marker-assisted selection using SSRs/SNPs |
| **Evolution** | Comparative genomics, synteny analysis |

| **Gene therapy** | Avoiding insertional mutagenesis by understanding chromatin |
| **Paternity testing** | VNTR/minisatellite-based identification |
| **Phylogenetics** | Alu insertion polymorphisms as human population markers |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — C-Value to Base Pairs Conversion

**Given:**
- C-value of *Homo sapiens* (haploid) = 3.5 pg
- 1 pg of DNA corresponds to $0.965 \times 10^9$ base pairs

**Find:** Total number of base pairs in the human haploid genome.

**Step 1:** Apply the conversion formula:

$$N_{bp} = C \text{ [pg]} \times 0.965 \times 10^9 \frac{\text{bp}}{\text{pg}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute C = 3.5 pg:

$$N_{bp} = 3.5 \times 0.965 \times 10^9$$

**Step 3:** Compute the product:

$$3.5 \times 0.965 = 3.3775$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$\boxed{N_{bp} \approx 3.38 \times 10^9 \text{ bp} \approx 3.2 \text{ Gb}}$$

This matches the **Human Genome Project** finding of **~3.2 Gb** haploid genome.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Length of Human Haploid Genome

**Given:** $N_{bp} = 3.2 \times 10^9$ bp, B-DNA rise per bp = $0.34 \text{ nm} = 0.34 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$.

**Step 1:** Apply DNA length formula:

$$L = N_{bp} \times r_{\text{bp}}$$

where $r_{\text{bp}}$ = rise per base pair.

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$L = (3.2 \times 10^9) \times (0.34 \times 10^{-9}) \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$L = 3.2 \times 0.34 = 1.088 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Result:

$$\boxed{L \approx 1.088 \text{ m per haploid genome}}$$

**Interpretation:** A single human haploid genome, if fully extended, would be **~1.09 meters long**. In a diploid cell, total DNA length is **~2.18 meters**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Molecular Weight of Human Diploid Genome

**Given:** Diploid cell, $2N = 6.4 \times 10^9$ bp, MW per bp = 660 g/mol.

**Step 1:** Apply:

$$M_W = N_{bp} \times 660 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$M_W = (6.4 \times 10^9) \times 660$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$6.4 \times 660 = 4224$$

**Step 4:** Final:

$$\boxed{M_W = 4.224 \times 10^{12} \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to picograms:

$$M_W \text{ [pg]} = \dfrac{4.224 \times 10^{12}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \times 10^{12}$$

$$= 7.01 \text{ pg}$$

**Sanity check:** Diploid human cell ~6.6 pg (consistent).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cot Curve Complexity and Copy Number

**Setup:** The renaturation of denatured DNA follows second-order kinetics:

$$\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k C^2$$

where $C$ is the concentration of complementary single strands at time $t$, and $k$ is the rate constant.

**Step 1:** Separate variables and integrate:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \dfrac{dC}{C^2} = -\int_0^t k\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\left[-\dfrac{1}{C}\right]_{C_0}^{C} = -kt$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $C$:

$$\dfrac{1}{C} - \dfrac{1}{C_0} = kt$$

**Step 4:** Define $C_0 t_{1/2}$ as the time when $C = \dfrac{C_0}{2}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{C_0/2} - \dfrac{1}{C_0} = k C_0 t_{1/2}$$

$$\dfrac{2}{C_0} - \dfrac{1}{C_0} = k C_0 t_{1/2}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$\dfrac{1}{C_0} = k C_0 t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{C_0 t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k}}$$

**Interpretation:** The $C_0 t_{1/2}$ value is inversely proportional to the rate constant of renaturation, and directly proportional to **genome complexity**.

**Step 6:** For a sequence present in $n$ identical copies per genome, the effective concentration is $nC_0$, so:

$$C_0 t_{1/2}^{\text{repetitive}} = \dfrac{1}{k \cdot n} = \dfrac{C_0 t_{1/2}^{\text{unique}}}{n}$$

$$\boxed{n = \dfrac{C_0 t_{1/2}^{\text{unique}}}{C_0 t_{1/2}^{\text{repetitive}}}}$$

**Example:** If the unique-sequence $C_0 t_{1/2} = 10^4$ and a satellite gives $C_0 t_{1/2} = 10^{-2}$, then:

$$n = \dfrac{10^4}{10^{-2}} = 10^6$$

→ The satellite is present in approximately **one million copies** per genome.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Tm Calculation for GC Content

**Given:** $T_m$ of a DNA duplex is the temperature at which 50% is denatured.

**Wallace–Itakura rule (for short oligos < 25 bp):**

$$T_m \text{ [°C]} = 2(A + T) + 4(G + C)$$

**Marmur–Schildkraut equation (for long DNA, > 100 bp):**

$$T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41 \cdot \%GC - \dfrac{600}{N}$$

**Step-by-step Example:**
- Given: 30-bp DNA with 12 G, 6 C, 7 A, 5 T in $0.1 \text{ M Na}^+$

**Using Wallace–Itakura (short):**

$$T_m = 2(7+5) + 4(12+6) = 24 + 72 = 96 \text{ °C}$$

**Using Marmur–Schildkraut (long-form approximated):**
- %GC = $\dfrac{18}{30} \times 100 = 60\%$
- $\log_{10}[Na^+] = \log_{10}(0.1) = -1$

$$T_m = 81.5 + 16.6(-1) + 0.41(60) - \dfrac{600}{30}$$

$$= 81.5 - 16.6 + 24.6 - 20 = 69.5 \text{ °C}$$

Both estimates bracket the true $T_m$ of ~80 °C (length-dependent).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Gene Density in Eukaryotes

**Given:** Human genome = 3.2 Gb, ~20,000 genes.

$$\rho_{human} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{3{,}200} \approx 6.25 \text{ genes/Mb}$$

**For *E. coli*:** 4,400 genes / 4.6 Mb:

$$\rho_{E.coli} = \dfrac{4{,}400}{4.6} \approx 957 \text{ genes/Mb}$$

**For Yeast (*S. cerevisiae*):** ~6,000 genes / 12 Mb:

$$\rho_{yeast} = \dfrac{6{,}000}{12} = 500 \text{ genes/Mb}$$

**Compression factor:** Human gene density is **~150× lower** than *E. coli*.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Fraction of Human Genome That Is Coding

**Given:**
- ~20,000 protein-coding genes
- Mean coding length = 1,350 bp
- Total exon length per gene (with 4–8 small exons) ≈ 1,350 bp average

**Step 1:** Total coding bases:

$$L_{coding} = 20{,}000 \times 1{,}350 = 2.7 \times 10^7 \text{ bp}$$

**Step 2:** Total haploid genome:

$$L_{genome} = 3.2 \times 10^9 \text{ bp}$$

**Step 3:** Coding fraction:

$$f_{coding} = \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^7}{3.2 \times 10^9} = 0.00844$$

$$\boxed{f_{coding} \approx 1.2\%}$$

This explains why **~98.8%** of the human genome is non-coding (often misleadingly called "junk DNA" — most has regulatory and structural roles).

---

## 3.8 Derivation 8 — Repetitive DNA Fraction in Humans

**Known fractions of human genome:**
- **LINEs (L1):** ~17%
- **SINEs (Alu):** ~10%
- **LTR retrotransposons:** ~8%
- **DNA transposons:** ~3%
- **Simple repeats:** ~3%
- **Segmental duplications:** ~5%
- **Tandem repeats (telomeric, centromeric, ribosomal):** ~8%
- **Total repetitive DNA:** ~46–50% (some estimates up to **69%** including degenerate copies)

**Verification:**
$$f_{repetitive} \approx 1 - f_{single-copy} = 1 - 0.50 = 0.50$$

The remaining ~50% is unique single-copy DNA — much of which is intronic and intergenic.

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Genome Features Across Domains

| Feature | Bacterium (E. coli) | Yeast (S. cerevisiae) | Worm (C. elegans) | Fly (D. melanogaster) | Human (H. sapiens) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Genome size (Mb) | 4.6 | 12 | 100 | 140 | 3,200 |
| Gene number | 4,400 | 6,000 | 19,000 | 14,000 | 20,000 |
| Gene density (genes/Mb) | 957 | 500 | 190 | 100 | 6.25 |
| Average introns/gene | < 0.1 | ~0.04 | ~5 | ~3 | ~6 |
| % Coding | ~90% | ~75% | ~25% | ~13% | ~1.2% |
| % Repetitive | < 1% | ~3.5% | ~12% | ~30% | ~50–69% |
| Transposons active | Few | LTR (Ty) | Few | P, gypsy | L1, Alu (active) |
| Operons | Many | Few | Rare | None | None |
| Polycistronic mRNA | Yes | Some | No | No | No |

---

## 3.10 Worked Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A research team isolates DNA from a new microbe. Spectrophotometry shows a haploid C-value of 0.002 pg. (a) Calculate the genome size in bp. (b) Estimate gene number assuming gene density similar to *E. coli*. (c) Calculate total DNA length in µm.

**Solution:**

**(a) Genome size in base pairs:**

$$N_{bp} = 0.002 \times 0.965 \times 10^9 = 1.93 \times 10^6 \text{ bp} \approx 1.93 \text{ Mb}$$

**(b) Gene number at ~1 gene per kb:**

$$N_{genes} \approx \dfrac{1.93 \times 10^6}{10^3} = 1{,}930 \text{ genes}$$

**(c) DNA length:**

$$L = 1.93 \times 10^6 \times 0.34 \times 10^{-9} = 6.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 656 \text{ µm} \approx 0.66 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** This microbe has a **1.93 Mb** genome, encodes approximately **1,930 genes**, and its DNA, if stretched, would measure **~656 µm**.

```mermaid
    nodeX1 --> nodeY2["INTERMEDIATE COMPONENT<br/>Mid Cot half<br/>LINEs SINEs<br/>10 to 10 5 copies"]
    nodeX1 --> nodeY3["SLOW COMPONENT<br/>High Cot half<br/>Single copy genes<br/>1 to 2 copies"]
    nodeY1 --> nodeZ["COT CURVE<br/>Three phases identifiable<br/>y axis fraction renatured<br/>x axis log Cot"]
    nodeY2 --> nodeZ
    nodeY3 --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1["Determine genome complexity X<br/>Determine repetitive fraction f"]
```
