<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: List any three functions of DNA Helicase during replication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Unwinds the parental double-stranded DNA at the replication fork by breaking hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs (1 mark).
2. Translocates along the lagging-strand template in the 5'→3' direction using **2 ATP molecules per base pair** unwound (1 mark).
3. Acts as a molecular motor and provides the platform for other replication proteins (primase, SSB) to assemble into the **replisome** (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q: What is the end-replication problem? Name the enzyme that solves it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **end-replication problem** is the inability of DNA polymerase to fully replicate the 5' ends of linear chromosomes because the lagging strand requires an RNA primer that, once removed, leaves a 3' overhang (2 marks).
- The problem is solved by **Telomerase**, a ribonucleoprotein that uses its intrinsic **RNA template (TERC)** and reverse transcriptase activity (**TERT**) to add **TTAGGG** repeats to chromosome ends (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **internal choice** — answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each Part B question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Comprehensive]**

**Q: Describe in detail the enzymes involved in DNA replication. Discuss their classification, mechanisms, and biological significance. Compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic replication enzymes.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Replication Enzymes (7 marks)**

DNA replication requires the coordinated action of multiple enzyme classes:

1. **Unwinding Enzymes:** DNA Helicase (DnaB in *E. coli*; MCM2-7 in eukaryotes) and Topoisomerases (Gyrase, Topo I, Topo II). These enzymes separate the parental strands and relieve torsional strain generated ahead of the replication fork.

2. **Stabilizing Proteins:** Single-Strand Binding (SSB) proteins in prokaryotes and Replication Protein A (RPA) in eukaryotes. They bind cooperatively to single-stranded DNA and prevent re-annealing or secondary structure formation.

3. **Primase:** A specialized RNA polymerase (DnaG in prokaryotes; primase subunit of Pol α in eukaryotes) that synthesizes short RNA primers (10–12 nt in prokaryotes; 8–10 nt in eukaryotes) to provide the free 3'-OH group needed by DNA polymerase.

4. **DNA Polymerases:** The main replicative enzymes. In prokaryotes, **DNA Pol III** performs bulk synthesis; in eukaryotes, **Pol δ** synthesizes the lagging strand and **Pol ε** synthesizes the leading strand. All DNA polymerases synthesize only in the **5'→3'** direction.

5. **Cleanup Enzymes:** DNA Pol I (prokaryotes) with its 5'→3' exonuclease activity removes RNA primers; in eukaryotes, **RNase H** and **FEN1** perform primer removal.

6. **Joining Enzyme:** DNA Ligase seals the nicks between Okazaki fragments using **ATP (eukaryotes) or NAD⁺ (prokaryotes)** as cofactor.

7. **Accessory Factors:** The sliding clamp (**β-clamp in prokaryotes; PCNA in eukaryotes**) and clamp loader (**γ-complex in prokaryotes; RFC in eukaryotes**) dramatically increase polymerase processivity.

8. **Telomerase:** A specialized reverse transcriptase present only in eukaryotes that replicates the ends of linear chromosomes.

**(b) Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic Comparison (7 marks)**

| Feature | Prokaryotes (*E. coli*) | Eukaryotes (Human) |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Number of origins | 1 (OriC, 245 bp) | ~30,000 |
| Replication rate | ~1000 nt/s | ~50 nt/s |
| Main polymerase | DNA Pol III (single enzyme for both strands) | Pol δ (lagging) + Pol ε (leading) |
| Sliding clamp | β-clamp (dimer) | PCNA (trimer) |
| Primer removal | DNA Pol I (5'→3' exonuclease) | RNase H + FEN1 |
| Telomerase | Absent (circular chromosome) | Present |
| Topology relief | DNA Gyrase (Topo II) | Topo IIα + Topo I |

**Biological Significance:**
- Fidelity of replication is approximately $10^{-10}$ after proofreading and mismatch repair.
- Defects in these enzymes cause cancer (mutations in Pol δ, Pol ε), progeria-like syndromes (telomerase defects), and are targets of major antibiotics (DNA Gyrase) and antivirals (viral DNA polymerases).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**Q: Explain the molecular mechanism of the replication fork. With suitable diagrams, describe the role of DNA polymerase, helicase, primase, ligase, and sliding clamp in coordinating leading and lagging strand synthesis.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Replication Fork Architecture & Leading/Lagging Strand Coordination (7 marks)**

The replication fork is a Y-shaped structure where the parental DNA is unwound and two daughter strands are synthesized simultaneously. Because DNA polymerase can only synthesize in the 5'→3' direction, the two antiparallel strands are replicated differently:

- **Leading Strand:** Synthesized continuously in the same direction as fork movement. Only **one RNA primer** is needed. The polymerase proceeds without interruption, synthesizing 5'→3' as the fork advances.

- **Lagging Strand:** Synthesized discontinuously in the direction **opposite** to fork movement. Multiple **Okazaki fragments** (1000–2000 nt in prokaryotes; 100–200 nt in eukaryotes) are synthesized, each requiring its own RNA primer.

**Replisome Composition:**
The leading and lagging strand polymerases are physically coupled as a **replisome** (a single dimeric DNA Pol III holoenzyme in prokaryotes), allowing simultaneous synthesis of both strands.

**(b) Step-by-Step Role of Each Enzyme (7 marks)**

1. **DNA Helicase (DnaB / MCM2-7):** Loaded onto the lagging-strand template; translocates 5'→3' unwinding DNA at ~1000 nt/s using 2 ATP/bp. Creates the replication fork.

2. **Topoisomerase (Gyrase / Topo I, II):** Removes positive supercoils that accumulate ahead of the fork, preventing DNA tangling.

3. **SSB / RPA:** Coats exposed single-stranded DNA; prevents hairpin formation and re-annealing.

4. **Primase (DnaG / Pol α):** Synthesizes short RNA primers (~10 nt) at the origin and repeatedly at the start of each Okazaki fragment.

5. **Sliding Clamp (β-clamp / PCNA):** Loaded by the clamp loader in an ATP-dependent reaction; tethers the polymerase to DNA, increasing processivity from ~10 nt to >10⁵ nt.

6. **DNA Polymerase (Pol III / Pol δ, ε):** Extends the new strand 5'→3' by adding dNTPs complementary to the template. Performs simultaneous synthesis of leading and lagging strands as a dimeric replisome.

7. **DNA Pol I / RNase H + FEN1:** Removes RNA primers using 5'→3' exonuclease (Pol I) or endonucleolytic cleavage (RNase H + FEN1).

8. **DNA Ligase:** Seals the nicks between adjacent Okazaki fragments using ATP (eukaryotes) or NAD⁺ (prokaryotes) to form phosphodiester bonds.

**Diagrammatic Representation:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Direction Confusion:** Students frequently write that DNA polymerase synthesizes 3'→5'. The correct direction is **5'→3'** — this single mistake can cost 2 marks per sub-question.
> 2. **Energy Source Confusion:** Topoisomerase I does **NOT** use ATP (it uses stored DNA energy); only **Topoisomerase II / Gyrase** uses ATP. Mixing these costs marks.
> 3. **Ligase Cofactor Confusion:** **Eukaryotic** DNA ligase uses **ATP**; **Prokaryotic** DNA ligase uses **NAD⁺**. This is a frequently asked 2-mark question.
> 4. **Forgetting Telomerase:** Eukaryotic-only nature of telomerase is often overlooked. Remember: **prokaryotes have circular DNA → no telomere problem**.
> 5. **RNA Primer vs DNA:** Primase makes **RNA**, not DNA. DNA polymerase cannot start de novo — it requires a primer.
> 6. **Pol I vs Pol III:** Students often confuse roles. **Pol III = main replicative enzyme; Pol I = cleanup (primer removal).**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCBBT402 Module 1:**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ DNA replication is **semi-conservative**, **semi-discontinuous**, and proceeds **5'→3'**.
- ☐ The replication fork contains at least **15+ distinct enzymes** working in concert.

### Key Enzymes & Their Roles
- ☐ **Helicase** unwinds DNA (5'→3' translocation; 2 ATP/bp).
- ☐ **Topoisomerase I** cuts one strand (no ATP); **Topo II / Gyrase** cuts both strands (ATP).
- ☐ **SSB / RPA** stabilizes ssDNA.
- ☐ **Primase** makes the **RNA primer** (DNA Pol cannot start de novo).
- ☐ **DNA Pol III** (prokaryotes) is the main replicative polymerase.
- ☐ **DNA Pol δ** = lagging strand; **Pol ε** = leading strand (eukaryotes).
- ☐ **DNA Pol I** removes primers via 5'→3' exonuclease (prokaryotes only).
- ☐ **RNase H + FEN1** remove primers in eukaryotes.
- ☐ **DNA Ligase** seals nicks (ATP in euk, NAD⁺ in prok).
- ☐ **β-clamp / PCNA** is the sliding clamp; loaded by **γ-complex / RFC**.
- ☐ **Telomerase** = reverse transcriptase + RNA template; adds **TTAGGG** repeats.

### High-Yield Numerical Values
- ☐ Replication rate: **1000 nt/s (prokaryotes); 50 nt/s (eukaryotes)**.
- ☐ Fidelity: **$10^{-5}$ → $10^{-8}$ after proofreading → $10^{-10}$ after MMR**.
- ☐ Okazaki fragment size: **1000–2000 nt (prok); 100–200 nt (euk)**.
- ☐ RNA primer length: **~10 nt (prok); ~8–10 nt (euk)**.
- ☐ Number of origins: **1 (E. coli); ~30,000 (human)**.
- ☐ Telomere repeat in humans: **TTAGGG**.
- ☐ Topoisomerase I changes linking number by **$\pm 1$**; Topo II by **$\pm 2$**.

### Drug Targets (Frequently Asked)
- ☐ **Ciprofloxacin** → inhibits DNA Gyrase.
- ☐ **Irinotecan / Topotecan** → inhibit Topo I (cancer chemotherapy).
- ☐ **AZT / Acyclovir** → inhibit viral DNA polymerase / reverse transcriptase.
- ☐ **Camptothecins** → Topo I inhibitors.

### Common Confusion Busters
- ☐ Leading strand = continuous synthesis = only **one primer**.
- ☐ Lagging strand = discontinuous = **many Okazaki fragments**, each with its own primer.
- ☐ DNA Pol **cannot** initiate synthesis — only **extend** from a primer.
- ☐ All DNA polymerases have **3'→5' exonuclease (proofreading)**; only **Pol I** has **5'→3' exonuclease**.
- ☐ **Telomerase is a reverse transcriptase**, not a DNA polymerase.
- ☐ The **lagging strand template** is the one on which helicase is loaded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** When asked about replication enzymes, always answer in the **functional order** of the replication fork: **Helicase → Topoisomerase → SSB → Primase → DNA Pol → Pol I / RNase H → Ligase**. This logical sequencing impresses examiners and ensures you cover every enzyme for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transcription in Prokaryotes and Eukaryotes: Role of Enzymes in Transcription, Major Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transcription in Prokaryotes and Eukaryotes: Role of Enzymes in Transcription, Major Steps # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Transcription** is the enzymatic process by which the genetic information encoded in a DNA template strand is copied into a complementary RNA molecule (primarily mRNA, but also tRNA, rRNA, and other non-coding RNAs) by the enzyme **RNA polymerase (RNAP)**. It represents the first step of gene expression and follows the base-pairing rules: **A–U, T–A, G–C, C–G** (note: Uracil replaces Thymine in RNA). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Transcription is the synthesis of an RNA strand complementary to a DNA template, catalyzed by DNA-dependent RNA polymerase, occurring in the **5' → 3'** direction using the template strand read in the **3' → 5'** direction. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef" Model Imagine the **DNA double helix** as a **locked, leather-bound recipe book** stored safely in a library (the **nucleus** in eukaryotes, or directly in the cytoplasm in prokaryotes). - The **cell** needs a copy of *only one specific recipe* (a gene) to cook a dish (protein). - The cell cannot take the original book out of the library — it might get damaged. So it sends a **scribe** (RNA polymerase) into the library. - The scribe **unlocks** the book at a specific page (the **promoter**), **transcribes** the recipe by hand onto a **working paper** (the **RNA transcript**), and **stops** at a designated "END" marker (the **terminator**). - The working paper (mRNA) is then taken out of the nucleus to the **kitchen** (ribosome) for translation. > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway:** Just as the working copy is **temporary** and used once, mRNA is short-lived and degraded after translation — preserving the original master recipe (DNA). ## 1.3 Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic Transcription — At a Glance | Feature | **Prokaryotes** | **Eukaryotes** | |---|---|---| | Location | Cytoplasm (no nucleus) | Inside the nucleus | | RNA Polymerase | **One type** (single multi-subunit enzyme) | **Three types** — RNAP I, II, III | | Promoter complexity | Simple (−10 Pribnow, −35) | Complex (TATA box, BRE, Inr, DPE) | | Coupling with Translation | Yes (coupled transcription-translation) | No (mRNA processed and exported first) | | RNA Processing | Rare (minimal) | Extensive (5' cap, poly-A tail, splicing) | | Chromatin Remodeling | Not required (no histones in bacteria) | Required (nucleosomes must be unwound) | | Sigma Factors | Single σ factor (σ⁷⁰) | Multiple TFs (TBP, TFIIB, TFIID) | | Terminators | ρ-dependent and ρ-independent | Less defined; cleavage/polyadenylation signals | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Central Players in Transcription ### 2.1.1 The DNA Template - Only **one strand** of DNA is read during transcription, called the **template strand** (or antisense / non-coding strand). - The other strand is the **coding strand** (or sense strand), which has the **same sequence as the mRNA** (with T instead of U). - The transcription unit is bounded by a **promoter** (start) and a **terminator** (end). ### 2.1.2 RNA Polymerase — The Master Enzyme **RNA Polymerase** is the workhorse enzyme. It performs **three catalytic functions**: 1. **Template binding** — recognizes and binds the promoter. 2. **RNA chain elongation** — adds complementary ribonucleotides (NTPs). 3. **RNA chain termination / 3' end processing** — releases the finished RNA. > [!IMPORTANT] > Unlike DNA polymerase, RNA polymerase does **NOT** require a primer to begin synthesis. It can *de novo* initiate RNA synthesis. ## 2.2 The Role of Enzymes in Transcription — A Logical Breakdown ### **A. Prokaryotic System: *E. coli* RNA Polymerase Holoenzyme** The bacterial RNAP core has the structure: **α₂ββ'ω** (5 subunits). - **α subunits (2x):** Promote assembly and bind regulatory proteins. - **β subunit:** Contains the catalytic Mg²⁺ center for NTP addition. - **β' subunit:** Binds the DNA template; contains the active site. - **ω subunit:** Stabilizes β' and aids assembly. For promoter recognition, a **sigma (σ) factor** binds to form the **holoenzyme**: **α₂ββ'ωσ**. - **σ⁷⁰ (housekeeping σ):** Recognizes the standard −10 (Pribnow) and −35 elements. - Other σ factors (e.g., σ³² for heat shock, σ⁵⁴ for nitrogen metabolism) redirect the RNAP to specific gene sets. **Why this matters:** The σ factor is the **molecular address label** that tells the enzyme *which* genes to read. ### **B. Eukaryotic System: Three Specialized RNA Polymerases** | Polymerase | Synthesizes | Location of Action | Key Function | |---|---|---|---| | **RNA Pol I** | Large rRNA (28S, 18S, 5.8S) | Nucleolus | Ribosome biogenesis | | **RNA Pol II** | mRNA, most snRNA, microRNA | Nucleoplasm | Protein-coding gene transcription | | **RNA Pol III** | tRNA, 5S rRNA, U6 snRNA | Nucleoplasm | Small structural/specialized RNAs | > [!NOTE] > For KTU exam purposes, focus primarily on **RNA Pol II** since it transcribes all protein-coding genes (mRNA), which is the most clinically and biotechnologically relevant. ### **C. Eukaryotic General Transcription Factors (GTFs)** Unlike prokaryotes, eukaryotic RNAP II cannot bind promoters on its own. It requires the stepwise assembly of **General Transcription Factors (GTFs)**: 1. **TFIID** (TBP + TAFs): Binds the **TATA box** (TATAAA) at approximately **−25 to −30** upstream of the transcription start site. 2. **TFIIA**: Stabilizes TBP-DNA binding. 3. **TFIIB**: Binds the BRE element; recruits RNAP II. 4. **TFIIF**: Brings RNAP II to the promoter. 5. **TFIIE & TFIIH**: TFIIH has **helicase activity** (unwinds DNA) and **kinase activity** (phosphorylates the C-terminal domain [CTD] of RNAP II to release it into elongation mode). **The pre-initiation complex (PIC) assembly order** is a **very high-yield KTU question**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter / Symbol** | **Value / Definition** | **Significance** | |---|---|---| | **Template strand direction** | Read **3' → 5'** | Mandatory for antiparallel base pairing | | **mRNA synthesis direction** | **5' → 3'** | Same as DNA replication elongation | | **Base pairing rule** | A(U) ↔ T, G ↔ C | Uracil in RNA instead of Thymine | | **Energy source** | **NTPs** (ATP, GTP, CTP, UTP) | Hydrolysis of α-β phosphodiester bond | | **Prokaryotic promoter −10 box (Pribnow)** | TATAAT | Melting of DNA strands | | **Prokaryotic promoter −35 box** | TTGACA | RNAP initial recognition | | **Eukaryotic TATA box position** | ~ **−25 to −30** | Bound by TBP (TFIID) | | **Eukaryotic Inr (Initiator)** | **+1** (YYANWYY) | Marks transcription start site | | **Core enzyme (prokaryote)** | α₂ββ'ω | Cannot recognize promoter | | **Holoenzyme (prokaryote)** | α₂ββ'ωσ | **σ** is the promoter-recognizing subunit | | **ρ factor (rho)** | ATP-dependent helicase | Releases RNA in ρ-dependent termination | | **CTD of RNAP II** | YSPTSPS repeats (×52) | Phosphorylation controls transcription cycle | | **RNA Pol subunits — prokaryotic core** | **β** (catalysis), **β'** (DNA binding) | Two largest subunits | | **5' cap of mRNA** | 7-methylguanosine (m⁷G) | Added co-transcriptionally | | **3' poly-A tail** | ~200 adenines in mammals | Aids export and stability | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Three Major Steps of Transcription Transcription is universally divided into **three phases**: **Initiation → Elongation → Termination**. We treat prokaryotic and eukaryotic systems comparatively. --- ## 3.2 PROKARYOTIC TRANSCRIPTION — Detailed Step-by-Step ### **Step 1: Initiation (Formation of Closed and Open Complexes)** **Sub-step 1.1 — RNAP Holoenzyme Binds Promoter** The σ⁷⁰ factor of the holoenzyme recognizes the **−35 box** (consensus TTGACA) and positions the enzyme. The Pribnow box (−10, TATAAT) is contacted next, anchoring the enzyme. $$ \text{RNAP-}\sigma \; + \; \text{Promoter (P)} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{Closed Complex (RP\textsubscript{c})} $$ **Sub-step 1.2 — DNA Melting (Open Complex Formation)** Around the −10 region, the AT-rich Pribnow box is **denatured** over ~12–14 base pairs, producing a "transcription bubble." This forms the **open complex**: $$ \text{RP\textsubscript{c}} \; \xrightarrow{\text{melting}} \; \text{Open Complex (RP\textsubscript{o})} $$ **Sub-step 1.3 — Synthesis of Short RNA (Abortive Initiation)** The RNAP begins synthesizing short RNA fragments (2–9 nt) without moving. Most are released (abortive). When a transcript ~10 nt forms, the σ factor dissociates and elongation begins. $$ \text{RP\textsubscript{o}} \; + \; \text{NTPs} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{RNAP-DNA-RNA ternary complex} \; \xrightarrow{\text{σ release}} \; \text{Elongation Complex} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why does σ dissociate?** Once elongation starts, σ is no longer needed for stability. Its release allows the RNAP core to transition into a processive elongation mode. --- ### **Step 2: Elongation** The RNAP core moves along the template strand in the **3' → 5'** direction, adding complementary NTPs to the **3' OH** of the growing RNA (so RNA grows **5' → 3'**). For each nucleotide added: $$ \text{RNA}_{n} + \text{NTP} \; \xrightarrow{\text{RNAP, Mg}^{2+}} \; \text{RNA}_{n+1} + \text{PP}_{i} $$ The released **pyrophosphate (PPᵢ)** is hydrolyzed to **2 Pᵢ** by pyrophosphatase, making the reaction **irreversible** (ΔG is large and negative). A "**transcription bubble**" of ~17 bp moves with the enzyme: ~12 nt of RNA remain base-paired to the template at any given moment, with the rest looping out. **Elongation factors in prokaryotes:** *NusA* and *NusG* assist processivity and couple transcription to translation. --- ### **Step 3: Termination** Two distinct mechanisms: #### (a) **ρ-independent termination (Intrinsic)** A **GC-rich hairpin** in the RNA, followed by a run of **uracils (U)**, forms: $$ \text{5'–...GGCCCCUUUUUUA...–3' (RNA)} $$ - The **hairpin** disrupts the RNAP–RNA interaction. - The **U–A base pairs** are weak (only 2 H-bonds between U and A), causing RNAP to pause and dissociate. - No additional protein factor required. #### (b) **ρ-dependent termination** - The **ρ (rho) protein** is a **hexameric helicase** that binds to a **rut site** (ρ-utilization) on the nascent RNA. - Using **ATP hydrolysis**, ρ translocates 5' → 3' along the RNA and **catches up** to a paused RNAP. - ρ's **helicase activity** unwinds the RNA-DNA hybrid, releasing the transcript. $$ \text{ρ-ATP + RNA-DNA hybrid} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{ρ-ADP + P}_{i} + \text{released RNA} $$ --- ## 3.3 EUKARYOTIC TRANSCRIPTION — Detailed Step-by-Step Eukaryotic transcription (focused on **RNAP II**, which transcribes mRNA) is fundamentally similar but with added layers of complexity. ### **Step 1: Initiation — Assembly of the Pre-Initiation Complex (PIC)** 1. **TFIID** (TBP + TAFs) binds the **TATA box** (TATAAA at −25 to −30), bending the DNA by ~80°. 2. **TFIIA** and **TFIIB** bind, stabilizing the complex and marking the start site. 3. **TFIIF** brings **RNAP II** to the promoter. 4. **TFIIE** recruits **TFIIH**. 5. **TFIIH** has two critical activities: - **Helicase activity** (XPB and XPD subunits) — melts the DNA to form the open complex. - **Kinase activity** — phosphorylates the Ser-5 residues of the RNAP II CTD heptad repeat (YSPTSPS). $$ \text{CTD Ser5} \; \xrightarrow{\text{TFIIH kinase}} \; \text{CTD Ser5-PO}_{4} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{Initiator → Elongation switch} $$ 6. **Promoter clearance**: After ~25–30 nt of RNA is synthesized, the PIC disassembles (most GTFs released) and RNAP II enters elongation mode. --- ### **Step 2: Elongation — Coupled with RNA Processing** A hallmark of eukaryotic transcription is that **5' capping, splicing, and 3' cleavage/polyadenylation occur co-transcriptionally**. | Co-transcriptional Event | Enzyme Complex | Function | |---|---|---| | **5' capping** | Capping enzyme (guanylyl transferase, methyl transferase) | Adds 7-methylguanosine (m⁷G) cap via 5'–5' triphosphate bridge; protects from exonucleases and is essential for ribosome binding. | | **Splicing** | Spliceosome (snRNPs: U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) | Removes introns; joins exons. | | **3' cleavage and polyadenylation** | CPSF, CstF, Poly(A) Polymerase (PAP) | Cleaves RNA ~10–30 nt downstream of AAUAAA signal; adds ~200 A residues (poly-A tail). | > [!IMPORTANT] > The CTD phosphorylation pattern dynamically changes during transcription: > - **Ser5-P** → peaks at initiation; recruits capping enzymes. > - **Ser2-P** → peaks in elongation; recruits splicing and 3' processing factors. **Elongation factors in eukaryotes:** **TFIIS, SPT5, P-TEFb** (which phosphorylates Ser2). --- ### **Step 3: Termination** Unlike prokaryotes, eukaryotic termination is not defined by a hairpin. Instead: 1. The polyadenylation signal (**AAUAAA**) is recognized by **CPSF** and **CstF**. 2. The RNA is cleaved ~10–30 nt downstream. 3. **Poly(A) Polymerase (PAP)** adds the poly-A tail. 4. The remaining "downstream" RNA fragment is degraded by **XRN2** (a 5' → 3' exonuclease), which catches up with and disassembles the RNAP II from the DNA template. This is known as the **"torpedo model"** of eukaryotic termination. --- ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic Transcription | **Parameter** | **Prokaryotes** | **Eukaryotes** | |---|---|---| | Number of RNAPs | 1 | 3 (Pol I, II, III) | | Promoter sequences | −35, −10 (Pribnow) | TATA, BRE, Inr, DPE, enhancer | | σ / GTFs | σ factor | TFIID, TFIIA, B, E, F, H | | Initiation rate | Fast | Slow (complex assembly) | | RNA processing | Minimal | 5' cap, splicing, poly-A | | Transcription unit | Polycistronic mRNA (operons) | Monocistronic mRNA | | Chromatin | No histones | Nucleosomes; histone modifications needed | | Termination | Hairpin/U or ρ factor | Poly-A signal + torpedo (XRN2) | | Coupling with translation | Yes (in cytoplasm) | No (separated by nuclear envelope) | | Regulator elements | Operators | Enhancers, silencers (can be far away) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Prokaryotic Transcription Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD DNA["Double-stranded DNA
Template strand 3 to 5"] -->|A. RNAP sigma holoenzyme scans| A1["Sigma factor recognizes -35 box"] A1 --> A2["RNAP binds -10 Pribnow box"] A2 --> A3["DNA melting to form open complex"] A3 --> A4["Abortive initiation short RNA 2 to 9 nt"] A4 --> A5["Sigma factor release"] A5 --> B1["Elongation phase begins"] B1 --> B2["NTPs added 5 to 3 direction"] B2 --> B3["Transcription bubble 17 bp moves"] B3 --> C1["Termination signal reached"] C1 --> C2{"Hairpin with U-rich tract?"} C2 -->|Yes| C3["Rho-independent termination"] C2 -->|No| C4["Rho protein binds rut site"] C4 --> C5["Rho helicase unwinds RNA-DNA hybrid"] C3 --> D1["RNA transcript released"] C5 --> D1["RNA transcript released"] D1 --> E1["Prokaryotic mRNA ready for translation"] ``` ## 4.2 Eukaryotic Transcription Initiation (RNAP II PIC Assembly) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["TATA box at position -25 to -30"] --> B["TFIID with TBP binds TATA box"] B --> C["TFIIA and TFIIB bind"] C --> D["TFIIF recruits RNAP II"] D --> E["TFIIE recruits TFIIH"] E --> F["TFIIH helicase melts DNA"] F --> G["TFIIH kinase phosphorylates CTD Ser5"] G --> H["Pre-initiation complex PIC complete"] H --> I["Promoter clearance and sigma like release"] I --> J["Elongation with Ser2 phosphorylation"] J --> K["Co-transcriptional 5 prime capping"] K --> L["Co-transcriptional splicing by spliceosome"] L --> M["3 prime cleavage at AAUAAA signal"] M --> N["Poly-A tail added by PAP"] N --> O["XRN2 torpedo triggers termination"] O --> P["Mature mRNA exported to cytoplasm"] ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Process Flow: Termination Mechanisms ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PROC["Prokaryotic Termination"] P1["Hairpin and U-rich tract"] --> P2["RNAP pauses"] P2 --> P3["RNA released"] P4["Rho binds rut site"] --> P5["Rho translocates using ATP"] P5 --> P6["Helicase unwinds hybrid"] P6 --> P3 end subgraph EUK["Eukaryotic RNAP II Termination"] E1["AAUAAA signal recognized"] --> E2["CPSF and CstF cleave RNA"] E2 --> E3["Poly-A tail added"] E3 --> E4["XRN2 exonuclease degrades downstream RNA"] E4 --> E5["Torpedo catches RNAP II"] E5 --> E6["RNAP II released"] end ``` ## 4.4 Roles of Each Subunit in Bacterial RNAP — Schematic Block ```mermaid flowchart TB Core["Core enzyme alpha2 beta beta prime omega"] --> Holo["Holoenzyme alpha2 beta beta prime omega sigma"] Holo --> F1["Sigma - Recognizes promoter at minus 35 and minus 10"] Holo --> F2["Beta - Catalytic center with Mg2+ for NTP addition"] Holo --> F3["Beta prime - Binds template DNA strand"] Holo --> F4["Omega - Assists folding of beta prime and stabilizes core"] Holo --> F5["Alpha - Dimerization, interaction with upstream activator proteins"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **Differentiate between the template strand and the coding strand during transcription. Why is the coding strand's sequence identical to the mRNA (with one exception)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **template strand** is the DNA strand actually read (3' → 5') by RNA polymerase, while the **coding strand** is the complementary, non-read strand (3 marks). - The mRNA synthesized is **antiparallel and complementary** to the template strand; therefore, it has the **same sequence as the coding strand** (except **U replaces T**), because both are complementary to the same template (≈1 mark in extended answers). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **Name the three eukaryotic RNA polymerases and specify the RNA product of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **RNA Pol I** → synthesizes large rRNAs (28S, 18S, 5.8S) in the nucleolus (1 mark). - **RNA Pol II** → synthesizes mRNA, most snRNAs, and microRNAs in the nucleoplasm (1 mark). - **RNA Pol III** → synthesizes tRNA, 5S rRNA, and U6 snRNA in the nucleoplasm (1 mark). --- ## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly confuse the **directionality** of template reading vs RNA synthesis. Always clearly state: **Template is read 3' → 5'** and **RNA is synthesized 5' → 3'**. Marks are lost for stating them in the same direction. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** **(a)** Describe the **role of RNA polymerase in prokaryotic transcription** with a well-labeled explanation of the **core enzyme and holoenzyme**. Highlight the function of the sigma factor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **mechanism of rho-dependent and rho-independent termination** in prokaryotes. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Role of Prokaryotic RNA Polymerase (7 marks):** 1. **Composition of RNAP core enzyme:** The *E. coli* RNA polymerase core enzyme has the subunit structure **α₂ββ'ω**, totalling ~400 kDa (1 mark). 2. **Role of each subunit:** - **α subunits:** Dimerization platform; interact with regulatory transcription factors (1 mark). - **β subunit:** Forms the catalytic site containing two **Mg²⁺** ions that coordinate the chemistry of NTP addition (1 mark). - **β' subunit:** Contains the **DNA-binding channel** that grips the template strand; also binds the antibiotic **rifampicin** (1 mark). - **ω subunit:** Helps fold β' and stabilizes the assembled core (0.5 mark). 3. **Holoenzyme formation:** Binding of the **sigma (σ) factor**, usually σ⁷⁰, to the core enzyme produces the **holoenzyme (α₂ββ'ωσ)**, which is the promoter-competent form (1 mark). 4. **Function of σ factor:** It recognizes the **−35 (TTGACA)** and **−10 (TATAAT)** consensus promoter elements and is essential for **promoter melting** (DNA strand separation) at the Pribnow box to form the open complex. σ dissociates after synthesis of ~10 nt of RNA (1 mark). 5. **Three catalytic activities** of RNAP: template binding, chain elongation, and chain termination/release (0.5 mark). **(b) Rho-Dependent and Rho-Independent Termination (7 marks):** 1. **Rho-independent (intrinsic) termination (3.5 marks):** - Requires a **GC-rich inverted repeat** in the nascent RNA, followed by a **stretch of uracils (U-rich tract)** (1 mark). - The inverted repeat folds into a **stable hairpin** that disrupts the transcription bubble and causes the RNAP to **pause** (1 mark). - The U-A base pairs (only 2 H-bonds) in the RNA-DNA hybrid are weak, so the RNA spontaneously **dissociates** (1 mark). - No additional protein factor is required (0.5 mark). 2. **Rho-dependent termination (3.5 marks):** - The **ρ (rho) protein** is a **hexameric ATP-dependent helicase** that binds the **rut site** (rho utilization site, ~80 nt of C-rich, G-poor RNA) on the nascent RNA (1 mark). - ρ hydrolyzes **ATP** to translocate 5' → 3' along the RNA, chasing the paused RNAP (1 mark). - Upon catching up, ρ's **helicase activity** unwinds the RNA-DNA hybrid in the transcription bubble (1 mark). - This releases the transcript and **dissociates the RNAP** from the DNA (0.5 mark). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **assembly of the pre-initiation complex (PIC) for RNA Polymerase II in eukaryotes**. Name the major transcription factors involved and their functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **co-transcriptional processing of eukaryotic mRNA** — covering the 5' cap, splicing, and 3' polyadenylation — and the role of the **CTD of RNAP II** in coordinating these events. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Assembly of the Pre-Initiation Complex for RNA Pol II (7 marks):** 1. **TFIID (TBP + TAFs) binds the TATA box** at position −25 to −30, causing a sharp ~80° **bend** in the DNA (1 mark). 2. **TFIIA and TFIIB** bind next, stabilizing TBP-DNA contact and contacting the **BRE (TFIIB recognition element)**; TFIIB also marks the transcription start site (1 mark). 3. **TFIIF** escorts **RNA Pol II** to the promoter and prevents non-specific DNA binding (1 mark). 4. **TFIIE** binds and recruits **TFIIH** to the complex (0.5 mark). 5. **TFIIH functions:** - **XPB and XPD** helicase subunits **melt the DNA** to form the open complex (~11 bp) (1 mark). - **Kinase activity** phosphorylates the **Ser-5 residues** of the CTD heptad repeats of RNAP II → triggers promoter clearance and escape (1 mark). 6. After synthesis of ~25–30 nt, the PIC disassembles (most GTFs released), and RNAP II enters **processive elongation** (0.5 mark). **Order summary (high-yield line for KTU):** $$ \text{TFIID} \rightarrow \text{TFIIA, TFIIB} \rightarrow \text{TFIIF + Pol II} \rightarrow \text{TFIIE} \rightarrow \text{TFIIH} $$ **(b) Co-transcriptional Processing of mRNA and Role of CTD (7 marks):** 1. **5' Capping (2 marks):** - Occurs co-transcriptionally when RNA reaches ~25 nt. - The **γ-phosphate of the 5' end is removed** by RNA triphosphatase. - A **GMP** is added by guanylyl transferase, forming a **5'–5' triphosphate bridge**. - The N-7 position of the guanosine is **methylated** to form **7-methylguanosine (m⁷G)** cap. - Function: protects from 5' exonucleases; required for nuclear export; recognized by eIF4E for translation initiation. 2. **Splicing (2.5 marks):** - **Introns** are removed and **exons** joined by the **spliceosome**, a complex of five snRNPs (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) and associated proteins. - The mechanism proceeds via two **transesterification reactions**, generating a **lariat intermediate**. - Splicing can be **alternative**, producing different mRNAs and protein isoforms from a single gene. 3. **3' Cleavage and Polyadenylation (2.5 marks):** - The signal sequence **AAUAAA** is recognized by **CPSF** (cleavage and polyadenylation specificity factor). - **CstF** binds a downstream GU-rich sequence. - Endonucleolytic cleavage occurs ~10–30 nt downstream of AAUAAA. - **Poly(A) Polymerase (PAP)** adds ~200 adenine residues to form the **poly-A tail**. - Function: enhances nuclear export, translation efficiency, and mRNA stability. 4. **Role of the CTD (heptad YSPTSPS, ×52 repeats):** - **Ser5 phosphorylation** peaks near the promoter → recruits **capping enzymes**. - **Ser2 phosphorylation** peaks in elongation → recruits **splicing factors and 3' processing complexes**. - **Tyr1 and Thr4** also have regulatory roles. - The CTD thus acts as a **"molecular scaffold"** coupling transcription to RNA processing — this is one of the most elegant examples of co-transcriptional gene regulation (1 mark). > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Answers:** > 1. Confusing the **roles of TFIIH** (helicase vs kinase). State them as **two separate functions**. > 2. Writing "polymerase reads DNA 5' to 3'" — **WRONG**. Template is read **3' → 5'**. > 3. Forgetting to mention that **σ factor dissociates** before elongation. Examiners specifically look for this. > 4. Confusing **rho-dependent and rho-independent** mechanisms. Remember: **hairpin + U-tract = rho-independent**. > 5. Not mentioning the **directionality (5' → 3')** of RNA synthesis. Mandatory for full marks. --- ## **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER** > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transcription in Prokaryotes and Eukaryotes** - **Definition:** Synthesis of RNA from a DNA template by RNA polymerase, producing mRNA, tRNA, or rRNA. - **Directionality:** Template read **3' → 5'**; RNA synthesized **5' → 3'**; antiparallel base pairing (A–U, T–A, G–C). - **Enzymes (prokaryote):** Single RNAP; holoenzyme = **α₂ββ'ωσ**; σ⁷⁰ for housekeeping genes. - **Enzymes (eukaryote):** **Pol I** (rRNA), **Pol II** (mRNA — most important), **Pol III** (tRNA, 5S rRNA). - **Promoter elements (prokaryote):** −35 (TTGACA) and −10 (TATAAT, Pribnow box). - **Promoter elements (eukaryote):** TATA box, BRE, Inr, DPE; plus enhancers/silencers (often far away). - **PIC assembly order:** TFIID → TFIIA → TFIIB → TFIIF + Pol II → TFIIE → TFIIH. - **TFIIH roles:** **Helicase** (XPB, XPD) + **Kinase** (phosphorylates CTD Ser5). - **Three phases:** Initiation → Elongation → Termination. - **Termination in prokaryotes:** (i) **Rho-independent** = GC hairpin + U-rich tract; (ii) **Rho-dependent** = ρ helicase catches RNAP via rut site. - **Termination in eukaryotes:** **AAUAAA signal** recognized by CPSF → cleavage → **Poly-A tail** by PAP → **XRN2 torpedo** degrades downstream RNA → RNAP II released. - **Co-transcriptional modifications in eukaryotes:** 5' **m⁷G cap**, **splicing** (spliceosome), **3' poly-A tail** — all coupled to the CTD phosphorylation state. - **CTD of RNAP II:** YSPTSPS heptad × ~52; **Ser5-P** recruits capping; **Ser2-P** recruits splicing/3' processing. - **Key contrast:** Prokaryotic transcription-translation is **coupled**; eukaryotic is **compartmentalized** (nucleus vs cytoplasm). - **Operons:** Prokaryotes transcribe **polycistronic** mRNA; eukaryotes make **monocistronic** mRNA. - **Antibiotic target (high-yield clinical link):** **Rifampicin** inhibits bacterial RNAP β subunit; **Actinomycin D** binds DNA and blocks both transcription and replication. - **Energy source:** **NTPs**; pyrophosphate (PPᵢ) hydrolysis by pyrophosphatase drives the reaction forward. - **No primer needed** — RNA polymerase initiates RNA synthesis de novo.

topics.title: Transcription Initiation Complex in Prokaryotes and Eukaryotes and Its Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transcription Initiation Complex in Prokaryotes and Eukaryotes and Its Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transcription Initiation Complex (TIC):** A multi-component nucleoprotein assembly formed at the gene promoter that recruits RNA polymerase (RNAP) and positions it at the transcription start site (TSS, designated **+1**), melts the double-stranded DNA, and initiates phosphodiester bond formation between the first ribonucleoside triphosphates (rNTPs).

In **prokaryotes**, the TIC consists of a single multi-subunit **RNA polymerase holoenzyme** (core enzyme + a sigma (σ) factor) bound to the promoter at conserved **−35 (TTGACA)** and **−10 (TATAAT)** hexamers.

In **eukaryotes**, the equivalent assembly — the **Pre-Initiation Complex (PIC)** — comprises **RNA Polymerase II (Pol II)**, six **General Transcription Factors (GTFs: TFIID, TFIIA, TFIIB, TFIIE, TFIIF, TFIIH)**, the **Mediator complex**, and co-activators, assembling over the core promoter elements: **TATA box (TATAAA, ~−25 to −30)**, **BRE (TFIIB recognition element, −35 to −40)**, **Inr (Initiator, around +1)**, and **DPE (Downstream Promoter Element, +28 to +32)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Molecular Casting Director":**
> Think of transcription initiation as staging a theatrical play. The **promoter** is the **stage** with painted markers (foot-lights). **RNA polymerase** is the **lead actor** who can only enter once told where to stand. The **sigma factor (prokaryotes)** or **TFIID + Mediator (eukaryotes)** is the **stage manager** who shows the actor to the correct spotlight position. The **−10 / −35 boxes** (prokaryotes) and **TATA / Inr / DPE** (eukaryotes) are the **glowing markers on the stage floor** that tell everyone exactly where to position. **Activators and repressors** are the **producers** — they can either turn up the spotlight (turn ON) or pull the curtain (turn OFF). The **chromatin** in eukaryotes is the **heavy velvet curtain** that often needs to be pulled aside (remodelled) before the play can even begin.

### 1.3 Standard Molecular Metrics

| Metric | Prokaryote | Eukaryote |
|---|---|---|
| RNA Polymerase mass | ~ **400 kDa** | Pol II ~ **550 kDa** |
| Holoenzyme subunits | **5** (α₂ββ'ωσ) | **12+** (Pol II + GTFs) |
| Promoter length recognized | ~ **60 bp** | ~ **80–120 bp** |
| TSS designation | **+1** | **+1** |
| Typical gene density | ~ **1 gene / 1 kbp** | ~ **1 gene / 100 kbp** |
| Operon presence | **Yes** (polycistronic) | **Absent** (monocistronic) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Prokaryotic Transcription Initiation Complex (TIC)

#### 2.1.1 Components of the *E. coli* Holoenzyme

The **RNA polymerase core enzyme (E)** has the stoichiometry **α₂ββ'ω** (~ **400 kDa**, 5 subunits) and synthesises RNA. It is catalytically competent but cannot locate promoters on its own. The **sigma (σ) factor** is a separate, **dissociable** protein (~ **70 kDa**, σ⁷⁰ being the housekeeping sigma) that confers **promoter specificity**.

The functional **holoenzyme (Eσ)** is:

$$E + \sigma \;\rightleftharpoons\; E\sigma \quad (K_d \approx 10^{-9}\,\text{M})$$

#### 2.1.2 Promoter Architecture in Prokaryotes

Four canonical consensus elements (numbered relative to TSS = **+1**):

| Element | Position (relative to +1) | Consensus | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UP element** | **−40 to −60** | AT-rich | Binds the **α-CTD** (α-carboxy terminal domain) of RNAP |
| **−35 box** | **−35** | **5'-TTGACA-3'** | Initial recognition by **σ region 4** |
| **−16 element** | **−16** | TRTG (R = purine) | Stabilises open complex (extended −10) |
| **−10 box (Pribnow)** | **−10** | **5'-TATAAT-3'** | DNA melting, A-T rich for easy strand separation |
| **Discriminator** | **−6 to +1** | GC-rich in rRNA | Initial transcribed region |

#### 2.1.3 Step-wise Assembly of the Prokaryotic TIC

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Sequential Stages of Prokaryotic Transcription Initiation:**

1. **Formation of the closed complex (RPc):** Eσ binds the duplex promoter. The **−35** is contacted by **σ region 4.2**; the **−10** is contacted by **σ region 2.4**. DNA is still base-paired.
2. **Isomerisation to the open complex (RPo):** σ region 2.3 (an "aromatic wedge", rich in Phe/Tyr) inserts into the minor groove, **melting ~12–14 bp** from −11 to +3. A **transcription bubble** is created. This is the rate-limiting step.
3. **Initial transcribing complex (ITC) / Abortive initiation:** RNAP synthesises short 2–9 nt RNA chains without promoter clearance, releasing them and re-starting.
4. **Promoter clearance (escape):** Once RNA grows beyond ~10 nt, σ region 3.2 "σ finger" is displaced from the RNA exit channel, σ factor is **released** (or partly retained), and the polymerase transitions to the **elongation complex (RPe)**.
5. **Elongation / Termination:** σ-independent processivity, modulated by **NusA**, **NusG**, **RfaH**, terminated by **ρ** or hairpin.

#### 2.1.4 Why the −10 Box is A-T Rich

The hydrogen-bonding energy of an **A–T pair (~ 2 H-bonds)** is less than that of a **G–C pair (~ 3 H-bonds)**. Melting therefore requires less free energy, and the **ΔG of bubble formation** is more favourable for AT-rich sequences.

$$\Delta G_{\text{melt}} \propto \text{GC content of the } -10 \text{ box}$$

### 2.2 The Eukaryotic Transcription Initiation Complex (PIC)

#### 2.2.1 The Three Nuclear RNA Polymerases

| Polymerase | Cellular Location | Transcribes | α-Amanitin Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RNA Pol I** | Nucleolus | 45S pre-rRNA (28S, 18S, 5.8S) | **Insensitive** |
| **RNA Pol II** | Nucleoplasm | mRNA, most snRNA, miRNA | **Highly sensitive** (inhibited at 1 µg/mL) |
| **RNA Pol III** | Nucleoplasm | tRNA, 5S rRNA, U6 snRNA | Moderately sensitive |

#### 2.2.2 General Transcription Factors (GTFs) for Pol II

| GTF | Subunits | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **TFIID** (TBP + TAFs) | TBP + ~13 TAFs | **TBP** bends DNA at TATA; **TAFs** recognise Inr, DPE |
| **TFIIA** | 2 (α, β) | Stabilises TBP-DNA; anti-repression |
| **TFIIB** | 1 | Bridges TBP and Pol II; selects TSS |
| **TFIIF** | 2 (RAP30, RAP74) | Recruits Pol II; prevents non-specific binding |
| **TFIIE** | 2 (α, β) | Recruits & regulates TFIIH |
| **TFIIH** | 9 (XPB, XPD, CDK7, CycH, MAT1, p62, p52, p44, p34) | **XPB/XPD** = ATPase/helicase melts DNA; **CDK7** phosphorylates CTD of Pol II at **Ser-5** |

#### 2.2.3 Core Promoter Elements in Eukaryotes

| Element | Position | Consensus | Recognised by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BREᵘ / BREᵈ** | −38 / −23 | SSRCGCC / RGWYV | TFIIB |
| **TATA box** | −30 to −25 | **TATA(A/T)A(A/T)** | TBP |
| **Inr** | −2 to +5 | **YYANWYY** | TAFs (TAF1, TAF2) |
| **MTE** | +18 to +27 | CSARCA | TAF1 |
| **DPE** | +28 to +32 | **RGWYV** | TAF6/TAF9 |

#### 2.2.4 Step-wise Assembly of the Eukaryotic PIC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ordered Assembly Pathway (TBP-Dependent, TATA-box promoter):**

1. **TBP binding:** TFIID (TBP) binds and **bends DNA ~80°** at the TATA box, creating a saddle-shaped deformation that opens the minor groove.
2. **TFIIA and TFIIB recruitment:** TFIIA stabilises TBP; TFIIB docks downstream and reads the **BRE**.
3. **Pol II + TFIIF recruitment:** TFIIF escorts Pol II to the promoter.
4. **TFIIE recruitment:** TFIIE binds Pol II and recruits **TFIIH**.
5. **TFIIH action:** **XPB** (3'→5' DNA helicase) and **XPD** (5'→3') unwind DNA; **CDK7** (kinase subunit) phosphorylates the **CTD heptad repeat (YSPTSPS)** of Pol II at **Ser-5**, triggering promoter clearance.
6. **Promoter escape:** Pol II disengages the GTFs and begins elongation, with CTD now phosphorylated at **Ser-2** (by P-TEFb) for elongation.

### 2.3 Regulation of Transcription Initiation

#### 2.3.1 Prokaryotic Regulation — The Operon Model (Lac Operon as Paradigm)

**Components:** Structural genes (**lacZ, lacY, lacA**), promoter (**P**), operator (**O**), regulatory gene (**lacI**), and the **CAP-cAMP** activator site.

| Condition | Repressor | cAMP | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glucose **absent**, Lactose **present** | **Inactive** (bound to allolactose) | **High** | CAP-cAMP binds → **Strong transcription** |
| Glucose **present**, Lactose **absent** | Active (binds O) | Low | No activator + repression → **No transcription** |
| Glucose **present**, Lactose **present** | Inactive | Low | No CAP binding → **Weak transcription** (basal only) |
| Glucose **absent**, Lactose **absent** | Active | High | **No transcription** (repressed) |

**Trp Operon:** Repressible — the **Trp repressor** is **inactive** until it binds its co-repressor **tryptophan**, which then binds the operator to shut down biosynthesis (negative feedback).

#### 2.3.2 Eukaryotic Regulation — Multi-Layered Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Eukaryotic Gene Regulation occurs at FIVE major checkpoints, but initiation is the master switch:**

1. **Chromatin level** — Histone acetylation (HATs) / deacetylation (HDACs); chromatin remodelling complexes (**SWI/SNF, ISWI, CHD, INO80**).
2. **Transcription factor level** — **Activators** (e.g., Sp1, AP-1, NF-κB) bind **Enhancers**; **Repressors** bind **Silencers**.
3. **Mediator complex** — bridges activators and Pol II; integrates signals.
4. **Epigenetic level** — **DNA methylation** (CpG islands silenced); **non-coding RNAs** (lncRNA, eRNA).
5. **CTD phosphorylation code** — **Ser-5** (initiation → capping), **Ser-2** (elongation), **Ser-7** (snRNA genes), **Tyr-1** (termination), **Thr-4** (histone mRNA 3' end).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations and Constants for KTU 2024 ESE Valuation:**

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| RNA polymerase holoenzyme (prokaryote) | $E + \sigma \rightleftharpoons E\sigma$ | $K_d \sim 10^{-9}$ M |
| Closed complex → Open complex | $RP_c \xrightarrow{k_i} RPo$ | $k_i$ is the isomerisation rate constant |
| Promoter strength (consensus) | $P_{\text{strength}} \propto \frac{k_{cat}}{K_M^{promoter}}$ | "Strong" promoters match consensus |
| Free energy of bubble formation | $\Delta G_{\text{melt}} = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | AT-rich ⇒ less positive ΔG |
| Equilibrium of repressor–operator | $[RO] = \frac{[R][O]}{K_d}$ | High $K_d$ = weak repression |
| Lac operon — catabolite repression | $[cAMP] \propto \frac{1}{[glucose]}$ | Low glucose → high cAMP |
| CTD repeat unit | $Y^{1}S^{2}P^{3}T^{4}S^{5}P^{6}S^{7}$ | Ser-5 phosphorylated at initiation |
| Polymerase subunit mass (Pol II) | $M_{Pol\,II} \approx 550\,\text{kDa}$ | 12 subunits, RPB1–RPB12 |
| TATA box consensus | $\text{5'-TATA(A/T)A(A/T)-3'}$ | Position −30 to −25 |
| −10 box consensus | $\text{5'-TATAAT-3'}$ | Position −10 |
| −35 box consensus | $\text{5'-TTGACA-3'}$ | Position −35 |
| RNA/DNA hybrid length (transcription bubble) | $\sim 8 \text{–} 9$ bp hybrid + 12 bp melted | in elongation complex |
| Bacterial mRNA half-life | $\sim 2$–**4 min** | vs. eukaryotic hours–days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering / Real-world utility:** Synthetic biology exploits these motifs by engineering **hybrid promoters** (e.g., **Tac**, **Trc**, **Plac/ara-1**) that combine strong prokaryotic −10/−35 with operator sites for **tunable, IPTG- or arabinose-inducible gene expression** in recombinant protein production (insulin, vaccines, enzymes).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Thermodynamic Basis of the −10 Box Melting (Quantitative Insight)

We treat the −10 region melting as the equilibrium between the closed complex $RP_c$ and the open complex $RP_o$. The standard free energy change is:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq} = -RT \ln \frac{[RP_o]}{[RP_c]}$$

For the **−10 box** containing *n* base pairs to be melted, the enthalpy and entropy contributions of hydrogen-bond breaking and stacking disruption are:

$$\Delta H_{\text{melt}} = n \cdot \Delta h_{bp} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta S_{\text{melt}} = n \cdot \Delta s_{bp}$$

Substituting the consensus **TATAAT** (six base pairs, ~ 4 AT, 2 GC):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_{\text{melt}} &= \Delta H_{\text{melt}} - T\Delta S_{\text{melt}} \\
&= (4 \cdot \Delta h_{AT} + 2 \cdot \Delta h_{GC}) - T(4 \cdot \Delta s_{AT} + 2 \cdot \Delta s_{GC})
\end{aligned}$$

Using literature values at 310 K: $\Delta h_{AT} \approx 7.2\,\text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta h_{GC} \approx 8.8\,\text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta s_{bp} \approx 22\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{\text{melt}} &= (4 \times 7.2) + (2 \times 8.8) = 28.8 + 17.6 = 46.4\,\text{kJ/mol} \\
\Delta S_{\text{melt}} &= 6 \times 22 = 132\,\text{J/(mol·K)} = 0.132\,\text{kJ/(mol·K)} \\
T\Delta S_{\text{melt}} &= 310 \times 0.132 = 40.92\,\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta G_{\text{melt}} = 46.4 - 40.92 = +5.48\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

This small **positive** value is what makes melting feasible: σ region 2.3 supplies additional binding energy (ΔG of RNAP–DNA contacts ≈ −30 to −40 kJ/mol) that **overwhelms** this unfavourable value, driving $RP_c \rightarrow RP_o$.

### 3.2 Deriving the Steady-State Expression of a Repressed Promoter (Prokaryotic Model)

Let the **active repressor (R)** bind operator (O) with dissociation constant $K_d$:

$$R + O \;\rightleftharpoons\; RO \quad ; \quad K_d = \frac{[R][O]}{[RO]}$$

At any time, the total operator is conserved: $[O]_T = [O] + [RO]$, and total repressor $[R]_T = [R] + [RO]$ (assuming no autoregulation). Solving for the **fractional occupancy** of the operator (i.e., the fraction of promoters repressed):

$$\begin{aligned}
[RO] &= \frac{[O]_T [R]_T}{K_d + [R]_T} \\
\theta_{\text{repressed}} &= \frac{[RO]}{[O]_T} = \frac{[R]_T}{K_d + [R]_T}
\end{aligned}$$

When an **inducer** (e.g., allolactose, IPTG) is present at concentration $[I]$ and binds $R$ with constant $K_i$, the **free active repressor** becomes:

$$[R]_{\text{active}} = \frac{[R]_T}{1 + \frac{[I]}{K_i}}$$

Thus, the **fraction of derepressed promoters** is:

$$\theta_{\text{active}} = 1 - \theta_{\text{repressed}} = 1 - \frac{[R]_{\text{active}}}{K_d + [R]_{\text{active}}}$$

**Inference:** Increasing $[I]$ (inducer) or $K_d$ (weaker repressor binding) drives $\theta_{\text{active}} \rightarrow 1$ (full induction). This is the basis of **lac operon induction by IPTG** in recombinant technology.

### 3.3 Eukaryotic CTD Phosphorylation "Code" Logic

The CTD of Pol II's largest subunit, **RPB1**, contains a heptad repeat **YSPTSPS** repeated **52 times** in mammals. Specific phosphorylations signal transition through the transcription cycle:

| Stage | Kinase | Residue modified | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| PIC assembly | — | None | Hypophosphorylated (Pol IIA) |
| Initiation | **CDK7** (TFIIH) | **Ser-5** | Recruits capping enzyme (RNGTT, RNMT) → 5' cap |
| Early elongation | **CDK9** (P-TEFb) | **Ser-2** | Recruits elongation factors (Spt5, PAF1) |
| Elongation | **CDK12/13** | **Ser-2** (sustained) | Recruits splicing & 3' end factors |
| snRNA genes | **CDK7** | **Ser-7** | snRNA 3' processing |
| Termination | CDK (?) | **Tyr-1, Thr-4** | Cleavage/polyadenylation |

This is a **binary molecular switch** and a classic KTU high-yield topic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Prokaryotic Transcription Initiation Complex Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free RNAP Core alpha2 beta beta prime omega] --> B[RNAP Core]
    C[Sigma 70 factor] --> B
    B --> D[E sigma Holoenzyme]
    D --> E[1. Scanning: Holoenzyme binds -35 box via sigma region 4.2]
    E --> F[2. Recognition: sigma region 2.4 contacts -10 TATAAT]
    F --> G[3. Closed Complex RPc duplex DNA]
    G --> H[4. Isomerisation: sigma region 2.3 aromatic wedge melts -11 to +3]
    H --> I[5. Open Complex RPo transcription bubble formed]
    I --> J[6. Abortive Initiation: 2 to 9 nt RNA released repeatedly]
    J --> K[7. Promoter Clearance: sigma displaced, RNA 10 nt]
    K --> L[8. Elongation Complex RPe: NusA NusG bound, rho or hairpin termination]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Eukaryotic Pol II Pre-Initiation Complex Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[DNA Promoter TATA Inr BRE DPE] --> A
    A[TBP subunit of TFIID binds TATA box and bends DNA 80 degrees] --> B
    B[TFIIA stabilises TBP on TATA] --> C
    C[TFIIB docks downstream reads BRE element] --> D
    D[TFIIF escorts RNA Pol II to promoter] --> E
    E[TFIIE binds Pol II and recruits TFIIH] --> F
    F[TFIIH XPB XPD helicases melt DNA around TSS plus CDK7 phosphorylates Ser-5 of CTD] --> G
    G[Pre Initiation Complete PIC Ser-5 P CTD]
    H[Mediator Complex bridged by activators at Enhancer] -.-> F
    I[Co-activators HATs acetylate histones open chromatin] -.-> A
    G --> J[Promoter Escape Pol II Ser-2 P enters Elongation]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Lac Operon Regulation (Negative + Positive)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Glucose Level] -->|Low| cAMPhigh[High cAMP]
    G -->|High| cAMPLow[Low cAMP]
    L[Lactose Level] -->|Present| ALL[Allolactose binds LacI repressor]
    L -->|Absent| NOALL[No allolactose]
    ALL --> REPoff[LacI repressor INACTIVE cannot bind Operator]
    NOALL --> REPon[LacI repressor ACTIVE binds Operator O blocks RNAP]
    cAMPhigh --> CAPon[CAP cAMP complex binds Promoter]
    cAMPLow --> CAPoff[No CAP binding]
    REPoff --> Q1{Repressed}
    REPon --> Q1
    CAPon --> Q1
    CAPoff --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Off, Off| OUT1[No Transcription]
    Q1 -->|Off, On| OUT2[Strong Transcription MWC induction]
    Q1 -->|On, On| OUT3[No Transcription Operon off]
    Q1 -->|On, Off| OUT4[Basal transcription only]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Eukaryotic Regulation Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stimulus Signal] --> B[Signal Transduction]
    B --> C[Transcription Factor Activation nuclear localisation]
    C --> D[TF binds Enhancer or Silencer 1 to 50 kb away]
    D --> E[Mediator complex bridges Enhancer to Promoter]
    E --> F[TFIID TBP TAFs recruit Pol II and GTFs]
    F --> G[CTD Ser-5 phosphorylation by TFIIH CDK7]
    G --> H[Initiation to Elongation transition]
    I[Chromatin State: HATs acetylate H3K27 H3K9] -->|Open| F
    J[HDACs deacetylate] -->|Closed| F
    K[DNA Methylation CpG silenced] -->|Blocks TF| F
    L[lncRNA eRNA] -->|Guide complexes| F
```
