## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between the *closed complex* and the *open complex* during prokaryotic transcription initiation. Mention the role of σ region 2.3.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Closed Complex (RPc) | Open Complex (RPo) |
|---|---|---|
| DNA state | Double-stranded, intact base pairs | Locally melted ("bubble" of ~12–14 bp, from −11 to +3) |
| σ contact | σ region 2.4 on −10; σ region 4.2 on −35 | σ region 2.3 inserts aromatic wedge into minor groove |
| Energy | Pre-isomerisation | σ supplies energy to overcome ΔG of strand separation |
| Read-out | Sequence-specific | Structural (strand separation) |

**Role of σ region 2.3:** Contains aromatic residues (Phe, Tyr) that act as a "wedge" to **melt the −10 box**, creating the transcription bubble and stabilising the single-stranded template. **(1 mark for each row + 1 mark for σ role)**

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the six General Transcription Factors (GTFs) required for RNA Pol II pre-initiation complex formation in eukaryotes. State one specific function of any two.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six GTFs are: **TFIID, TFIIA, TFIIB, TFIIE, TFIIF, TFIIH** (0.5 mark each for listing).

Functions:
- **TFIID:** Contains TBP, which binds the TATA box and bends DNA ~80°. TAFs recognise Inr and DPE.
- **TFIIH:** Contains helicase subunits (XPB, XPD) that unwind promoter DNA and a kinase subunit (CDK7) that phosphorylates the Pol II CTD at Ser-5.
(Any two correct functions = 2 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** confuse **TATA-binding protein (TBP)** with the entire TFIID complex — TFIID = TBP + **TAFs**, only TBP binds the TATA box. *(Lose 1 mark if missed.)*
> - In Part B answers, **always draw/describe a labelled diagram** of the PIC — this is the #1 marking differentiator.
> - When asked "regulation of *lac* operon", you MUST mention **both** negative (repressor) **and** positive (CAP-cAMP) control. Mentioning only one = −3 marks.
> - Spell the σ factor as **"sigma"** in main text; do not write "∑" or other symbols.
> - Always specify the **phosphorylated residue** when discussing Pol II CTD (e.g., **Ser-5** for initiation, **Ser-2** for elongation).
> - Avoid writing "RNA Pol II is phosphorylated" — always specify which residue and which kinase.

---

#### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **step-wise assembly of the prokaryotic transcription initiation complex** at the *lac* operon promoter. Discuss the role of the **σ⁷⁰ factor** and the conserved **−35 and −10 promoter elements**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **molecular mechanism of catabolite repression** in the *lac* operon, with a focus on the **CAP-cAMP** regulatory module. How does this ensure **glucose优先** utilisation? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.A

**(a) [7 marks]**

The prokaryotic transcription initiation complex (TIC) is the assembly of the RNA polymerase holoenzyme (Eσ⁷⁰) at the lac promoter, leading to the formation of the open complex and transcription initiation.

**Step 1 — Holoenzyme formation:** Core RNAP (α₂ββ'ω) binds σ⁷⁰ → Eσ⁷⁰. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2 — Closed complex (RPc):** Eσ⁷⁰ contacts the **−35 box (5'-TTGACA-3')** via σ region **4.2** and the **−10 box (5'-TATAAT-3')** via σ region **2.4**. DNA is still double-stranded. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Isomerisation to open complex (RPo):** σ region **2.3** (aromatic wedge) inserts into the minor groove at −10, melting the DNA from **−11 to +3**, creating a transcription bubble. This is the rate-limiting step. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Abortive initiation:** RNAP synthesises and releases 2–9 nt RNA without leaving the promoter. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 5 — Promoter clearance:** Once RNA grows to ~10 nt, σ region 3.2 ("σ finger") is displaced from the RNA exit channel; σ factor is released. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Elongation complex (RPe):** σ-free RNAP, assisted by NusA and NusG, transcribes processively. **(0.5 mark)**

**Roles of −35 and −10 elements:** −35 = initial recognition; −10 = strand melting. The α-CTD also contacts the **UP element (−40 to −60)**, enhancing transcription 10–30×. **(1 mark for promoter role + diagram)**

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Catabolite repression** is the preferential use of **glucose** over lactose, mediated by cAMP and the **CAP (Catabolite Activator Protein)**.

**Mechanism:**
1. **Adenylate cyclase (CyaA)** converts ATP → cAMP. Glucose entry into the cell **inhibits CyaA** and **activates cAMP phosphodiesterase**, lowering cAMP. **(1 mark)**
2. When **[glucose] is low**, **[cAMP] rises**. cAMP binds **CAP** (a dimeric transcription activator encoded by *crp* gene). **(1 mark)**
3. **cAMP-CAP complex** binds a specific site **~62 bp upstream** of the lac promoter, **upstream of the −35 box**, bending the DNA ~90°. **(1 mark)**
4. cAMP-CAP makes direct protein–protein contacts with the **α-CTD of RNAP**, recruiting it to the promoter. This **synergises** with repressor removal to give **strong transcription** (up to ~1000× basal). **(1.5 marks)**
5. **If glucose is high**, cAMP is low, CAP cannot bind, and only basal transcription occurs — even if lactose is present. **(1 mark)**

**Glucose priority:** Evolutionarily, glucose is the preferred carbon source. By coupling lac transcription to intracellular cAMP (which inversely reflects [glucose]), the cell **avoids wastefully** producing β-galactosidase when glucose is already available. **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare the **core promoter architecture** of prokaryotes and eukaryotes. Describe the **assembly of the RNA Polymerase II pre-initiation complex (PIC)** in eukaryotes, naming the GTFs in order of recruitment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **CTD code hypothesis** of RNA Pol II. How do specific phosphorylations of the **YSPTSPS** heptad orchestrate the transition from **initiation to elongation and termination**? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.B

**(a) [7 marks]**

| Feature | Prokaryote | Eukaryote (Pol II) |
|---|---|---|
| Promoter elements | −35, −10 (Pribnow), UP | TATA, BRE, Inr, MTE, DPE |
| Recognition protein | σ⁷⁰ | TBP (part of TFIID) + TAFs |
| RNAP subunits | 5 (α₂ββ'ω) + σ | 12 (RPB1–RPB12) |
| Operon | Polycistronic (lac Z,Y,A) | Monocistronic mRNA |
| Activator site | Within promoter / operator | Enhancer 1–50 kb away (or downstream) |
| Chromatin | None | Nucleosomes regulate access |

**Eukaryotic PIC assembly (order of recruitment):** **(1 mark for full order)**

1. **TFIID (TBP + TAFs)** binds the TATA box, bending DNA ~80°. **(0.5)**
2. **TFIIA** stabilises TBP and prevents repression by negative cofactors (e.g., Dr1, Mot1). **(0.5)**
3. **TFIIB** binds downstream; its N-terminal "B-finger" reaches the active centre of Pol II and selects the **TSS**. **(1)**
4. **TFIIF** (RAP30/74) recruits Pol II and prevents non-specific DNA binding. **(0.5)**
5. **TFIIE** binds Pol II and recruits **TFIIH**. **(0.5)**
6. **TFIIH** (XPB/XPD helicases + CDK7 kinase) melts DNA (~−9 to +2) and phosphorylates **Ser-5** of the Pol II CTD. **(1.5)**
7. Mediator + activators bridge enhancer-bound TFs to Pol II. **(1)**

**(b) [7 marks]**

The **CTD of RPB1** has 52 heptad repeats of **Y¹S²P³T⁴S⁵P⁶S⁷** in mammals. The "CTD code" hypothesis states that **dynamic, site-specific phosphorylation** creates a **molecular barcode** that recruits stage-specific RNA processing factors.

| Phosphorylation | Stage | Kinase | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ser-5-P** | Initiation (promoter escape) | **CDK7** (TFIIH) | Recruits **capping enzyme (RNGTT + RNMT)** → adds 7-methylguanosine cap to 5' end of nascent RNA. **(1.5 marks)** |
| **Ser-7-P** | Initiation (snRNA genes) | CDK7 | Required for snRNA 3' end processing by Integrator. **(0.5 mark)** |
| **Ser-2-P** | Elongation | **CDK9** (P-TEFb) early; **CDK12/13** late | Recruits **Spt5, PAF1, FACT** for elongation; later, **cleavage/polyadenylation factors (CPSF, CstF)**. **(2 marks)** |
| **Tyr-1-P** | Termination / early elongation | CDK | Antagonises Ser-2-P; involved in termination. **(0.5 mark)** |
| **Thr-4-P** | Histone mRNA 3' end | CDK | Recruits **SLBP** for histone pre-mRNA 3' cleavage (no poly-A). **(0.5 mark)** |
| **Ser-2-P (sustained) + Tyr-1-P loss** | Termination | Ssu72, Rtr1 | Dephosphorylation resets CTD for re-initiation. **(1 mark)** |

**Mechanistic logic:** As Pol II moves from promoter → gene body → 3' end, **Ser-5-P is gradually removed** by **Ssu72 / Rtr1 phosphatases**, while **Ser-2-P accumulates** via **CDK12/13**. This creates a "**phosphorylation gradient**" along the gene that acts as a molecular timeline: capping → elongation → polyadenylation → termination → recycling. **(1 mark for overarching logic)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Transcription Initiation):**

- **Prokaryotic RNAP core:** α₂ββ'ω (**5 subunits, ~400 kDa**). Sigma factor is a **separate, dissociable** protein; the holoenzyme is **E + σ**.
- **σ⁷⁰ domains:** Region 2.4 → −10 recognition; **2.3** → −10 melting (aromatic wedge); **4.2** → −35 recognition.
- **Consensus sequences:** −35 = **TTGACA**; −10 = **TATAAT**; +1 = TSS.
- **Five initiation stages:** Closed complex (RPc) → Open complex (RPo) → Abortive initiation → Promoter clearance → Elongation (RPe).
- **Bacterial transcription bubble:** ~12–14 nt melted; RNA-DNA hybrid ~8–9 bp.
- **Eukaryotic Pols:** Pol I (rRNA) → insensitive to α-amanitin; **Pol II (mRNA) → HIGHLY sensitive**; Pol III (tRNA, 5S rRNA) → moderately sensitive.
- **Eukaryotic GTFs (Pol II):** TFIID → TFIIA → TFIIB → Pol II + TFIIF → TFIIE → TFIIH (order matters).
- **TFIIH dual function:** Helicase (XPB, XPD) + Kinase (CDK7/CycH/MAT1).
- **Core promoter elements:** **TATA box** (TATAAA, −30 to −25); **Inr** (around +1); **DPE** (+28 to +32); **BRE** (−38 to −23).
- **CTD repeat:** **YSPTSPS**, 52× in mammals; Ser-5-P (initiation/capping) → Ser-2-P (elongation/poly-A) → Tyr-1-P (termination).
- **TBP** bends DNA ~80° at the TATA box; **TFIID** = TBP + TAFs.
- **Lac operon:** **Negative control** (LacI repressor + allolactose inducer) + **Positive control** (CAP-cAMP activated by low glucose). **Glucose ABSENT + Lactose PRESENT = maximum transcription.**
- **Trp operon:** **Repressible** — Trp repressor + Trp (co-repressor) → shutdown when Trp is abundant. **Attenuation** is an additional fine-tuning via the leader peptide (TrpL).
- **Eukaryotic regulation layers:** (1) Chromatin (HATs/HDACs, SWI/SNF), (2) TFs (activators/repressors), (3) Mediator, (4) Epigenetics (CpG methylation), (5) CTD phosphorylation code.
- **Enhancers** can act **upstream, downstream, or within introns**, and are **orientation-independent**; silencers do the opposite.
- **Mediator complex** (>20 subunits, ~1.2 MDa) bridges activators to Pol II.
- **Antibiotic connections (high-yield):** Rifampicin → binds β subunit, blocks initiation; **α-amanitin** → binds Pol II RPB1, blocks elongation (used in taxonomy).
- **Engineering takeaway:** **T7 promoter** is ~10× stronger than *E. coli* σ⁷⁰ promoters; **T7 RNAP** (single subunit, ~100 kDa) is used in **pET vectors** for high-yield recombinant protein production.

**End of Module 2 — Transcription Initiation Complex Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Post-Transcriptional Processing of Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic Transcripts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Post-Transcriptional Processing of Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic Transcripts # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Post-Transcriptional Processing (PTM)** refers to the series of enzymatic, covalent, and structural modifications that a primary RNA transcript (pre-mRNA / pre-rRNA / pre-tRNA) undergoes **after its synthesis by RNA polymerase** but **before it becomes a functionally mature RNA molecule** capable of directing translation, ribosome biogenesis, or amino-acid transport. In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the term is often expanded as **"RNA Processing / Post-Transcriptional Maturation"** and is treated as the molecular bridge between **Transcription (Module 1)** and **Translation (Module 3)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim-style):** > *"Post-transcriptional modification encompasses all sequence-specific and structural alterations — including 5′-end capping, 3′-end polyadenylation, intron splicing, RNA editing, and base modifications — that convert an immature primary transcript into a mature, translationally competent RNA."* ### Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic — The Central Contrast | Feature | Prokaryotes (E. coli paradigm) | Eukaryotes (Human / Yeast paradigm) | |---|---|---| | Cellular compartment | Cytoplasm (no nucleus) | Nucleus (processing) → Cytoplasm (translation) | | Transcript–translation coupling | **Yes** (co-transcriptional translation) | **No** (strictly separated) | | Introns in mRNA | **Absent** (rare self-splicing exceptions) | **Abundant** (∼95% of human genes contain introns) | | 5′ Cap (m⁷G) | Absent | Present on every mRNA | | 3′ Poly-A tail | Short, transient (degradation signal) | Long (∼200 A residues), stability signal | | Splicing | None (operonic polycistronic mRNAs) | Spliceosome-mediated, ATP-dependent | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Newspaper Printing Press" Imagine **DNA** is the **master editor's master file** stored in a vault. **Transcription** is like sending the raw draft (with advertisements, blank pages, scribbled corrections, and editor's notes) to the **printing press**. The newspaper **cannot be sold to the public (ribosome) in raw draft form**. **Post-transcriptional processing is the print-shop's finishing department:** - **5′ Capping** = Putting the **front-page masthead with the date** (so everyone knows where to start reading). - **3′ Polyadenylation** = Tucking the **back-page index and barcode** (so the paper isn't lost and can be catalogued). - **Splicing** = **Cutting out the advertisements and editor's margin notes** (introns) and pasting only the news articles (exons) into the final paper. - **RNA Editing** = **Correcting any spelling errors** that slipped past the editor (C → U editing in apoB mRNA, A → I editing in glutamate receptor). - **Base Modifications (rRNA / tRNA)** = Treating the ink and paper with special coatings so they survive long enough to be useful. In **prokaryotes**, the printing press (ribosome) sits **right next to the typesetting machine** — by the time one paragraph is typed, it is already being read. There is essentially **no finishing department**, so the raw draft goes straight to press. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** > **"Prokaryotes = Street Food (eat while cooking); Eukaryotes = Fine Dining (cook, garnish, plate, then serve)."** ## 1.3 Why Post-Transcriptional Processing Matters (Engineering / Biomedical Utility) - **Genetic Disease Diagnostics**: ∼15% of human genetic disorders (e.g., **β-thalassemia**, **spinal muscular atrophy**) are caused by **splice-site mutations**, not coding-sequence mutations. - **Therapeutic Splicing Modulation**: **Antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs)** like *nusinersen* (Spinraza) and *eteplirsen* (Exondys 51) correct aberrant splicing — direct clinical exploitation of PTM biology. - **mRNA Vaccine Technology**: **Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna COVID-19 vaccines** rely on synthetic mRNAs containing a **5′ cap (Cap-1)**, optimized **5′-UTR, 3′-UTR**, and a **∼100-nt poly(A) tail** to maximize translation efficiency in human cells. - **Synthetic Biology / Gene Design**: Codon-optimized, intron-containing synthetic genes (e.g., for monoclonal antibody production in CHO cells) exploit splicing to boost heterologous protein yields by 10–100×. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Five Canonical Eukaryotic mRNA Processing Events Below is the operational logic of each modification, broken down as the **Why → How → Enzyme → Output** sequence expected in KTU valuation keys. ### 2.1.1 5′-End Capping (Co-transcriptional) - **WHY**: A naked 5′-triphosphate is recognized by innate immune sensors (RIG-I, IFIT proteins) as "viral." The cap (i) masks this triphosphate, (ii) promotes nuclear export, (iii) is recognized by eIF4E for translation initiation. - **HOW**: Three sequential enzymatic steps occur on the **first transcribed nucleotide** (usually a purine): 1. **RNA triphosphatase (RTase)** removes the γ-phosphate → 5′-diphosphate. 2. **Guanylyltransferase (GTase)** transfers GMP from GTP → 5′–5′ triphosphate bridge (GpppN). 3. **Methyltransferase (MTase)** methylates the N⁷ position of guanine → **m⁷GpppN (Cap-0)**. Subsequent 2′-O-methylation on the first ribose yields **Cap-1**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The cap structure is therefore: **m⁷G(5′)ppp(5′)N(m)** — a **5′-to-5′ triphosphate linkage** (NOT the standard 3′–5′ phosphodiester). ### 2.1.2 3′-End Polyadenylation - **WHY**: (i) Protects mRNA from 3′-exonuclease degradation, (ii) enhances translation via PABP–eIF4G interaction, (iii) required for nuclear export. - **HOW**: Two-step cleavage + addition: 1. **Endonucleolytic cleavage** ∼10–30 nt downstream of the **AAUAAA hexamer** (polyadenylation signal, PAS) and a GU-rich downstream sequence element (DSE). Carried out by **CPSF** (cleavage/polyadenylation specificity factor) + **CstF**. 2. **Poly(A) polymerase (PAP)** adds **∼200 adenine residues** (in mammals) in a template-independent manner. **PABP II** binds the growing tail and stimulates PAP processivity. > [!NOTE] > **Sizing rule:** Nuclear poly(A) tail is ∼200 nt in mammals; ∼70 nt in *S. cerevisiae*; ∼30 nt in plants. Cytoplasmic poly(A) tail shrinks with each translation round (deadenylation = first step of mRNA decay). ### 2.1.3 RNA Splicing (Spliceosome-Mediated) - **WHY**: Removes non-coding **introns** from pre-mRNA to produce a continuous coding sequence (CDS). - **HOW**: A two-step **transesterification** reaction carried out by the **spliceosome** (a ∼12.5 MDa ribonucleoprotein complex of 5 snRNPs: U1, U2, U4, U5, U6 + numerous non-snRNP factors). - **Recognition signals**: - **5′ Splice site (5′SS)**: GU at intron start (in GU-AG introns; ∼99.5% of human introns). - **Branch point (BP)**: Adenine, typically **CURAY** (Y = pyrimidine), located 18–50 nt upstream of 3′SS. - **3′ Splice site (3′SS)**: AG at intron end, preceded by a polypyrimidine tract (PPT). ### 2.1.4 The Two Transesterification Steps 1. **Step 1**: 2′-OH of the **branch-point adenine** attacks the **5′ splice-site phosphodiester bond** → lariat intermediate (5′ exon free, intron-3′ exon as a lariat with 2′–5′ bond). 2. **Step 2**: 3′-OH of the freed **5′ exon** attacks the **3′ splice-site phosphodiester bond** → ligated exons released + intron lariat → debranched by **DBR1** (debranching enzyme). ### 2.1.5 Alternative Splicing — Expanding the Proteome A single human gene can produce **multiple mRNA isoforms** via: - **Exon skipping** (most common in metazoans) - **Mutually exclusive exons** - **Alternative 5′ splice sites / 3′ splice sites** - **Intron retention** (common in plants and fungi) - **Alternative promoters / polyadenylation** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU high-yield fact:** Human genome has ∼20,000 protein-coding genes but produces **>100,000 distinct proteins** — alternative splicing is the principal mechanism generating this expansion. ### 2.1.6 RNA Editing - **C-to-U editing**: Catalyzed by **APOBEC-1** (apolipoprotein B editing catalytic subunit 1) on apoB mRNA — creates a stop codon (CAA → UAA) producing **apoB48** in intestine instead of **apoB100** in liver. - **A-to-I editing**: Catalyzed by **ADAR** (adenosine deaminase acting on RNA) on dsRNA — prevalent in neuronal transcripts (e.g., GluR-B Q/R site). I (inosine) is read as G by ribosomes. ## 2.2 Prokaryotic mRNA Processing — The Minimalist Model In prokaryotes (e.g., *E. coli*): - **No 5′ cap**, **no introns in standard mRNA**, **no spliceosome**. - **Polyadenylation exists but is short (∼10–40 nt)** and serves as a **degradation signal**, not a stability signal — added by **PAP I** (poly(A) polymerase I, encoded by *pcnB* gene) and **PNPase** (polynucleotide phosphorylase). - **mRNA half-life is short (∼2–5 min)**, allowing rapid response to environmental change. - **Polycistronic operons** (lac, trp, ara) are transcribed as a single mRNA and translated by multiple ribosomes simultaneously → no need for splicing. - **tRNA and rRNA** DO undergo processing in prokaryotes: - **tRNA**: 5′ cleavage by **RNase P** (ribozyme!), 3′ cleavage by **RNase D / Z / T**, CCA addition by **tRNA nucleotidyltransferase**, base modifications. - **rRNA**: 30S primary transcript → mature 16S, 23S, 5S rRNAs via **RNase III**, **RNase E**, **RNase G**, etc. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value / division bars inside table cells. Do NOT use `|`.** | S.No. | Concept | Mathematical / Structural Expression | Typical Magnitude | Biological Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cap structure (Cap-0) | $m^{7}G(5')ppp(5')N$ | 1 per mRNA | Translation initiation, immune evasion | | 2 | Cap-1 structure | $m^{7}GpppN^{m}$ | 1 per mRNA | Higher eukaryotes / mammals | | 3 | Poly(A) tail length (mammals) | $\text{poly}(A) \approx 200\,\text{nt}$ | 150–250 nt | mRNA stability, nuclear export | | 4 | Poly(A) tail length (*S. cerevisiae*) | $\text{poly}(A) \approx 70\,\text{nt}$ | 60–80 nt | Yeast-specific | | 5 | Poly(A) tail length (prokaryotic) | $\text{poly}(A) \approx 10\text{–}40\,\text{nt}$ | Variable | Degradation signal | | 6 | Half-life (typical eukaryotic mRNA) | $t_{1/2} \approx 10\,\text{h}$ | 0.5 – 24 h | Long enough for protein synthesis | | 7 | Half-life (typical *E. coli* mRNA) | $t_{1/2} \approx 2\text{–}5\,\text{min}$ | Seconds – minutes | Rapid gene regulation | | 8 | Spliceosome mass | $\approx 12.5\,\text{MDa}$ | — | Largest known RNP after ribosome | | 9 | snRNP subunits in spliceosome | U1, U2, U4, U5, U6 | 5 | Catalytic core | | 10 | Human genes → proteins ratio | $\sim 20{,}000 \text{ genes} \rightarrow >100{,}000 \text{ proteins}$ | — | Alternative splicing impact | | 11 | Average human intron size | $\sim 3{,}000\,\text{nt}$ | 60 nt – 100 kb | Huge variation | | 12 | Average human exon size | $\sim 150\,\text{nt}$ | 50 – 300 nt | Constrained by exon definition | | 13 | Branch-point consensus (mammals) | $CURAY$ | 18–50 nt upstream of 3′SS | Splicing fidelity | | 14 | Polyadenylation signal (PAS) | $AAUAAA$ | — | Cleavage site positioning | | 15 | Downstream sequence element (DSE) | $GU/U$-rich | — | CstF binding | | 16 | Coding sequence continuity | $\text{Length}_{\text{exons}} \approx 0.05 \times \text{Length}_{\text{gene}}$ | — | Why splicing dramatically reduces mRNA size | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Biopharmaceutical manufacturing**: Therapeutic monoclonal antibodies (mAbs, e.g., trastuzumab, adalimumab) are produced in **CHO cells** using vectors engineered with optimal introns (e.g., CMV intron A) and strong polyadenylation signals (bGH polyA, SV40 late polyA) to maximize yield. - **mRNA therapeutics**: All approved mRNA vaccines incorporate a **Cap-1 structure**, **100-nt poly(A)** (not 200), and codon-optimized ORFs lacking problematic secondary structure. - **CRISPR guide RNA design**: Synthetic gRNAs require specific 5′ and 3′ chemical modifications (e.g., 2′-O-methyl, phosphorothioate) to mimic the protective effects of natural cap and tail. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Estimation of Splicing's Effect on Transcript Length A **central KTU-style calculation** in molecular biology is determining how much the pre-mRNA shrinks after splicing, and what fraction of the gene is coding. **Problem-type:** Given a human gene with the following features, calculate (a) the average number of exons, (b) the mature mRNA length, (c) the coding fraction. **Given:** - Gene length (genomic span) $L_{g} = 24{,}000\,\text{nt}$ - Number of introns = 8 - Average exon length = 150 nt - 5′ UTR ≈ 100 nt, 3′ UTR ≈ 800 nt, Poly(A) tail ≈ 200 nt - Start codon to stop codon (CDS) = 1,300 nt **Find:** - (i) Total exon length $L_{e,\text{total}}$ - (ii) Total intron length $L_{i,\text{total}}$ - (iii) Pre-mRNA length $L_{\text{pre}}$ - (iv) Mature mRNA length $L_{\text{mature}}$ - (v) Coding fraction $f_{\text{CDS}} = L_{\text{CDS}} \div L_{g}$ --- ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Determine the number of exons.** The number of exons always exceeds the number of introns by **one** in a linear gene: $$n_{\text{exons}} = n_{\text{introns}} + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9$$ *Conversion logic:* Each intron physically separates two exons, so for *k* introns there must be *k + 1* exons in a linear transcript. --- **Step 2 — Compute total exon length.** $$L_{e,\text{total}} = n_{\text{exons}} \times \overline{L}_{\text{exon}}$$ $$L_{e,\text{total}} = 9 \times 150\,\text{nt} = 1{,}350\,\text{nt}$$ *Conversion logic:* Multiplying the count of exons by their mean length yields aggregate exonic span. --- **Step 3 — Compute total intron length using the genomic equation.** The genomic span equals exons + introns: $$L_{g} = L_{e,\text{total}} + L_{i,\text{total}}$$ Rearranging: $$L_{i,\text{total}} = L_{g} - L_{e,\text{total}}$$ $$L_{i,\text{total}} = 24{,}000\,\text{nt} - 1{,}350\,\text{nt} = 22{,}650\,\text{nt}$$ *Conversion logic:* Subtracting exonic nucleotides from total genomic length isolates the intronic portion. This number also serves as a cross-check via the average intron size (≈ 22,650 / 8 ≈ 2,831 nt, biologically plausible). --- **Step 4 — Compute the pre-mRNA length (primary transcript).** The primary transcript contains BOTH exons and introns plus the polyadenylation signal-derived 3′ end and the cap nucleotide: $$L_{\text{pre}} = L_{e,\text{total}} + L_{i,\text{total}}$$ $$L_{\text{pre}} = 1{,}350\,\text{nt} + 22{,}650\,\text{nt} = 24{,}000\,\text{nt}$$ In this simplified model, $L_{\text{pre}} \approx L_{g}$, which is the standard assumption (the primary transcript mirrors the transcribed genomic span). --- **Step 5 — Compute the mature mRNA length (after splicing + polyadenylation).** After splicing, all introns are removed; the poly(A) tail is added post-cleavage and is NOT encoded in the gene: $$L_{\text{mature}} = L_{e,\text{total}} + L_{\text{poly}(A)}$$ $$L_{\text{mature}} = 1{,}350\,\text{nt} + 200\,\text{nt} = 1{,}550\,\text{nt}$$ *Conversion logic:* Splicing deletes intronic nt (22,650 nt), and poly(A) polymerase adds 200 adenylates. The 5′ cap is a single modified guanosine (does not extend length in nt count). --- **Step 6 — Compute the coding fraction (CDS as a fraction of gene).** $$f_{\text{CDS}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{CDS}}}{L_{g}}$$ $$f_{\text{CDS}} = \dfrac{1{,}300\,\text{nt}}{24{,}000\,\text{nt}} \approx 0.0542 \approx 5.4\%$$ *Conversion logic:* This demonstrates the famous "**genes are mostly introns**" observation — only about **5% of the gene's DNA encodes the actual amino acid sequence**. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Processing Footprint > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` and `\mid` for bars (do not use ASCII pipe in tables).** | Step / Feature | Equation Form | Numerical Plug-in | Result | Biological Interpretation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Exon count | $n_{\text{exons}} = n_{\text{introns}} + 1$ | $8 + 1$ | $9$ | Linear gene architecture | | Total exonic span | $L_{e,\text{total}} = n_{\text{exons}} \times \overline{L}_{\text{exon}}$ | $9 \times 150$ | $1{,}350\,\text{nt}$ | Survives splicing | | Total intronic span | $L_{i,\text{total}} = L_{g} - L_{e,\text{total}}$ | $24{,}000 - 1{,}350$ | $22{,}650\,\text{nt}$ | Discarded as lariat | | Primary transcript | $L_{\text{pre}} \approx L_{g}$ | $24{,}000$ | $24{,}000\,\text{nt}$ | Pre-splicing | | Mature mRNA | $L_{\text{mature}} = L_{e,\text{total}} + L_{\text{poly}(A)}$ | $1{,}350 + 200$ | $1{,}550\,\text{nt}$ | Translation-ready | | Coding fraction | $f_{\text{CDS}} = L_{\text{CDS}} / L_{g}$ | $1{,}300 / 24{,}000$ | $\approx 5.4\%$ | Introns dominate | --- ### 3.3 Bonus Derivation — mRNA Stability Half-Life vs. Poly(A) Tail Length For many eukaryotic mRNAs, the cytoplasmic poly(A) tail is progressively shortened by **deadenylases** (CCR4–NOT, PAN2–PAN3) before the body is degraded. A simplified **first-order decay model** is: $$N(t) = N_{0} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$ where $\lambda$ is the decay constant and $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda$. **Worked example:** - Initial poly(A) tail length $L_{0} = 200\,\text{nt}$ - Deadenylation rate $r_{d} = 10\,\text{nt / min}$ - mRNA body decay rate constant $\lambda_{b} = 0.001\,\text{min}^{-1}$ **Time for the tail to be "non-functional" (degraded below $\approx$ 25 nt — the PABP dissociation threshold):** $$t_{\text{decap}} = \dfrac{L_{0} - L_{\text{threshold}}}{r_{d}}$$ $$t_{\text{decap}} = \dfrac{200 - 25}{10} = 17.5\,\text{min}$$ *Conversion logic:* Once the tail falls below the threshold, decapping enzyme (DCP1/DCP2) removes the cap and the 5′ → 3′ exonuclease (XRN1) rapidly destroys the body. **Overall mRNA half-life:** $$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{b}} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{0.001} \approx 693\,\text{min} \approx 11.6\,\text{h}$$ *Conversion logic:* This matches the typical mammalian mRNA half-life range stated in the formula sheet. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Eukaryotic mRNA Maturation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB geneA[GENOMIC DNA TEMPLATE] preA[PRE-MRNA PRIMARY TRANSCRIPT

[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **"Define post-transcriptional modification. List the major modifications that occur on a eukaryotic pre-mRNA."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Post-transcriptional modification is the set of enzymatic and covalent alterations that convert a primary RNA transcript, synthesized by RNA polymerase, into a mature, functionally active RNA molecule capable of participating in translation or ribosome assembly. **Major modifications of eukaryotic pre-mRNA (2 marks):** 1. **5′-End capping** — addition of m⁷G via a 5′–5′ triphosphate linkage; protects mRNA from 5′-exonuclease attack and aids translation initiation. 2. **3′-End polyadenylation** — cleavage at the AAUAAA signal followed by addition of ∼200 adenine residues; enhances mRNA stability and nuclear export. 3. **Splicing** — removal of introns and ligation of exons by the spliceosome (a snRNP complex). 4. **RNA editing** — site-specific base changes (e.g., C→U via APOBEC-1, A→I via ADARs) that alter coding potential. 5. **Base / ribose methylation** — minor modifications on rRNA and snRNA that fine-tune folding and function. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Repeated Topic]** **"Compare the post-transcriptional processing of mRNA in prokaryotes and eukaryotes."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Prokaryotes | Eukaryotes | |---|---|---| | 5′ Cap (m⁷G) | Absent | Present; protects mRNA and aids translation | | 3′ Poly-A tail | Short (10–40 nt); signals degradation | Long (∼200 nt); signals stability | | Introns / Splicing | Absent (rare self-splicing introns) | Abundant; spliceosome removes them | | Transcript–translation coupling | Coupled (cotranscriptional) | Separated (nuclear → cytoplasmic) | | mRNA half-life | ∼2–5 min | Several hours | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions provide an internal choice. Students must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (each carrying 14 marks, with sub-parts worth 7 + 7). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Describe in detail the process of **5′-end capping** of eukaryotic mRNA. Add a note on the enzymes involved and the biological significance of the cap structure. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the mechanism of **RNA splicing** by the spliceosome. Illustrate the two transesterification steps and the role of snRNPs. *(7 marks)* --- ### **Model Answer for Question A:** #### Part (a) — 5′-End Capping (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark) — Significance:** The 5′ cap is a **7-methylguanosine (m⁷G)** attached to the first transcribed nucleotide of the mRNA via an unusual **5′-to-5′ triphosphate bridge**. It is essential for (i) mRNA stability (protects from 5′ → 3′ exonucleases), (ii) nuclear export, (iii) translation initiation (recognized by cap-binding protein **eIF4E**), and (iv) evasion of innate immune sensors (e.g., IFIT proteins). **Step 2 (3 marks) — Three enzymatic steps:** The capping reaction occurs **co-transcriptionally** when the nascent transcript is only ∼20–30 nt long. 1. **RNA triphosphatase (RTase)** hydrolyses the γ-phosphate from the 5′-triphosphate end of the pre-mRNA, leaving a 5′-diphosphate: $$\text{pppN} \xrightarrow{\text{RTase}} \text{ppN} + P_{i}$$ 2. **Guanylyltransferase (GTase)** transfers the GMP moiety from **GTP** to the 5′-diphosphate via a covalent enzyme–GMP intermediate (lysyl–GMP), forming the cap core: $$\text{ppN} + \text{GTP} \xrightarrow{\text{GTase}} \text{GpppN} + PP_{i}$$ 3. **Methyltransferase (MTase)** transfers a methyl group from **S-adenosylmethionine (SAM)** to the N⁷ position of guanine: $$\text{GpppN} + \text{SAM} \xrightarrow{\text{MTase}} m^{7}\text{GpppN} + \text{SAH}$$ The product **m⁷GpppN** is the **Cap-0** structure. In higher eukaryotes, a second methylation on the 2′-O of the first ribose (mediated by **MTr1 / CMTr1**) yields **Cap-1** (m⁷GpppNᵐ), which is the dominant cap in mammals. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Enzyme summary:** - **In yeast:** **Cet1** (RTase), **Ceg1** (GTase), **Abd1** (MTase) — all recruited to the **CTD of RNA Pol II** via the **capping enzyme complex (CEC)** when Ser5-P (phosphorylated serine 5 of the heptad repeat) is present. - **In mammals:** **RNGTT** (RTase/GTase bifunctional), **RNMT** (MTase) + **RAM** (RNMT-activating mini-protein). **Step 4 (1 mark) — Cap-binding proteins in translation:** - **eIF4E** binds the cap; **eIF4G** scaffolds; **eIF4A** is the helicase. Together they recruit the 43S pre-initiation complex. The cap is thus the **physical and functional gateway to translation**. --- #### Part (b) — RNA Splicing (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Splicing signals:** The pre-mRNA contains short **consensus sequences** that define intron–exon boundaries: - **5′ Splice site (5′SS)**: GU at the 5′ end of the intron, within the consensus $MAG \mid GURAGU$ (M = A/C; R = purine). - **Branch point (BP)**: A conserved adenine, typically within $CURAY$ (Y = pyrimidine), 18–50 nt upstream of the 3′SS. - **Polypyrimidine tract (PPT)**: A run of 10–20 pyrimidines immediately upstream of the 3′SS. - **3′ Splice site (3′SS)**: AG at the intron–exon boundary, consensus $YAG \mid$. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Spliceosome composition and assembly:** The spliceosome is a **∼12.5 MDa** dynamic RNP complex composed of **5 snRNPs** (U1, U2, U4/U6, U5) and >100 auxiliary proteins. Assembly proceeds through defined complexes: | Complex | Composition | Recognition step | |---|---|---| | **E (Early)** | U1 snRNP binds 5′SS | 5′SS recognition | | **A** | U2 snRNP binds branch point | BP recognition (requires ATP + SF1, SF3a/b) | | **B** | U4/U6·U5 tri-snRNP joins | Full spliceosome assembly | | **B → C** | U1 and U4 released; U6 pairs with U2 | Catalytic activation | | **C** | Active spliceosome | Two transesterifications | **Step 3 (2 marks) — Two transesterification reactions:** **Reaction 1:** The **2′-OH of the branch-point adenosine** performs a nucleophilic attack on the **phosphorus atom at the 5′ splice site**, cleaving the 5′ exon–intron bond and forming a **2′-5′ phosphodiester bond** within the intron (creating the **lariat intermediate**). The 5′ exon is released with a free 3′-OH. **Reaction 2:** The free **3′-OH of the 5′ exon** attacks the **phosphorus atom at the 3′ splice site**, ligating the two exons and releasing the **intron as a lariat**. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Disassembly and intron fate:** The lariat is debranched by **DBR1 (debranching enzyme)** into a linear intron, which is then degraded by exonucleases (XRN1, the exosome). The spliceosome disassembles, snRNPs are recycled. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the process of **3′-end polyadenylation** of eukaryotic mRNA. Describe the cis-acting signals and the proteins that recognize them. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Differentiate between **prokaryotic and eukaryotic mRNA processing**. Add a note on the significance of alternative splicing in expanding proteome diversity. *(7 marks)* --- ### **Model Answer for Question B:** #### Part (a) — 3′-End Polyadenylation (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Cis-acting signals:** Three sequence elements direct cleavage and polyadenylation in mammals: - **AAUAAA hexamer (PAS)**: Located 10–30 nt upstream of the cleavage site. The most conserved element; point mutations drastically reduce polyadenylation. - **Upstream sequence element (USE)**: U-rich, enhances efficiency; not strictly required. - **Downstream sequence element (DSE)**: GU-rich or U-rich, located within 30 nt downstream of the cleavage site; binds CstF. **Step 2 (3 marks) — Trans-acting factors and the two-step mechanism:** The reaction occurs in **two distinct enzymatic steps**: **Step 2A — Endonucleolytic cleavage:** The cleavage occurs between the PAS and the DSE, generating a **5′ upstream fragment** ending in a 3′-OH (to which the poly(A) tail will be added) and a **3′ downstream fragment** that is rapidly degraded. The cleavage requires the assembly of a multiprotein complex: - **CPSF (Cleavage and Polyadenylation Specificity Factor)** — binds AAUAAA; subunits include CPSF-160, CPSF-100, CPSF-73 (the actual endonuclease — a **metallo-β-lactamase fold enzyme**), CPSF-30, Fip1. - **CstF (Cleavage Stimulation Factor)** — binds the DSE; consists of CstF-50, CstF-64, CstF-77. - **Cleavage factors**: CF I and CF II assist. - **Symplekin** — a scaffold protein. **Step 2B — Poly(A) tail addition:** - **Poly(A) polymerase (PAP, also called PAP-α)** catalyzes the template-independent addition of AMP residues from ATP onto the 3′-OH: $$\text{mRNA-}3'\text{OH} + n\,\text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{PAP}} \text{mRNA-poly}(A)_{n} + n\,PP_{i}$$ - The reaction starts slowly, but once **∼10 A residues** have been added, **PABP II (Poly(A) Binding Protein II)** binds the nascent tail and **stimulates PAP processivity**, allowing rapid elongation to ∼200 nt. - **PABP II** also determines the final tail length by acting as a "molecular ruler." **Step 3 (2 marks) — Biological significance:** 1. **mRNA stability** — PABP bound to the tail interacts with **eIF4G** at the 5′ cap, forming a **closed-loop** structure that protects both ends from exonucleases and enhances translation re-initiation. 2. **Nuclear export** — PABP II bound to poly(A) is recognized by the **TREX / NXF1** export machinery, allowing passage through the nuclear pore. 3. **Translation efficiency** — the closed-loop structure allows ribosomes to recycle efficiently on the same mRNA. **Difference between nuclear and cytoplasmic poly(A) tails:** - The **nuclear** tail is fixed at ∼200 nt in mammals. - The **cytoplasmic** tail shrinks with each translation round due to deadenylases (CCR4–NOT, PAN2–PAN3); when it drops below ∼25 nt, the cap is removed and the body is degraded 5′ → 3′ by XRN1. --- #### Part (b) — Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic Processing & Alternative Splicing (7 marks) **Step 1 (4 marks) — Comparative table:** | Aspect | Prokaryotes (e.g., *E. coli*) | Eukaryotes (e.g., *Homo sapiens*) | |---|---|---| | Cellular compartmentalization | Cytoplasmic (no nucleus) | Nuclear processing → cytoplasmic translation | | Transcript–translation coupling | Coupled (cotranscriptional) | Strictly uncoupled | | 5′ Cap (m⁷GpppN) | Absent | Present on every mRNA | | 3′ Poly(A) tail | Short (10–40 nt); marks degradation | Long (∼200 nt); confers stability | | Introns in mRNA | Essentially absent | Abundant (∼95% of human genes) | | Splicing | None | Spliceosome-mediated, ATP-dependent | | Polycistronic mRNAs | Common (operons: lac, trp, ara) | Absent | | mRNA half-life | ∼2–5 min (rapid turnover) | Hours to days | | RNA editing | Rare (e.g., *E. coli* minimal) | Widespread (C→U by APOBEC-1; A→I by ADARs) | | rRNA / tRNA processing | RNase III, RNase E, RNase P (a ribozyme) | Similar enzymes but more complex (e.g., snoRNAs guide rRNA modification) | **Step 2 (3 marks) — Alternative splicing and proteome expansion:** **Definition (1 mark):** Alternative splicing is the process by which a single pre-mRNA is spliced in **more than one way** to produce **multiple mature mRNA isoforms**, each encoding a distinct protein variant (isoform). **Mechanistic modes (1 mark):** - **Exon skipping** — most common in metazoans (e.g., *DMD* gene: skipping of exon 51 produces a partially functional dystrophin in Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients treated with eteplirsen). - **Mutually exclusive exons** — only one of a pair is included (e.g., α-tropomyosin exons 2a/2b). - **Alternative 5′SS / 3′SS** — alternative splice sites within an exon. - **Intron retention** — common in plants, fungi, lower metazoans. - **Alternative promoters / polyadenylation** — produce mRNAs differing in UTRs but sometimes also in CDS. **Proteome expansion (1 mark):** The human genome encodes only ∼20,000 protein-coding genes, yet produces **>100,000 distinct protein isoforms** and the total proteome is estimated at **>1 million** when post-translational modifications are included. Alternative splicing is the principal mechanism generating this 5- to 10-fold expansion at the RNA level. **Tissue-specific and developmental-stage-specific splicing factors** (e.g., **PTB, hnRNP A1, Nova, RBFOX, MBNL1, SRSF proteins**) bind auxiliary **exonic splicing enhancers (ESEs)** or **silencers (ESSs)** to control which exons are included in a given cell type. Dysregulation of these factors causes diseases such as **myotonic dystrophy (MBNL1 sequestration by CUG repeats)** and **spinal muscular atrophy (SMN2 exon 7 skipping)**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Avoid these!)** > > 1. **Writing "5′-to-3′ linkage" instead of "5′-to-5′ triphosphate linkage" in the cap structure.** This is an automatic **-1 mark** deduction by KTU evaluators because it directly contradicts the textbook definition of the cap. > 2. **Confusing "poly(A) tail function in prokaryotes" with eukaryotes.** In prokaryotes, poly(A) is a **degradation tag**, not a stability tag. Many students write "it stabilizes mRNA" — **lose 1 mark**. > 3. **Forgetting to mention the enzymes by name.** CPSF, CstF, and PAP each carry marks individually. Writing vaguely "proteins add the tail" costs at least 2 marks. > 4. **Splicing diagrams missing the lariat intermediate.** The 2′–5′ lariat is a **mandatory visual** expected in any answer worth ≥5 marks. Without it, examiners deduct a full mark. > 5. **Stating "introns are non-coding DNA" instead of "introns are non-coding sequences within the pre-mRNA that are removed during splicing."** The word "DNA" is technically wrong here — by the time we are discussing introns in this context, we are talking about the **RNA copy**, not the genome. > 6. **Alternative splicing answer without tissue-specific examples.** KTU 2024 papers increasingly reward context (e.g., mentioning **DMD exon 51**, **SMN2 exon 7**, or **Tropomyosin exons 2a/2b**). Generic answers score 4–5; contextualized answers score 6–7. > 7. **Numerical problems without units.** Always write "nt" (nucleotides), "min," "Da," etc. A correct number without a unit is treated as **incomplete** in KTU valuation. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam.** ### ✅ High-Yield Definitions to Memorize - **Post-transcriptional modification** — enzymatic/covalent alterations converting primary transcript → mature RNA. - **5′ Cap (Cap-0)** — m⁷G(5′)ppp(5′)N; 5′-to-5′ triphosphate bridge. - **Cap-1** — m⁷GpppNᵐ; 2′-O-methylated first ribose. - **Polyadenylation signal (PAS)** — AAUAAA hexamer, 10–30 nt upstream of cleavage site. - **Branch point consensus** — CURAY (Y = C or U). - **Spliceosome** — ∼12.5 MDa RNP of 5 snRNPs (U1, U2, U4/U6, U5) + >100 proteins. ### ✅ Critical Distinctions - **Prokaryote poly(A) tail = degradation signal.** **Eukaryote poly(A) tail = stability signal.** - **Prokaryotes have NO 5′ cap, NO spliceosome, NO intron removal in standard mRNAs.** - **Transcription and translation are coupled in prokaryotes, separated in eukaryotes.** ### ✅ High-Yield Numerical Facts - Human poly(A) tail ≈ 200 nt; yeast ≈ 70 nt; prokaryotic ≈ 10–40 nt. - Spliceosome mass ≈ 12.5 MDa. - ∼95% of human genes contain introns; average exon ≈ 150 nt; average intron ≈ 3,000 nt. - Human genome → ∼20,000 genes → >100,000 proteins via alternative splicing. - mRNA half-life: prokaryote 2–5 min; eukaryote hours. ### ✅ Five Core Processing Events in Eukaryotes (Mnemonic: **"Cap-Splice-PolyA-Edit-Modify"**) 1. **5′ Capping** (RTase → GTase → MTase) → Cap-0/Cap-1. 2. **Splicing** (spliceosome: U1, U2, U4/U6, U5) → 2 transesterifications. 3. **3′ Polyadenylation** (CPSF + CstF → cleavage; PAP + PABP II → tail). 4. **RNA Editing** (APOBEC-1 → C-to-U; ADARs → A-to-I). 5. **Base Modification** (rRNA, tRNA, snRNA methylations; guided by snoRNAs in eukaryotes). ### ✅ Enzyme-to-Function Quick-Map | Enzyme | Function | Processing Event | |---|---|---| | RNA triphosphatase (RTase) | Removes γ-phosphate | Capping | | Guanylyltransferase (GTase) | Adds GMP via 5′-5′ bond | Capping | | Methyltransferase (MTase) | Adds methyl group | Capping | | CPSF (CPSF-73) | Endonuclease | Polyadenylation (cleavage) | | CstF | Binds DSE | Polyadenylation (cleavage) | | PAP (Poly(A) Polymerase) | Adds A residues | Polyadenylation (tail) | | PABP II | Binds tail, stimulates PAP | Polyadenylation (tail length) | | APOBEC-1 | C-to-U editing | RNA editing | | ADAR | A-to-I editing | RNA editing | | DBR1 | Debranches lariat | Splicing | | RNase P | Cleaves 5′ of pre-tRNA | tRNA processing (prok. + euk.) | | RNase III | Cleaves pre-rRNA | rRNA processing (prok. + euk.) | ### ✅ Clinical / Biotech Hooks (Examiner loves these) - **β-thalassemia** — splice-site mutations in β-globin. - **Spinal muscular atrophy** — *SMN2* exon 7 skipping; treated by **nusinersen (Spinraza)**. - **Duchenne muscular dystrophy** — *DMD* exon 51; treated by **eteplirsen**. - **mRNA COVID vaccines** — Cap-1 + ∼100 nt poly(A) + codon optimization. - **ApoB editing** — C-to-U editing produces intestinal apoB48 vs. hepatic apoB100. ### ✅ Final Exam Mantra > **"From gene to protein, the RNA must be CAPPED, SPLICED, POLYADENYLATED, and EDITED before it earns its ribosome ticket."**

topics.title: Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic Translation Processes: Basic Components and Steps, Post-Translational Modifications, Protein Folding, HTH, HLH, Zinc Finger Motif; Molecular Mechanism of Gene Regulation in Prokaryotes - Transcriptional Regulation in Prokaryotes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic Translation Processes: Basic Components and Steps, Post-Translational Modifications, Protein Folding, HTH, HLH, Zinc Finger Motif; Molecular Mechanism of Gene Regulation in Prokaryotes - Transcriptional Regulation in Prokaryotes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Translation — The Central Dogma's Final Pillar

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Translational Process):**
> **Translation** is the cellular process by which the sequence of codons in a messenger RNA (mRNA) molecule is decoded by the ribosome to synthesize a specific polypeptide chain (protein). It is the final step of gene expression, where the linear **nucleotide code (4-letter alphabet)** is converted into a **linear amino acid code (20-letter alphabet)** using **transfer RNA (tRNA)** as the physical adaptor molecule.

The central dogma of molecular biology, as proposed by **Francis Crick (1958, restated 1970)**, states that genetic information flows in a unidirectional manner:

$$\text{DNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Transcription}} \text{mRNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Translation}} \text{Protein}$$

**Translation** is the *RNA → Protein* step. The molecular machinery, players, and energy requirements differ significantly between prokaryotes and eukaryotes — a favorite KTU board exam discriminator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must know the **basic components and steps** of translation in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes, **post-translational modifications (PTMs)**, and **protein folding mechanisms** including chaperones. Also covered are **DNA-binding motifs** (HTH, HLH, Zinc Finger) and **prokaryotic transcriptional regulation** (lac operon, trp operon).

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line"

Imagine a **car manufacturing plant**:
- The **blueprint (mRNA)** is read on a conveyor belt (the **ribosome**).
- **Robotic arms (tRNA)** pick up the correct **car parts (amino acids)** based on the blueprint instructions.
- **Workers (aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases)** charge each robotic arm with the correct part.
- The **finished car (folded protein)** is then inspected, polished, and modified (post-translational modifications) before being shipped out.

If the blueprint has errors (mutations) or the conveyor belt stalls, the product is defective — analogous to disease states (e.g., sickle cell anemia caused by a single point mutation in the β-globin gene).

---

## 1.2 Basic Components Required for Translation

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Components for Both Prokaryotic & Eukaryotic Translation:**

| Component | Function | Key Property |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **mRNA** | Encodes the amino acid sequence via triplet codons | Read 5′ → 3′ |
| **tRNA** | Adaptor molecule; carries amino acid and anticodon | L-shaped, ~**76 nt** long |
| **Ribosome** | Catalyzes peptide bond formation (peptidyl transferase) | rRNA + proteins |
| **Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases** | Charge tRNA with the correct amino acid | 20 enzymes (one per aa) |
| **GTP & ATP** | Energy currency | **2 GTP per peptide bond** minimum |
| **Initiation, Elongation, Termination factors** | Protein helpers at each stage | Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic differ |

---

## 1.3 DNA-Binding Motifs — The "Protein Switches"

Gene regulation requires proteins that specifically **recognize DNA sequences**. They do this through small, recurring structural modules called **DNA-binding motifs**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HTH (Helix-Turn-Helix):** A **60-amino-acid** motif consisting of two α-helices separated by a β-turn. The "recognition helix" inserts into the **major groove** of DNA. Found in **lambda repressor, cro protein, lac repressor, CAP, homeodomain proteins**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HLH (Helix-Loop-Helix):** Two α-helices joined by a loop of variable length. Often forms **dimers** that grip DNA like a pair of chopsticks. Found in **MyoD, myc, E2A, USF**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zinc Finger Motif:** A small structural domain stabilized by a **Zn²⁺** ion coordinated to **Cys and His residues** (e.g., Cys₂His₂ or Cys₄). Looks like a finger protruding into the DNA major groove. Found in **TFIIIA, Sp1, steroid receptors (with Cys₄ fingers)**.

---

## 1.4 Transcriptional Regulation in Prokaryotes — The Operon Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operon Definition (Jacob & Monod, 1961):** A cluster of genes under the control of a single **promoter** and **operator**, transcribed as a single polycistronic mRNA. The operon is the fundamental unit of **coordinate gene regulation** in prokaryotes.

The classic example: the **lac operon** — an *inducible* system — and the **trp operon** — a *repressible* system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Genetic Code — The Translation Dictionary

The **genetic code** is a set of rules by which information encoded in genetic material (DNA/RNA sequences) is translated into proteins by living cells. It is a **triplet, non-overlapping, comma-free, and degenerate** code.

### Properties of the Genetic Code

1. **Triplet:** Each codon consists of **3 nucleotides**.
   $$4^3 = 64 \text{ codons possible}$$
2. **Degeneracy:** 64 codons code for only **20 amino acids + 3 stop signals** → most amino acids are specified by multiple codons (synonymous codons).
3. **Unambiguity:** Each codon specifies only **one** amino acid.
4. **Universal (with minor exceptions):** Same in almost all organisms. Exceptions: mitochondrial code, *Mycoplasma*, ciliates.
5. **Start codon:** **AUG** (codes for methionine; sometimes GUG in prokaryotes).
6. **Stop codons (non-sense):** **UAA, UAG, UGA** — do not code for any amino acid.

### The Codon Table (RNA form, 5′ → 3′)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, memorize the codon for **AUG (Met — start)**, **stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA)**, and key disease-associated codons like **GAG → GUG (Glu → Val in sickle cell)**.

---

## 2.2 Prokaryotic Translation — Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In prokaryotes, **transcription and translation are coupled** — translation begins on the mRNA **before its synthesis is complete** (because there is no nuclear membrane). Ribosomes are **70S** (made of **50S large + 30S small** subunits).

### Stage 1: Initiation (Prokaryotic)

**Key components:**
- **30S ribosomal subunit**
- **mRNA** with **Shine-Dalgarno sequence** (5′-AGGAGG-3′) located **~7 nt upstream** of the AUG start codon
- **fMet-tRNA^fMet** (formyl-methionyl-tRNA) — the initiator
- **Initiation factors:** **IF-1, IF-2, IF-3**
- **GTP** (1 molecule)

**Steps:**
1. **IF-3** binds the 30S subunit, preventing premature 50S binding.
2. **IF-2-GTP** recruits **fMet-tRNA^fMet** to the **P-site**.
3. **IF-1** occupies the A-site, blocking premature tRNA entry.
4. The 30S pre-initiation complex binds the **Shine-Dalgarno sequence** of mRNA, aligning the start codon at the P-site.
5. The **50S subunit** joins; GTP is hydrolyzed; IFs are released. The **70S initiation complex** is formed.

### Stage 2: Elongation (Prokaryotic)

**Key components:**
- **Elongation factors:** **EF-Tu** (delivers aa-tRNA to A-site), **EF-Ts** (recycles EF-Tu-GDP → EF-Tu-GTP), **EF-G** (translocation)
- **GTP** (2 molecules per cycle)

**Steps:**
1. **Decoding:** EF-Tu·GTP carries the correct aa-tRNA into the **A-site**. Correct codon-anticodon pairing triggers GTP hydrolysis.
2. **Peptide bond formation:** The **23S rRNA** of the 50S subunit (acts as **ribozyme** — peptidyl transferase) catalyzes attack of the A-site amino group on the P-site ester bond. The polypeptide is transferred to the A-site tRNA.
3. **Translocation:** **EF-G·GTP** binds; the ribosome moves **3 nucleotides (1 codon)** toward 3′ of mRNA. The tRNAs shift: A-site → P-site, P-site → E-site. GTP is hydrolyzed; EF-G is released.

### Stage 3: Termination (Prokaryotic)

**Release factors:** **RF-1** (recognizes UAA, UAG), **RF-2** (recognizes UAA, UGA), **RF-3** (GTPase).

**Steps:**
1. A stop codon enters the A-site. No tRNA recognizes it.
2. **RF-1 or RF-2** enters the A-site.
3. **Peptidyl transferase** hydrolyzes the bond, releasing the polypeptide.
4. **RF-3·GTP** helps dissociate the ribosome.

---

## 2.3 Eukaryotic Translation — Differences from Prokaryotic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Eukaryotic translation is **compartmentalized**: transcription occurs in the **nucleus**, translation in the **cytoplasm** (or on the **rough ER**). The mRNA undergoes **processing (5′ cap, poly-A tail, splicing)** before export. Ribosomes are **80S** (made of **60S large + 40S small**).

### Comparative Table — Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic Translation

| Feature | Prokaryotic | Eukaryotic |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Ribosome size | **70S** (50S + 30S) | **80S** (60S + 40S) |
| Initiator tRNA | **fMet-tRNA^fMet** | **Met-tRNAi^Met** (non-formylated) |
| Start codon positioning | Shine-Dalgarno (AGGAGG) | **Kozak sequence** (ACC**AUG**G) |
| Initiation factors | IF-1, IF-2, IF-3 (3 factors) | **eIF-1, eIF-2, eIF-3, eIF-4, eIF-5, eIF-6** (≥12 factors) |
| Coupled with transcription | **Yes** | **No** (spatially separated) |
| mRNA | Polycistronic | **Monocistronic** (one ORF per mRNA) |
| First amino acid | **N-formylmethionine** | **Methionine** |
| Elongation factors | EF-Tu, EF-Ts, EF-G | **eEF-1α, eEF-1βγ, eEF-2** |
| Termination factors | RF-1, RF-2, RF-3 | **eRF-1, eRF-3** |
| Location | Cytoplasm | Cytoplasm / Rough ER |
| Regulation | Mainly **transcriptional** | **Translational** control also crucial |

### Eukaryotic Initiation — Cap-Dependent Mechanism

1. **eIF-4F complex** (eIF-4E + eIF-4G + eIF-4A) binds the **5′ m⁷G cap** of mRNA.
2. **eIF-4G** acts as a scaffold, recruiting **eIF-3** and the **40S subunit**.
3. **eIF-2·GTP·Met-tRNAi^Met** ternary complex joins; scans the 5′ UTR to find the **AUG** in proper Kozak context.
4. **eIF-5** triggers GTP hydrolysis; release of factors.
5. **60S subunit** joins (mediated by **eIF-5B-GTP**) → 80S initiation complex.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 favourite exam point:** The **closed-loop model** of eukaryotic translation — eIF-4G binds both the 5′ cap (via eIF-4E) and the **poly-A binding protein (PABP)**, circularizing the mRNA. This enhances translation efficiency and allows **ribosome recycling**.

---

## 2.4 Post-Translational Modifications (PTMs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Translational Modifications (PTMs)** are covalent chemical alterations to amino acid side chains of a protein **after** its synthesis on the ribosome. PTMs regulate **protein activity, localization, stability, and interactions**.

### Major Types of PTMs

| PTM | Target Residue | Example / Significance |
|-----|----------------|------------------------|
| **Phosphorylation** | Ser, Thr, Tyr | Kinase signaling (e.g., insulin receptor) |
| **Glycosylation** | Asn (N-linked), Ser/Thr (O-linked) | Membrane/receptor proteins |
| **Acetylation** | Lysine (N-terminus or side chain) | Histone regulation; protein stability |
| **Methylation** | Lys, Arg | Histone code |
| **Ubiquitination** | Lys | Marks proteins for **proteasomal degradation** |
| **Sumoylation** | Lys | Nuclear transport, transcription |
| **Prenylation** | Cys (CAAX box) | Membrane anchoring (Ras proteins) |
| **Hydroxylation** | Pro, Lys | Collagen triple-helix stability |
| **Disulfide bond formation** | Cys | Extracellular proteins (insulin, antibodies) |
| **Proteolytic cleavage** | Various | Insulin (proinsulin → insulin + C-peptide) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ubiquitin-Proteasome System (UPS):** Ubiquitin is a **76-aa** protein attached via an **isopeptide bond** between its C-terminal Gly and the ε-amino group of a target Lys. Poly-ubiquitination (≥4 Ub) targets proteins to the **26S proteasome** for ATP-dependent degradation. Nobel Prize (2004) — **Ciechanover, Hershko, Rose**.

---

## 2.5 Protein Folding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protein folding** is the physical process by which a linear polypeptide chain acquires its **native three-dimensional structure** (conformation), driven by the hydrophobic effect, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, and ionic interactions.

### Anfinsen's Experiment (1972 Nobel Prize)

Christian Anfinsen showed that **ribonuclease A** could be **denatured** (loss of structure and function by urea + β-mercaptoethanol) and then **spontaneously refolded** to its native, functional state when denaturants were removed.

> **Implication:** The **primary sequence (amino acid sequence)** contains all the information needed for proper folding — known as **Anfinsen's dogma** or the **thermodynamic hypothesis**.

$$F_{\text{native}} \longleftrightarrow F_{\text{unfolded}}$$

$$K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{[F_{\text{native}}]}{[F_{\text{unfolded}}]}$$

The native state has the **lowest Gibbs free energy** under physiological conditions:

$$\Delta G_{\text{folding}} = G_{\text{native}} - G_{\text{unfolded}} < 0$$

### The Levinthal Paradox

> Cyrus Levinthal (1969) noted that a 100-residue protein has ~$3^{100}$ possible conformations. If the protein sampled one per picosecond, it would take **~10^77 years** to find the native state. Yet proteins fold in **milliseconds to seconds**.

> **Resolution:** Folding is **funnel-shaped** on the energy landscape — guided by progressive stabilization, not random search. The **folding funnel** concept (Wolynes, Onuchic) explains the paradox.

### Chaperones — Folding Assistants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molecular chaperones** are proteins that assist the **folding, assembly, and refolding** of other proteins, but are **not part of the final functional structure**.

| Chaperone Family | Type | Function | Location |
|------------------|------|----------|----------|
| **Hsp70 (DnaK in E. coli)** | ATP-dependent | Binds exposed hydrophobic patches; prevents aggregation | Cytosol, ER, mitochondria |
| **Hsp60 / GroEL/GroES** | ATP-dependent | **Anfinsen cage** — provides isolated chamber for folding | Mitochondria, bacteria |
| **Hsp90** | ATP-dependent | Stabilizes signaling proteins (steroid receptors, kinases) | Cytosol |
| **Trigger Factor** | ATP-independent | First chaperone in bacteria; binds emerging polypeptide at ribosome exit tunnel | Bacteria |
| **Calnexin / Calreticulin** | Ca²⁺-dependent | Quality control of N-glycosylated proteins in ER | ER |

### Protein Misfolding Diseases

| Disease | Misfolded Protein | Pathology |
|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| Alzheimer's | Amyloid-β (Aβ) | Extracellular plaques |
| Parkinson's | α-synuclein | Lewy bodies |
| Prion diseases (CJD) | PrP^Sc | Spongiform encephalopathy |
| Cystic fibrosis | ΔF508 CFTR | Misfolded, degraded |
| Type II Diabetes | Amylin (IAPP) | Amyloid in pancreatic islets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Prion Concept (Prusiner, 1997 Nobel):** Prions are **infectious proteins** that propagate by inducing the **misfolding** of the normal cellular protein (PrP^C → PrP^Sc). No nucleic acid is required for infectivity.

---

## 2.6 DNA-Binding Motifs — Detailed Structural Analysis

### Helix-Turn-Helix (HTH)

- **Length:** ~**60 amino acids** (in some cases only 20 aa form the actual motif)
- **Structure:** Two α-helices (α2 and α3) connected by a **β-turn** of 3-4 residues
- **DNA contact:** The **recognition helix (α3)** inserts into the **major groove**; the other helix stabilizes the structure
- **Geometry:** Approximate angle between two helices = **~120°**
- **Examples:** **λ repressor, Cro protein, lac repressor (DNA-binding domain), CAP (catabolite activator protein), homeodomain proteins (e.g., Antennapedia)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HTH motif is the **most common DNA-binding motif** in prokaryotes. It was first characterized in the **lambda phage cro protein** by Pabo & Lewis (1982).

### Helix-Loop-Helix (HLH)

- **Length:** ~**40-50 amino acids**
- **Structure:** Two α-helices separated by a **loop of variable length (10-25 aa)**
- **DNA binding:** The two helices (H1 and H2) from **two separate proteins** form a **4-helix bundle dimer** that grips DNA
- **Variants:**
  - **bHLH** (basic HLH): Contains a **basic region** N-terminal to the first helix, which directly contacts DNA. Examples: **MyoD, E12/E47, USF, c-Myc**
  - **bHLH-LZ** (basic HLH-Leucine Zipper): Combines HLH with a leucine zipper (e.g., **Myc/Max**)
- **Examples:** MyoD (muscle differentiation), E2A, c-Myc (oncogene)

### Zinc Finger Motif

- **Length:** ~**30 amino acids per finger**
- **Metal ion:** **Zn²⁺** coordinated tetrahedrally
- **Two main types:**
  - **Classical (Cys₂His₂):** Most common in eukaryotes. Sequence: **(Tyr/Phe)-X-Cys-X₂₋₄-Cys-X₁₂-His-X₃₋₄-His**. Found in **TFIIIA, Sp1, Zif268**.
  - **Cys₄ / Cys-Cys class:** Found in **steroid/nuclear hormone receptors** (e.g., glucocorticoid receptor, estrogen receptor).
- **DNA contact:** The α-helix inserts into the major groove; tandem fingers wrap around the DNA.
- **Discovery:** First identified in **TFIIIA** (transcription factor for 5S rRNA) by **Aaron Klug** (Nobel 1982, though for viral structures; zinc finger work by Miller & Pabo in the 1980s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Zinc finger chimera (ZFN) technology:** Engineered zinc fingers fused to **FokI nuclease** are used for **targeted genome editing** — a precursor to CRISPR-Cas9.

---

## 2.7 Transcriptional Regulation in Prokaryotes

### The lac Operon (Inducible System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **lac Operon Structure (E. coli):** Discovered by **François Jacob & Jacques Monod (1961 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1965).** The operon contains:
> - **lacZ** — β-galactosidase (cleaves lactose → glucose + galactose)
> - **lacY** — permease (imports lactose)
> - **lacA** — transacetylase (detoxifies thiogalactosides)
> - **Promoter (P)** — RNA polymerase binding site
> - **Operator (O)** — repressor binding site
> - **Regulatory gene (lacI)** — codes for the **lac repressor** (tetramer)

#### Negative Regulation (Repression)

- In the **absence of lactose**, the **lac repressor (LacI)** binds the operator and **blocks transcription**.
- The repressor has an **allosteric site** for the inducer.
- When **lactose is present**, it is converted to **allolactose** (the natural inducer) by β-galactosidase. Allolactose binds the repressor → **conformational change** → repressor dissociates from the operator → **transcription ON**.

#### Positive Regulation (Catabolite Repression)

- When **glucose is high**, **cAMP levels are LOW** → CAP cannot bind → low transcription even if lactose is present (this is the "glucose effect").
- When **glucose is low**, **cAMP levels are HIGH** → cAMP binds **CAP (catabolite activator protein, also called CRP)** → cAMP-CAP complex binds the **lac promoter** upstream of the operator, **recruiting RNA polymerase** → high transcription.

> [!NOTE]
> **Glucose effect / Catabolite repression:** Cells prefer glucose. They only use lactose when glucose is depleted. This is mediated by the **cAMP-CAP positive control** superimposed on the **repressor negative control**.

#### Summary Table — lac Operon Regulation

| Glucose | Lactose | cAMP | Repressor | CAP binding | Transcription |
|---------|---------|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| High | Absent | Low | Active (bound) | No | **OFF** |
| High | Present | Low | Inactive | No | **Low / OFF** |
| Low | Absent | High | Active | Yes | **OFF** |
| Low | Present | High | Inactive | Yes | **ON (high)** |

### The trp Operon (Repressible System)

- Contains 5 structural genes: **trpE, trpD, trpC, trpB, trpA** for tryptophan biosynthesis.
- **Regulation:**
  1. **Negative regulation by repressor:** The **trp repressor** is encoded by **trpR**; it is **inactive** until it binds **tryptophan (the corepressor)**. The trp-repressor complex binds the operator → transcription OFF.
  2. **Attenuation:** A **leader sequence (trpL)** with a **4-codon pause site** (encoding Trp-Trp-Trp-Trp-Stop) allows fine-tuning. When Trp is abundant, the ribosome translates the leader quickly → terminator hairpin forms → transcription stops. When Trp is scarce, the ribosome stalls → anti-terminator hairpin forms → transcription continues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Attenuation** is a **transcription-coupled translation regulation** mechanism unique to prokaryotes. It uses the rate of ribosome movement to determine whether a terminator or anti-terminator hairpin forms in the mRNA.

### Other Regulatory Mechanisms in Prokaryotes

1. **Positive control — Arabinose operon (ara):** The **AraC protein** acts as both activator and repressor. Arabinose converts AraC to its activator form.
2. **Riboswitches:** mRNA leader sequences that bind metabolites (e.g., vitamins, amino acids) and regulate transcription/translation via structural changes. No protein factor required.
3. **Two-component systems:** Sensor histidine kinase (e.g., EnvZ) phosphorylates a response regulator (e.g., OmpR) which then modulates transcription.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Possible Codons

The genetic code uses a **triplet (3-nucleotide) reading frame**. There are **4 nucleotide bases** (A, U/equivalent in DNA, G, C).

**Step 1:** Each position in the codon can be any of the 4 bases.

**Step 2:** For a triplet, multiply the choices:

$$N_{\text{codons}} = 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 4^3 = 64$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the 3 nonsense (stop) codons to get the sense codons:

$$N_{\text{sense}} = 64 - 3 = 61$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that 61 codons encode only 20 amino acids. The ratio:

$$\text{Degeneracy ratio} = \frac{61}{20} = 3.05 \text{ codons per amino acid (on average)}$$

**Real-world engineering utility:** This degeneracy underlies the design of **codon-optimized expression vectors** used in **recombinant protein production** (e.g., insulin in *E. coli*). The codon usage of the host organism is matched to optimize translation efficiency — a key concept in **synthetic biology and bioprocess engineering**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Cost of Translation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions sometimes ask for the **minimum energy cost** of synthesizing a protein.

**Step 1:** Charging of tRNA (aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase reaction):

$$\text{Amino acid} + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{Aminoacyl-AMP} + \text{PP_i}$$

$$\text{Aminoacyl-AMP} + \text{tRNA} \rightarrow \text{Aminoacyl-tRNA} + \text{AMP}$$

**Net:** ATP → AMP + PP_i. The hydrolysis of PP_i into 2 Pi makes this equivalent to **2 ATP → 2 ADP + Pi**. So **1 ATP equivalent is consumed per amino acid** for charging (more rigorously, **2 high-energy phosphate bonds**).

**Step 2:** Elongation uses **2 GTP per amino acid** added:
- 1 GTP for EF-Tu·GTP hydrolysis (delivery of aa-tRNA)
- 1 GTP for EF-G·GTP hydrolysis (translocation)

**Step 3:** Initiation uses **1 GTP** (IF-2/eIF-2) and termination uses **1 GTP** (RF-3/eRF-3) per protein.

**Step 4:** Total energy cost for a protein of **n** amino acids:

$$E_{\text{total}} = n \cdot (\text{ATP for charging}) + n \cdot 2 \cdot \text{GTP} + \text{GTP}_{\text{init}} + \text{GTP}_{\text{term}}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} \approx 2n + 2n + 1 + 1 = 4n + 2 \text{ high-energy phosphate bonds}$$

**For a 100-aa protein (small peptide):**

$$E_{\text{total}} \approx 4(100) + 2 = 402 \text{ P–P bonds}$$

**Step 5:** Conversion to kcal (1 P–P bond ≈ **7.3 kcal/mol** under standard conditions):

$$E_{\text{total}} = 402 \times 7.3 \approx 2934 \text{ kcal/mol}$$

> **Real-world utility:** Metabolic engineering exploits this energy budget. Cells under nutrient stress **downregulate translation** to conserve ATP/GTP — a key consideration in industrial bioprocess design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Levinthal's Paradox (Quantitative)

> Cyrus Levinthal estimated the time for a 100-residue protein to fold by random sampling.

**Step 1:** Each residue has ~**3 allowed backbone conformations** (phi, psi angles, with ~10 sterically allowed basins, simplified to 3).

**Step 2:** Total conformations for 100 residues:

$$N_{\text{conf}} = 3^{100} \approx 5 \times 10^{47}$$

**Step 3:** Sampling time per conformation: ~**1 picosecond** (10⁻¹² s) — based on the fastest molecular vibrations.

**Step 4:** Total time to exhaustively sample:

$$T_{\text{search}} = N_{\text{conf}} \times 10^{-12} \text{ s} = 5 \times 10^{35} \text{ s}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to years (1 year ≈ 3.15 × 10⁷ s):

$$T_{\text{search}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{35}}{3.15 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{28} \text{ years}$$

**Comparison to age of universe (~1.4 × 10¹⁰ years):**

$$\frac{T_{\text{search}}}{T_{\text{universe}}} \approx 10^{18}$$

> **Resolution:** Real proteins do NOT sample randomly. They follow a **downhill energy funnel** (Wolynes, Onuchic), collapsing rapidly into the native state. **Folding intermediates** and **molten globule states** exist.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Codon Recognition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 typical 3-mark question:** *A point mutation in a gene changes the codon CAA to UAA. What is the consequence?*

**Step 1:** Identify the original codon: **CAA** codes for **Glutamine (Gln, Q)**.

**Step 2:** Identify the new codon: **UAA** is a **stop codon (ochre)**.

**Step 3:** Consequence: This is a **nonsense mutation**, which causes **premature termination of translation**. The resulting polypeptide is truncated, typically **non-functional**, and may be subject to **nonsense-mediated mRNA decay (NMD)** in eukaryotes.

**Step 4:** Disease example: Nonsense mutations in the **CFTR gene** cause ~10% of cystic fibrosis cases (e.g., **G542X** in exon 11).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Shine-Dalgarno Interaction

**Question:** An mRNA has the sequence 5′-...AGGAGG**AUG**...-3′. Identify the SD sequence and the start codon. Calculate the optimal spacing.

**Step 1:** The **Shine-Dalgarno (SD) consensus** is 5′-**AGGAGG**-3′ (can be 5′-GGAGG-3′ as a minimum).

**Step 2:** SD is located **~7 nt upstream** of the AUG (commonly 5-10 nt). It base-pairs with the anti-SD sequence at the **3′ end of 16S rRNA**: 3′-**CCUCCU**-5′.

**Step 3:** Base pairing:

$$\text{5′-AGGAGG-3′ (mRNA)} \longleftrightarrow \text{3′-CCUCCU-5′ (16S rRNA)}$$

**Step 4:** This interaction aligns the start codon in the P-site of the 30S subunit.
