## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define mutation. Differentiate between point mutations and frameshift mutations.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Mutation:** A permanent, heritable change in the DNA nucleotide sequence.
>
> | Feature | Point Mutation | Frameshift Mutation |
> |---------|---------------|---------------------|
> | **Scale** | Single base pair changed | 1–2 bp inserted or deleted |
> | **Reading frame** | Preserved | Disrupted |
> | **Severity** | Mild to moderate | Usually severe |
> | **Types** | Silent, missense, nonsense | Insertion, deletion (indels) |
> | **Example** | Sickle cell (GAG→GTG) | Tay-Sachs, many cancers |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for tabular differentiation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What are base analogs? Give two examples and explain how 5-bromouracil induces mutations.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Base analogs** are chemical mutagens that resemble normal DNA bases and are incorporated during replication but pair incorrectly.
>
> **Examples:** 5-Bromouracil (5-BU), 2-Aminopurine (2-AP).
>
> **Mechanism of 5-BU:**
> 1. 5-BU is a thymine analog — incorporates opposite A in *keto* form.
> 2. It tautomerizes to the *enol* form (resembles cytosine).
> 3. Enol-5-BU pairs with **G** during next replication.
> 4. Result: original A·T replaced by **G·C** — a **transition mutation**.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + examples, 2 for mechanism)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE pattern: Module-wise choice. Either Q11 OR Q12 from Module 4. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify mutations in detail with suitable examples. Explain the difference between transitions and transversions.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the various spontaneous mechanisms that lead to mutations in DNA. Add a note on depurination and oxidative damage.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification of Mutations:**

**1. Gene-Level (Small-Scale) Mutations**

- **Point (Substitution) Mutations:**
  - **Silent** — codon changes but encodes same amino acid (e.g., GCA → GCC both = Alanine) — degeneracy of code.
  - **Missense** — codon changes to encode different amino acid (e.g., HbS: GAG→GTG → Glu→Val).
  - **Nonsense** — codon changes to a STOP codon (UAA, UAG, UGA) → truncated protein.

- **Frameshift Mutations:** Insertion or deletion of 1–2 bp → reading frame shifts → often premature STOP.

- **In-frame Mutations:** Indels of multiples of 3 bp → frame preserved.

**2. Chromosome-Level (Large-Scale) Mutations**

- **Deletion** — segment lost (e.g., Cri-du-chat syndrome — 5p deletion).
- **Duplication** — segment copied (e.g., Charcot-Marie-Tooth 1A — PMP22).
- **Inversion** — segment reversed (e.g., Inv(9) in humans).
- **Translocation** — segment moves (e.g., Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) in CML).
- **Ring chromosome / Isochromosome / Aneuploidy**.

**Transition vs. Transversion:**

| Feature | Transition | Transversion |
|---------|-----------|--------------|
| Definition | Purine↔Purine or Pyrimidine↔Pyrimidine | Purine↔Pyrimidine |
| Examples | A↔G, C↔T | A↔C, A↔T, G↔C, G↔T |
| Frequency | More common | Less common |
| Causes | Tautomerism, 5-BU, EMS | 8-oxoG, ionizing radiation |

Transitions: **4 possible**. Transversions: **8 possible**.

*(7 marks: 4 for classification with examples, 3 for transition/transversion table)*

**(b) Spontaneous Mechanisms of Mutation:**

**1. Tautomerism:** Bases shift between keto–enol / amino–imino forms. Rare tautomers mispair → transitions.

**2. Deamination:** Loss of –NH₂ group:
- C → U (removed by Uracil-DNA glycosylase)
- 5-MeC → T (NOT recognized → CpG hotspots)
- A → Hypoxanthine (pairs with C)

**3. Depurination:** Hydrolytic cleavage of N-glycosidic bond of A or G → apurinic (AP) site. Occurs ~10,000 times/cell/day. Replication across AP site inserts a random base → transversion.

**4. Depyrimidination:** Loss of T or C (less common).

**5. Oxidative Damage:** ROS (·OH) attack bases; 8-oxoguanine (8-oxoG) pairs with A → **G·C → T·A transversion** (signature of oxidative damage).

**6. Replication Slippage:** Slippage at microsatellite/trinucleotide repeats → insertions/deletions of repeat unit (e.g., Huntington's disease CAG expansion).

**7. Illegitimate Recombination / Fork Collapse:** Strand breaks mis-repaired → chromosomal rearrangements.

*(7 marks: 1 each for 5 mechanisms, 2 for depurination/oxidative note)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the action of the following mutagens with mechanisms: (i) Base analogs, (ii) Alkylating agents, (iii) Intercalating agents.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the Luria–Delbrück fluctuation test. How is it used to estimate mutation rate? What did it prove about the origin of mutations?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Mutagen Action Mechanisms:**

**(i) Base Analogs:** Substitute for normal bases during replication but mispair.

- *5-Bromouracil (5-BU):* T analog. Keto form pairs with A; enol form pairs with G → A·T → G·C transition.
- *2-Aminopurine (2-AP):* A analog. Pairs with T (normal) or C (rare) → transitions.

**(ii) Alkylating Agents:** Add alkyl (–CH₃, –C₂H₅) groups to bases.

- *EMS (Ethyl methanesulfonate):* Alkylates **O⁶ of guanine** → O⁶-ethylguanine pairs with T → **G·C → A·T transition**.
- *MNNG (N-methyl-N′-nitro-N-nitrosoguanidine):* Powerful alkylator at multiple sites.
- *Mustard gas:* Crosslinks DNA strands.

**(iii) Intercalating Agents:** Flat, planar polycyclic molecules (acridines, ethidium bromide, proflavine) **slide between base pairs**, distorting helix and causing polymerase to skip or insert a base.

- Result: **±1 base insertions/deletions → frameshift mutations**.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 for each category)*

**(b) Luria–Delbrück Fluctuation Test (1943):**

**Principle:** To distinguish whether mutations arise **spontaneously and randomly** (Luria–Delbrück) or in **response to selection** (Lamarckian / directed mutation hypothesis).

**Procedure:**
1. Inoculate many parallel small cultures of *E. coli* from a single non-mutant (e.g., T1-phage sensitive) inoculum.
2. Allow independent growth.
3. Plate each culture on selective medium (e.g., T1-phage).
4. Count resistant colonies in each culture.

**Predicted outcomes:**
- **If mutations are spontaneous (random) before selection:** Variance in mutant counts between cultures will be HUGE (jackpot cultures).
- **If mutations are induced by selection:** Each culture will have similar (Poisson) numbers of mutants.

**Observation:** Luria & Delbrück observed **HUGE variance** → **mutations are spontaneous and random**, occurring during replication independent of selection. This earned them the **1969 Nobel Prize** in Physiology or Medicine.

**Mathematical estimation of mutation rate:**

$$
P_0 = e^{-m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = -\ln(P_0)
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m}{N}
$$

Where:
- $P_0$ = fraction of cultures with zero mutants.
- $m$ = mean number of mutations per culture.
- $N$ = total cells per culture.
- $\mu$ = mutation rate per locus per generation.

**Worked example:** If 14 of 20 cultures have zero mutants, $P_0 = 0.7$, $N = 10^8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= -\ln(0.7) = 0.357 \\
\mu &= \frac{0.357}{10^8} \approx 3.57 \times 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for principle/procedure, 2 for what it proved, 3 for math/estimation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse transition with transversion** in tabular comparison questions. A 1-mark penalty per wrong pair.
> 2. **In Luria–Delbrück questions**, students often forget the `m = –ln(P₀)` formula and write only the qualitative answer — full marks require the **mathematical derivation**.
> 3. **Point mutation types** — examiners expect you to list **all three** (silent, missense, nonsense) with **at least one example** for each. Writing only "substitution" is insufficient.
> 4. **5-BU mechanism** — many students skip the *tautomerization step* (keto → enol). This step is the **key mark earner**.
> 5. **Frameshift vs. In-frame** — do not lump together. Frame-preservation must be explicitly stated.
> 6. **Depurination frequency** (~10,000/cell/day) and **mutation rate** (~10⁻⁹ per bp) — examiners love testing constants.
> 7. **Chromosomal vs. Gene mutations** — for 7-mark classification questions, a **proper hierarchical table** is worth 3–4 marks alone.
> 8. **Germline vs. Somatic** — if asked, always clarify heritability implications.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Exam Revision — Module 4 — Mutations**

- ✅ Mutation = **permanent, heritable DNA change**; wild-type = reference.
- ✅ **Three types of point mutations:** Silent (synonymous, no AA change), Missense (AA change), Nonsense (STOP).
- ✅ **Missense** can be **conservative** (similar AA) or **non-conservative** (dissimilar AA).
- ✅ **Frameshift:** ±1 or ±2 bases → reading frame disrupted → severe effect.
- ✅ **In-frame:** multiples of 3 → frame preserved → mild effect.
- ✅ **Transition** = purine↔purine OR pyrimidine↔pyrimidine (4 types).
- ✅ **Transversion** = purine↔pyrimidine (8 types) — rarer.
- ✅ **Spontaneous mechanisms:** Tautomerism, Deamination, Depurination, Oxidation, Slippage.
- ✅ **Deamination of 5-MeC → T** = **CpG hotspot** (unrepaired).
- ✅ **Depurination rate:** ~10,000/cell/day in humans; AP sites cause transversions.
- ✅ **8-Oxoguanine** (from ·OH attack on G) pairs with A → G·C → T·A transversion.
- ✅ **Base analogs:** 5-BU mimics T (keto pairs with A, enol pairs with G → transition).
- ✅ **Alkylating agents:** EMS alkylates O⁶-G → pairs with T → G·C → A·T transition.
- ✅ **Intercalators** (acridine, EtBr) → frameshift.
- ✅ **UV radiation** → Thymine dimers + 6-4 photoproducts; signature C→T transitions at dipyrimidines.
- ✅ **Ionizing radiation** → strand breaks, chromosomal aberrations.
- ✅ **Luria–Delbrück (1943):** proved mutations are **spontaneous and random** (Nobel 1969).
- ✅ **Luria–Delbrück formula:** $m = -\ln(P_0)$, $\mu = m/N$.
- ✅ **AMES test:** uses *Salmonella typhimurium* His⁻ → detects mutagens by reversion to His⁺.
- ✅ **Germline** = heritable; **Somatic** = not heritable, causes cancer/mosaicism.
- ✅ **Loss-of-function** = usually recessive; **Gain-of-function** = usually dominant.
- ✅ **Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22)** = translocation in CML — high-yield KTU example.
- ✅ **Huntington's disease** = CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion (replication slippage).
- ✅ **Sickle cell anemia** = GAG→GTG (Glu→Val) at β-globin codon 6 — classic missense example.
- ✅ **Mutation rate** in humans ≈ **10⁻⁸ per bp per generation**.
- ✅ **Mutagens can be carcinogens** — Ames test predicts carcinogenicity.
- ✅ **Repair pathways** (context): MMR (MutS/L/H in *E. coli*), NER (UV), BER (oxidative/AP sites) — fix errors BEFORE mutation is fixed.
- ✅ **Mutation ≠ recombination** — mutation alters sequence, recombination rearranges existing sequences.
- ✅ **Mnemonic: "Silly Mice Never Fear Large Chromosomes"** = Silent, Missense, Nonsense, Frameshift, Large-scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DNA Repair and Types of Repair, DNA Mismatch Repair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DNA Repair and Types of Repair, DNA Mismatch Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 DNA Repair — Formal Definition

**DNA Repair** is the collective set of cellular mechanisms by which living organisms identify and correct damage to the DNA molecule, restoring the original nucleotide sequence and maintaining genomic integrity. Damage to DNA can arise from endogenous (spontaneous) sources such as replication errors, base deamination, depurination, and reactive oxygen species, or from exogenous (environmental) sources such as ultraviolet (UV) radiation, ionizing radiation, and chemical mutagens. Without repair, the mutation rate of a cell would be catastrophic, leading to cell death, senescence, or oncogenic transformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBBT402, Module 4):** Students must be able to differentiate between the major repair pathways — **Direct Repair, Base Excision Repair (BER), Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER), Mismatch Repair (MMR)**, and **Double-Strand Break (DSB) Repair** — and explain the molecular players in each pathway.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spell-Check" of the Cell

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — DNA Repair as a Publishing House:** Imagine DNA as a massive encyclopedia being photocopied billions of times in a giant publishing house (the cell). Typos (mismatches) inevitably occur, and pages get smudged, torn, or stained (damage). The publishing house therefore employs multiple teams of editors:
> - **Proofreaders** work *during* photocopying to catch typos right away → analogous to **proofreading by DNA polymerase** (3′→5′ exonuclease).
> - **Specialist editors (BER team)** correct single-character typos → **Base Excision Repair**.
> - **Section editors (NER team)** remove a whole paragraph that has been damaged by sunlight → **Nucleotide Excision Repair**.
> - **Senior quality auditors (MMR team)** review the freshly printed pages *after* photocopying to catch errors missed by the proofreader → **Mismatch Repair**.
> - **Emergency restorers (HR/NHEJ)** glue back completely torn pages → **Double-Strand Break Repair**.

This is precisely why the cell invests enormous energy in DNA repair — *an unrepaired error becomes a permanent mutation in the next generation of cells.*

## 1.3 Why DNA Repair Matters — Quantitative Importance

The human genome contains approximately **3.2 × 10⁹ base pairs**, and during each cell division, an estimated **~10⁵** to **~10⁶** lesions occur per cell per day. Without repair, the cumulative damage would be lethal within hours. The spontaneous mutation rate in healthy cells is kept remarkably low at approximately **~10⁻⁹ mutations per base pair per generation**, thanks to the layered defense of repair systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Boards):** Spontaneous mutation rate = **~10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹⁰ per bp per generation**. This is the **fingerprint of a functional repair system.**

## 1.4 Overview of Major DNA Repair Pathways

| Pathway | Type of Damage Corrected | Key Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Repair** | Specific alkylated bases, pyrimidine dimers (in some organisms) | O⁶-methylguanine, CPDs |
| **Base Excision Repair (BER)** | Single damaged base (deaminated, oxidized, alkylated, uracil) | DNA glycosylases |
| **Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER)** | Bulky helix-distorting lesions (pyrimidine dimers, adducts) | UV light, benzo[a]pyrene |
| **Mismatch Repair (MMR)** | Replication mismatches, small insertion/deletion loops | DNA polymerase slippage |
| **Homologous Recombination (HR)** | Double-strand breaks, stalled replication forks | Ionizing radiation |
| **Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ)** | Double-strand breaks (especially in G1 phase) | V(D)J recombination, IR |
| **SOS Repair (Translesion Synthesis)** | Last-resort, error-prone bypass of lesions | Extensive DNA damage |

---

> [!NOTE]
> This module places special emphasis on **Mismatch Repair (MMR)**, which is examined regularly in KTU papers as a 14-mark descriptive question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind DNA Damage

DNA is a chemically stable molecule, yet it is constantly assaulted. The key **endogenous** sources are:
- **Hydrolytic damage:** Depurination (~10⁴ events/cell/day) and cytosine deamination (~100 events/cell/day).
- **Oxidative damage:** Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) such as ·OH, H₂O₂, and O₂·⁻ generated by mitochondria, producing **8-oxoguanine** lesions.
- **Replicative errors:** Misincorporation by DNA polymerase (~1 error per 10⁵ bases) and polymerase slippage at microsatellites.

The key **exogenous** sources are:
- **UV-B radiation (280–315 nm):** Produces cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs) and 6-4 photoproducts.
- **UV-A (315–400 nm):** Generates ROS indirectly.
- **Ionizing radiation (X-rays, γ-rays):** Causes double-strand breaks (DSBs).
- **Alkylating agents (EMS, MMS):** Add methyl/ethyl groups to bases, generating O⁶-methylguanine.
- **Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs, e.g., benzo[a]pyrene):** Bulky adducts that distort the helix.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Multiple Repair Pathways

Different lesions have different structures, sizes, and effects on the DNA double helix, so a *single* repair mechanism is biologically impossible. The cell therefore maintains a **layered, lesion-specific** defense:

1. **Direct repair** is the fastest (no DNA cutting required).
2. **BER** handles small, non-helix-distorting base damage.
3. **NER** handles bulky, helix-distorting lesions.
4. **MMR** specifically targets *replication errors that escaped polymerase proofreading*.
5. **DSB repair** is the most complex and uses sister chromatids (HR) or direct ligation (NHEJ).

## 2.3 Detailed Mechanism — Direct Repair

### 2.3.1 Photoreactivation (Direct Reversal of Pyrimidine Dimers)
- **Enzyme:** Photolyase (in bacteria, plants, and most eukaryotes — *absent in placental mammals including humans*).
- **Mechanism:** The enzyme binds the cyclobutane pyrimidine dimer (CPD), absorbs a photon of **blue light (~300–500 nm)**, and uses the energy to break the cyclobutane ring, restoring the two original pyrimidines.
- **Key cofactor:** FADH⁻ and a light-harvesting antenna cofactor (methenyltetrahydrofolate in some photolyases).

### 2.3.2 O⁶-Methylguanine-DNA Methyltransferase (MGMT)
- **Damage repaired:** O⁶-methylguanine (a mispairing lesion: O⁶-meG pairs with thymine instead of cytosine, causing GC → AT transition mutations).
- **Mechanism:** MGMT is a "suicide enzyme" — it transfers the methyl group from the O⁶ position of guanine onto a cysteine residue in its own active site (Cys145 in humans), permanently inactivating itself. A new MGMT molecule must be synthesized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** MGMT is *stoichiometric* — one enzyme per one repair event. It is also a key biomarker in glioblastoma therapy; promoter methylation of *MGMT* gene predicts better response to alkylating chemotherapeutics (e.g., temozolomide).

## 2.4 Detailed Mechanism — Base Excision Repair (BER)

Operates on small, non-helix-distorting base lesions (uracil, 8-oxoguanine, 3-methyladenine, hypoxanthine).

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Recognition & base removal** by a **DNA glycosylase** (e.g., Uracil-DNA Glycosylase/UDG, 8-oxoguanine glycosylase/MYH, AAG/MPG).
2. The glycosylase flips the damaged base out of the helix and cleaves the N-glycosidic bond, producing an **abasic (AP) site**.
3. **AP endonuclease (APE1)** cleaves the phosphodiester backbone 5′ to the AP site, generating a nick.
4. **DNA Polymerase β** removes the deoxyribose phosphate residue and inserts the correct nucleotide.
5. **DNA Ligase III / XRCC1** seals the nick.

Two sub-pathways: **Short-patch BER** (1 nucleotide replaced) and **Long-patch BER** (2–10 nucleotides replaced using Pol β/δ/ε, FEN1, and Ligase I).

## 2.5 Detailed Mechanism — Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER)

Operates on **bulky, helix-distorting lesions** (UV-induced CPDs, 6-4 photoproducts, benzo[a]pyrene adducts, cisplatin-DNA adducts).

Two sub-pathways:
- **Global Genome NER (GG-NER):** Scans the entire genome; uses **XPC-RAD23B-CEN2** for damage recognition.
- **Transcription-Coupled NER (TC-NER):** Initiates when RNA Polymerase II stalls at a lesion; uses **CSA, CSB**.

**Core reaction (5 steps):**
1. **Damage recognition** (XPC complex in GG-NER).
2. **DNA unwinding** by **TFIIH** (XPB and XPD helicases).
3. **Dual incision**: XPF-ERCC1 cuts 5′ (15–24 nt upstream) and XPG cuts 3′ (3–9 nt downstream) of the lesion.
4. **Excision** of the oligonucleotide (~24–32 nt) containing the lesion.
5. **Resynthesis** by **DNA Pol δ/ε** and ligation by **Ligase I/XRCC1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical correlation:** Defects in NER genes cause **Xeroderma Pigmentosum (XP)** — extreme UV sensitivity, >1000-fold increased risk of skin cancer.

## 2.6 Detailed Mechanism — Mismatch Repair (MMR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **KTU-favored topic** for 14-mark questions. Mastery of MMR is non-negotiable.

### 2.6.1 Function and Significance
MMR corrects **base-base mismatches** and **small insertion/deletion loops (IDLs)** that escape the 3′→5′ proofreading activity of DNA polymerase during replication. By doing so, MMR improves replication fidelity by **~100- to 1000-fold**, lowering the mutation rate from ~10⁻⁶ to ~10⁻⁹ per base pair per generation.

### 2.6.2 Mismatch Repair in *E. coli* — The Paradigm System

The MutH/MutL/MutS system was the first MMR system to be elucidated and is the model for understanding the process.

**Step 1 — Mismatch recognition:** A homodimer of **MutS** recognizes and binds the mismatched base pair or the IDL, inducing a sharp bend (~45°–60°) in the DNA.

**Step 2 — Recruitment of MutL:** A homodimer of **MutL** binds MutS and acts as a molecular scaffold, connecting MutS to the downstream repair machinery.

**Step 3 — Strand discrimination — the role of methylation:** This is the *most ingenious step*. *E. coli* DNA is methylated at the **N⁶ position of adenine** in **GATC** sequences by **DNA adenine methylase (Dam methylase)**. Newly synthesized DNA is **hemimethylated** (the parental strand is methylated; the daughter strand is not yet methylated). The system therefore *knows which strand is the daughter* and selectively repairs the unmethylated strand.

**Step 4 — Activation of MutH:** MutL recruits **MutH**, an endonuclease that introduces a nick on the **unmethylated (daughter) strand** at a nearby hemimethylated GATC site. GATC sites occur on average every ~256 bp, so a mismatch is rarely more than ~1 kb from a GATC site.

**Step 5 — Excision:** A **helicase (UvrD)** and one of three exonucleases (ExoI, ExoVII, RecJ, or ExoX) degrade the daughter strand from the nick *toward* the mismatch, removing the error. Direction of degradation is dictated by the position of the nick relative to the mismatch.

**Step 6 — Resynthesis:** **DNA Pol III** re-synthesizes the strand using the parental strand as template. **DNA Ligase** seals the nick.

### 2.6.3 Mismatch Repair in Eukaryotes (Humans)

Eukaryotes cannot use methylation for strand discrimination (it is not preserved during replication). Instead, eukaryotes use the **strand discontinuities** in the lagging strand (nicks between Okazaki fragments) and, less clearly, transient nicks in the leading strand, as the signal for strand discrimination.

| Bacterial Protein | Eukaryotic Homolog | Function |
|---|---|---|
| MutS | **MSH2–MSH6** (MutSα) and **MSH2–MSH3** (MutSβ) | Mismatch recognition; MutSα recognizes base-base mismatches and small IDLs; MutSβ recognizes larger IDLs (loop of 1–10 nt) |
| MutL | **MLH1–PMS2** (MutLα), **MLH1–MLH3** (MutLγ), **MLH1–PMS1** (MutLβ) | Mediator; coordinates excision |
| MutH | **None (no homolog)** | Strand-discrimination endonuclease — not present in eukaryotes |
| UvrD (helicase) | **EXO1**, **DNA2**, **RPA**, **RFC**, **PCNA** | Strand excision machinery |
| Dam methylase | **None** (methylation not used) | Strand-discrimination marker |

**Eukaryotic MMR steps:**
1. PCNA (Proliferating Cell Nuclear Antigen) loads **MutSα/MSH2-MSH6** (or MutSβ) onto the DNA at the mismatch.
2. **MutLα (MLH1-PMS2)** is recruited.
3. The complex is activated by **RFC** and the latent endonuclease activity of **PMS2** nicks the *daughter* strand at a discontinuity.
4. **EXO1** (a 5′→3′ exonuclease) or **DNA2** resects the strand from the nick toward the mismatch.
5. **RPA** stabilizes the single-stranded gap; **Pol δ** re-synthesizes; **Ligase I** seals.

### 2.6.4 Clinical Significance of MMR Defects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Clinical Box:** Defects in MMR are responsible for **Lynch syndrome** (Hereditary Non-Polyposis Colorectal Cancer, HNPCC), the most common hereditary colon cancer syndrome. Mutations in *MLH1*, *MSH2*, *MSH6*, *PMS2*, or *EPCAM* cause microsatellite instability (MSI) — the accumulation of length variations in short tandem repeat sequences — and a high lifetime risk of colorectal, endometrial, ovarian, gastric, and urothelial cancers. **Sporadic** MMR defects are common in ~15% of colorectal cancers, ~30% of endometrial cancers, and many gliomas, often due to *MLH1* promoter hypermethylation.

**Muir-Torre syndrome** (a variant of Lynch syndrome with sebaceous skin tumors) and **Constitutional Mismatch Repair Deficiency (CMMR-D)** in biallelic carriers are other clinically important associations.

## 2.7 The Mismatch Recognition Specificity of MutSα vs. MutSβ

| Complex | Recognizes | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| **MutSα** (MSH2 + MSH6) | Base-base mismatches (G:T, A:C, A:G, T:C, G:G) and small (1–2 nt) IDLs | MSH2 + MSH6 |
| **MutSβ** (MSH2 + MSH3) | Larger IDLs (1–10 nt loops), some base mismatches | MSH2 + MSH3 |

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Relationship | Key Constants / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Spontaneous mutation rate (no repair) | $M_0 \approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ per bp/replication | per base pair per generation |
| Mutation rate *with* proofreading alone | $M_{\text{proof}} \approx 10^{-7}$ per bp/generation | per base pair per generation |
| **Final mutation rate (with MMR)** | $M_{\text{final}} \approx 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ per bp/generation | per base pair per generation |
| Combined fidelity | $M_{\text{final}} = M_0 \times F_{\text{proofreading}} \times F_{\text{MMR}}$ | where $F$ = fidelity factor (~10²–10³ each) |
| Frequency of GATC sites in *E. coli* | $1/(\tfrac{1}{4})^4 = 256$ bp | 1 site per ~256 bp |
| Deamination rate (cytosine → uracil) | $\sim 100$ events/cell/day | per diploid human cell |
| Depurination rate | $\sim 10{,}000$ events/cell/day | per diploid human cell |
| MGMT stoichiometry | **1 enzyme = 1 repair** (suicide enzyme) | turnover number = 1 |

## 2.9 Real-World Utility of DNA Repair Knowledge

- **Cancer therapy:** Alkylating chemotherapeutics (temozolomide, carmustine) work by overloading the MGMT and BER systems. Tumors with silenced *MGMT* promoter are more sensitive.
- **Cancer diagnostics:** Microsatellite instability (MSI) testing by PCR is a standard clinical test for Lynch syndrome screening.
- **Cancer predisposition counseling:** Identification of germline MMR mutations enables prophylactic surveillance.
- **Antibiotic development:** SOS response inhibitors are being explored as antibiotic adjuvants.
- **Aging research:** Accumulation of unrepaired DNA damage is a central tenet of the *damage theory of aging*.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 2 Recap:** The cell uses multiple overlapping repair pathways; MMR is the *post-replicative auditor* that catches what polymerase misses, and its dysfunction is the molecular basis of Lynch syndrome.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Contribution of Repair to Overall Mutation Rate

Let $M_0$ be the intrinsic error frequency of DNA polymerase *without any correction mechanisms*, defined as the number of mismatches per base pair per replication. In *E. coli*, $M_0 \approx 10^{-5}$.

Now we apply the *sequential error-reduction* model.

### Step 1 — Apply 3′→5′ proofreading exonuclease activity
The polymerase's intrinsic proofreading improves fidelity by a factor $F_{\text{proof}}$, defined as:

$$F_{\text{proof}} = \frac{\text{Initial error rate}}{\text{Post-proofreading error rate}}$$

Empirically, $F_{\text{proof}} \approx 100$. So the post-proofreading error rate is:

$$M_1 = \frac{M_0}{F_{\text{proof}}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^2} = 10^{-7} \text{ per bp per replication}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Mismatch Repair
MMR provides an additional fidelity factor $F_{\text{MMR}}$. Empirically, $F_{\text{MMR}} \approx 100$ to $1000$. Taking the conservative value:

$$F_{\text{MMR}} = 10^2$$

Then the final observed mutation rate $M_{\text{final}}$ is:

$$M_{\text{final}} = \frac{M_1}{F_{\text{MMR}}} = \frac{10^{-7}}{10^2} = 10^{-9} \text{ per bp per replication}$$

### Step 3 — General combined equation
Combining steps 1 and 2, the net fidelity is multiplicative:

$$M_{\text{final}} = \frac{M_0}{F_{\text{proof}} \times F_{\text{MMR}}}$$

$$M_{\text{final}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^2 \times 10^2} = 10^{-9} \text{ per bp per replication}$$

This 100-million-fold reduction in errors is the *combined contribution* of accurate base selection ($M_0$), proofreading, and mismatch repair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Board:** If MMR is knocked out, the mutation rate increases by $10^2$ to $10^3$-fold, i.e., the mutation rate rises from $\sim 10^{-9}$ to $\sim 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ per bp/generation — the hallmark mutator phenotype of Lynch syndrome tumors.

## 3.2 Derivation — Probability of a Mismatch Being Within Reach of a GATC Site in *E. coli*

In *E. coli*, GATC is recognized by Dam methylase. A mismatch must lie within the reach of MutH-mediated nicking at a hemimethylated GATC. The excision machinery (helicase + exonuclease) can degrade ~1 kb of single-stranded DNA. The probability $P$ that a mismatch is within distance $L$ of a GATC site is:

$$P = 1 - e^{-L / d}$$

where $d$ is the mean spacing between GATC sites. Since the *E. coli* genome is 25% GC (so each base = 25% chance of being A or T, and a 4-bp site occurs with probability $(1/4)^4 = 1/256$):

$$d = 256 \text{ bp}$$

For a degradation reach of $L = 1000$ bp on each side of the mismatch:

$$P = 1 - e^{-1000 / 256} = 1 - e^{-3.91} \approx 1 - 0.020 = 0.98$$

Thus, ~98% of mismatches are within reach of a GATC site — explaining why MMR is so efficient in bacteria.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of Repair Pathways

| Feature | Direct Repair | BER | NER | MMR | HR | NHEJ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Damage type** | Alkylated bases, CPDs | Single damaged base | Bulky helix distortion | Replication mismatches, IDLs | Double-strand breaks | Double-strand breaks |
| **Strand specificity** | Either | Either | Either | Daughter (new) strand | Sister chromatid (S/G2) | Either (error-prone) |
| **Number of nucleotides removed** | 0 | 1 (short-patch) or 2–10 (long-patch) | 24–32 | Up to 1000 (bacteria) | Variable | Variable (0–4 nt homology used) |
| **Key enzyme (bacteria)** | Photolyase, Ada, Ogt | UDG, Fpg, MutM, Pol I | UvrA, UvrB, UvrC | MutS, MutL, MutH | RecA, RecBCD | Ku, LigD (in prokaryotes) |
| **Key protein (humans)** | MGMT | APE1, Pol β, XRCC1 | XPA–XPG, TFIIH, ERCC1 | MSH2, MSH6, MLH1, PMS2 | RAD51, BRCA1/2, MRE11 | Ku70/80, DNA-PKcs, XRCC4, Lig IV |
| **Error-prone?** | No | No | No | No | No (high fidelity) | Yes (loss of 1–4 nt) |
| **Clinical correlation** | MGMT promoter methylation in glioblastoma | MUTYH-associated polyposis | Xeroderma pigmentosum, Cockayne syndrome, TTD | **Lynch syndrome (HNPCC)** | BRCA1/2 breast/ovarian cancer | SCID (Lig IV, Artemis defects) |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Calculating Mutation Burden with MMR Deficiency

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Question:** A human cell undergoes DNA replication. Without any repair, the error rate is $M_0 = 10^{-5}$ per bp. Proofreading reduces it by 100-fold, and a fully functional MMR reduces it by a further 200-fold. Calculate the observed mutation rate. If MMR is knocked out, what is the new mutation rate, and how many new mutations would arise in a 6 × 10⁹ bp diploid genome per replication?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Mutation rate with all systems functional:

$$M_{\text{final}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{100 \times 200} = \frac{10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^4} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ per bp per replication}$$

Step 2 — Number of new mutations per diploid genome:

$$N_{\text{new}} = M_{\text{final}} \times N_{\text{bp}} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \times 6 \times 10^9 = 3 \text{ mutations per replication}$$

Step 3 — MMR knockout. Without MMR, only proofreading remains:

$$M_{\text{MMR-/-}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{100} = 10^{-7} \text{ per bp per replication}$$

Step 4 — Number of mutations per replication in MMR-deficient cell:

$$N_{\text{MMR-/-}} = 10^{-7} \times 6 \times 10^9 = 600 \text{ mutations per replication}$$

Step 5 — Increase factor:

$$\text{Fold increase} = \frac{N_{\text{MMR-/-}}}{N_{\text{new}}} = \frac{600}{3} = 200 \text{-fold}$$

This 200-fold increase in the per-replication mutation burden is precisely the *mutator phenotype* that drives tumorigenesis in Lynch syndrome cells.

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation is a **favourite KTU problem** (Part A or Part B sub-question). Memorize the methodology.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step — Recognition of Mismatches by MutSα (MSH2-MSH6)

The molecular events of mismatch recognition are:

1. **ATP binding by MSH2** (Walker A motif, P-loop) — keeps the complex in an "open," DNA-scanning state.
2. **Mismatch-induced DNA bending** — MutSα bends the DNA ~45° at the mismatch, flipping the mismatched base into a Phe-X-Glu recognition pocket of MSH6.
3. **Mismatch-specific contacts:** MSH6's Phe432 stacks with the mismatched base; the GLU339 residue forms a hydrogen bond with the mismatched base; the neighboring correctly paired base is recognized by the "DX" motif.
4. **ADP → ATP exchange** in MSH3 (or MSH6) — converts the recognition complex into a sliding clamp that can move along DNA.
5. **Recruitment of MutLα** to the MutSα-DNA complex.
6. **Activation of PMS2 endonuclease** by PCNA + RFC, creating a nick on the daughter strand.
7. **EXO1-mediated strand excision** of the region containing the mismatch.
8. **Resynthesis by Pol δ** and **ligation by Ligase I.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Students often confuse which MutS complex recognizes which lesion. Mnemonic: **MSH6 = Six → Single-base mispairs; MSH3 = Three → Three-or-more nucleotide loops.**

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 3 Recap:** The mutation rate is the product of multiple fidelity factors; MMR contributes a ~100- to 1000-fold increase in replication accuracy, and its loss creates a mutator phenotype central to Lynch syndrome.
