# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

> **Q: What is the Exon Junction Complex (EJC)? Briefly describe its role in mRNA nuclear export and NMD.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – 90 words target):**

The EJC is a multiprotein complex deposited by the spliceosome **~20–24 nt upstream** of every exon-exon junction on a spliced mRNA. Its core consists of **eIF4A3, Y14, MAGOH, and MLN51** in a **1:1:1:1 stoichiometry**, where eIF4A3 acts as the central RNA-binding ATPase.

**Roles:**
- **(1) NMD licensing:** EJCs mark splice sites; a stop codon lying **≥ 50–55 nt upstream** of a downstream EJC is recognized as a **PTC**, recruiting **UPF1/UPF2/UPF3** to trigger NMD.
- **(2) Export facilitation:** EJCs recruit **ALY/REF and UAP56**, which hand off the mRNP to **TAP-p15** for NPC translocation.
- **(3) mRNA stability:** EJCs protect mRNA from premature nuclear decay and ensure only fully spliced transcripts are exported.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

> **Q: Differentiate between Ran-dependent and Ran-independent mRNA export pathways. Give one example receptor for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Ran-Dependent Export | Ran-Independent Export |
|---|---|---|
| **GTP cofactor** | Requires **Ran-GTP** gradient (nucleus:cytoplasm ~500:1) | **No Ran** requirement |
| **Mechanism** | Receptor binds cargo + Ran-GTP in nucleus; complex dissociates in cytoplasm upon GTP hydrolysis | Receptor binds cargo directly via RRM / LRR domains |
| **Energy source** | Ran-GTP hydrolysis | **ATP** hydrolysis (Dbp5, UAP56) |
| **Example receptor** | **CRM1 (Exportin1 / Xpo1)** — exports snRNA, NES-bearing proteins | **TAP (NXF1) – p15 (NXT1)** — exports bulk mRNA |
| **Cargo examples** | snRNA, snoRNA, some rRNAs, NES-tagged proteins | Processed mRNA, certain viral RNAs (e.g., HIV-1 CTE) |
| **Directionality** | Provided by Ran-GTP gradient | Provided by Dbp5 ATPase at cytoplasmic NPC face |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> **Q: Describe in detail the mechanism of mRNA nuclear export from the nucleus to the cytoplasm in eukaryotes. Discuss the role of the THO/TREX complex, UAP56, ALY/REF, and the TAP-p15 receptor in the export process. Add a note on the directionality of transport.**

#### (a) Co-transcriptional mRNP assembly and processing (7 Marks)

1. **Transcription by RNA Pol II:** Initiates in the nucleus. The C-terminal domain (CTD) heptad repeats (YSPTSPS) are phosphorylated at Ser5 near the 5′ end and Ser2 toward the 3′ end. This CTD acts as a binding platform for RNA processing factors.

2. **5′ Capping (Cotranscriptional):** Occurs when transcript is ~25 nt long. **RNGTT (guanylyltransferase)** adds GMP to the 5′-diphosphate end via a 5′–5′ triphosphate bridge, forming GpppN. **RNMT (methyltransferase)** then methylates the N-7 position of guanine to produce the **m⁷G cap (m⁷GpppN)**. The cap is recognized by the **Cap-Binding Complex (CBP20/CBP80)**.

3. **Splicing (Cotranscriptional):** The **spliceosome** (U1, U2, U4/U6.U5 tri-snRNPs and ~100 auxiliary proteins) removes introns. The **EJC** is deposited ~20–24 nt upstream of each exon-exon junction.

4. **3′ End Processing (Cotranscriptional):**
   - **CPSF** (CPSF-160, CPSF-100, CPSF-73, CPSF-30, Fip1) recognizes the poly-A signal (AAUAAA).
   - **CstF** binds the downstream GU-rich element.
   - **CPSF-73** endonuclease cleaves ~15–30 nt downstream of AAUAAA.
   - **PAP (Poly-A Polymerase)** adds ~200–250 adenosines.
   - **PABPN1** controls tail length and promotes nuclear export.

5. **THO/TREX Complex Recruitment:** As transcription proceeds, the **THO subcomplex (THOC1–THOC7)** is loaded onto the mRNA. THO recruits the **TREX (TRanscription-EXport) complex** containing **UAP56, ALY/REF, THOC5, THOC7**.

6. **UAP56 (DDX39B) Action:** A **DExD/H-box ATPase** that remodels mRNP secondary structure, displaces hnRNP proteins, and transfers the mRNA to **ALY/REF**.

#### (b) Export through NPC and directionality (7 Marks)

7. **ALY/REF (THOC4) as Adaptor:** ALY/REF directly binds **TAP (NXF1)** and hands the mRNP over.

8. **TAP-p15 (NXF1-NXT1) Loading:** TAP has an **NTF2-like domain** (binds FG-Nups), an **RRM** (binds RNA), a **LRR** (binds p15), and a **UBA-like** domain. p15 binding enhances FG-Nup affinity ~10-fold.

9. **Docking at NPC:** TAP-p15 engages FG-repeat nucleoporins (**Nup62, Nup98, Nup153, Nup214, Nup358**) and threads the mRNP through the central channel.

10. **Cytoplasmic Release — The Dbp5 Engine:**
    - **Dbp5 (DDX19)** ATPase is anchored at cytoplasmic NPC fibrils by **Nup214** and activated by **Gle1 + IP₆ (inositol hexaphosphate)**.
    - Dbp5 uses ATP hydrolysis to **strip off export factors (TAP, p15, ALY/REF)** from the mRNA.
    - This remodeling is the **irreversible step** that ensures directionality.

11. **Directionality Principle:**
    - The mRNP carries a **nuclear "identity"** (TAP-p15 bound) that is recognized only by nuclear FG-Nups.
    - Once Dbp5 removes these factors in the cytoplasm, the mRNA **cannot re-enter** the nucleus.
    - ATP hydrolysis by Dbp5 provides the **free energy** for this kinetic trap:

$$\Delta G_{\text{cyto release}} = -RT \ln K_{\text{eq}} = -n \cdot RT \ln \frac{[\text{ADP}][\text{Pi}]}{[\text{ATP}]}$$

    - For $n = 1$ ATP molecule hydrolyzed at standard cellular concentrations, $\Delta G \approx -50 \text{ kJ/mol}$, more than enough to drive the mRNP into the cytoplasmic translation pool.

12. **Summary Equation of Directionality:**

$$\text{mRNP}_{\text{nucl}} \xrightarrow{\text{TAP-p15, FG-Nups, NPC}} \text{mRNP}_{\text{cyto}} \xrightarrow{\text{Dbp5, Gle1, IP}_6, \text{ATP}} \text{mRNA}_{\text{translation-competent}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 – Alternative Choice]`

> **Q: Explain the molecular mechanisms of mRNA coherence control in eukaryotes. Discuss the Nonsense-Mediated Decay (NMD), Non-Stop Decay (NSD), and No-Go Decay (NGD) pathways with their protein factors and trigger conditions.**

#### (a) Nonsense-Mediated Decay (NMD) (7 Marks)

1. **Definition:** NMD selectively degrades mRNAs containing a **premature termination codon (PTC)**, thereby preventing the synthesis of truncated, often dominant-negative, proteins.

2. **The 50–55 Nucleotide Rule:**
   - EJCs are deposited by the spliceosome ~20–24 nt upstream of exon-exon junctions.
   - A terminating ribosome protects ~30 nt of mRNA downstream of the stop codon.
   - A PTC is recognized when there is a **downstream EJC within ~50–55 nt** of the stop codon:

$$d_{\text{PTC to next EJC}} \geq 50\text{--}55 \text{ nt} \Rightarrow \text{NMD ON}$$

   - A normal stop codon in the final exon (no downstream EJC) escapes NMD.

3. **Core NMD Protein Factors:**
   - **UPF1 (SMG2):** RNA helicase, central effector.
   - **UPF2 (SMG3):** Bridges UPF1 to UPF3.
   - **UPF3 (UPF3A/B):** EJC-binding factor.
   - **SMG1:** PI3K-related kinase that phosphorylates UPF1.
   - **SMG5, SMG6, SMG7:** Recruit decay enzymes.
   - **SMG8, SMG9:** SMG1 regulatory subunits.

4. **Mechanistic Steps of NMD:**
   - **Step 1:** PTC recognized by terminating ribosome (eRF1-eRF3 complex releases nascent chain).
   - **Step 2:** UPF3B on the downstream EJC recruits UPF2.
   - **Step 3:** UPF2 binds and activates UPF1 (UPF1 is loaded onto the mRNA by eRF3).
   - **Step 4:** SMG1 (with SMG8/SMG9) phosphorylates UPF1 → UPF1-P.
   - **Step 5:** UPF1-P recruits **SMG6** (an endonuclease that cleaves the mRNA near the PTC) and **SMG5-SMG7** (which recruit the **CCR4–NOT** deadenylase complex and **DCP1/DCP2** decapping enzyme).
   - **Step 6:** mRNA is degraded:
     - 5′→3′ decay by **XRN1**
     - 3′→5′ decay by the **exosome (EXOSC1–9 + EXOSC10/Rrp6)**
   - **Step 7:** **PP2A** dephosphorylates UPF1, allowing recycling.

5. **NMD triggers beyond classical EJCs:**
   - **Long 3′ UTRs** (>1 kb) can trigger NMD even without EJCs, via UPF1/UPF2/UPF3 binding.
   - **uORF (upstream ORF)** termination can trigger NMD.
   - **Programmed frameshifts** in viral RNAs are also NMD-sensitive.

#### (b) Non-Stop Decay (NSD) and No-Go Decay (NGD) (7 Marks)

6. **Non-Stop Decay (NSD):**
   - **Trigger:** mRNA **lacks an in-frame stop codon** (due to premature polyadenylation, endonucleolytic cleavage, or readthrough of the natural stop).
   - **Mechanism:**
     - The ribosome reads through to the poly-A tail.
     - **PABP** (bound to the poly-A tail) is encountered in the ribosomal A-site, which stalls the ribosome.
     - In yeast, the **Ski complex (Ski2-Ski3-Ski8)** plus the non-canonical release factor **Ski7** recognize the stalled ribosome.
     - Ski7 recruits the **cytoplasmic exosome (Exo9 + Exo10)** for 3′→5′ decay.
     - The residual 5′ fragment is degraded by **Xrn1**.
   - **Mammalian NSD:** Functional homologs of Ski7 (e.g., **HBS1, ETF1/eRF1** family) operate; the exosome is the central effector.

7. **No-Go Decay (NGD):**
   - **Trigger:** Ribosome stalls on **damaged bases, strong secondary structures, rare codons, or polylysine-coding sequences**.
   - **Mechanism:**
     - Stalled ribosome is recognized by **Dom34 (Pelota in mammals)** — a release factor homolog.
     - **Hbs1** (eRF3 homolog) binds Dom34, providing GTPase activity.
     - Dom34-Hbs1 splits the ribosome into subunits.
     - An endonuclease (recently identified as **Cue2 in yeast**) cleaves the mRNA at the stall site.
     - **5′ fragment → Xrn1** (5′→3′ decay).
     - **3′ fragment → exosome** (3′→5′ decay).

8. **Integrated Surveillance — P-bodies and Stress Granules:**
   - **P-bodies (Processing bodies):** Cytoplasmic foci containing DCP1/DCP2, XRN1, GW182, AGO proteins. Sites of mRNA decapping and 5′→3′ decay; also store translationally repressed mRNAs.
   - **Stress Granules:** Contain stalled 48S pre-initiation complexes, eIF4G, eIF2α-P, G3BP1. Form under stress to triage mRNAs between translation, storage, and decay.

9. **Comparison Summary Table:**

| Pathway | Trigger | Sensor | Decay Direction | Key Proteins |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NMD** | PTC + downstream EJC | UPF1/UPF2/UPF3 | 5′→3′ (XRN1) + 3′→5′ (exosome) | UPF1–3, SMG1–9 |
| **NSD** | No stop codon | Ski7 + PABP | 3′→5′ (exosome) + 5′→3′ (Xrn1) | Ski2, Ski3, Ski7, Ski8, exosome |
| **NGD** | Ribosome stall | Dom34-Hbs1 | Endonucleolytic → both directions | Dom34, Hbs1, Cue2, Xrn1, exosome |

10. **Clinical / Biotechnological Relevance:**
    - NMD is implicated in **β-thalassemia (HBB mutations)**, **Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD gene nonsense mutations)**, **Marfan syndrome (FBN1)**, and many cancers (e.g., BRCA1/2).
    - **NMD inhibitors (e.g., SMG1i, amlexanox)** are being explored as therapeutic strategies to boost expression of full-length proteins from PTC-containing transcripts.
    - Modulation of NMD affects **antigen presentation** in cancer immunotherapy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert (Module 4, Q B):**
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Students often write *NMD = NSD = NGD* without distinguishing their triggers. Examiners dock **2 marks** for this confusion. **Always state the trigger first.**
> 2. **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the **role of the EJC** in NMD — at least **1 mark** is reserved for the 50–55 nt rule derivation.
> 3. **Common Mistake:** Confusing **eRF1/eRF3** (normal termination) with **Dom34/Hbs1** (NGD) and **Ski7** (NSD). The molecular mimicry of these release factors is a favorite examiner question.
> 4. **Common Mistake:** Skipping the **PP2A dephosphorylation step** of UPF1 — examiners often include a "complete the cycle" sub-question worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Diagram Penalty:** Students who write only text without a labeled pathway diagram lose up to **2 marks** under KTU's visual presentation norms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — mRNA Nuclear Export & Coherence Control**

### A. mRNA Export — Must-Know Facts
- [ ] mRNA export is **co-transcriptional** and **co-processing**.
- [ ] **Cap, splicing, poly-A tail** = export license.
- [ ] **THO/TREX** complex: THOC1–7, UAP56, ALY/REF.
- [ ] **UAP56 (DDX39B)** is the DExD/H helicase that remodels mRNP.
- [ ] **ALY/REF (THOC4)** is the adaptor to TAP.
- [ ] **TAP (NXF1) – p15 (NXT1)** is the major mRNA export receptor (yeast: **Mex67–Mtr2**).
- [ ] Export is **largely Ran-independent** (unlike CRM1-mediated snRNA/protein export).
- [ ] **Dbp5 (DDX19)** + **Gle1** + **IP₆** at the cytoplasmic NPC face provides **directionality** via ATP hydrolysis.
- [ ] **FG-Nups** (Nup62, Nup98, Nup153, Nup214, Nup358) form the selective hydrogel barrier.
- [ ] NPC central channel: **~9 nm passive / ~39 nm active**.

### B. mRNA Coherence Control — Must-Know Facts
- [ ] **NMD** = PTC recognition; **NSD** = no stop codon; **NGD** = ribosome stall.
- [ ] **EJC** core: **eIF4A3 : Y14 : MAGOH : MLN51** at **1:1:1:1** ratio, deposited **~20–24 nt upstream** of exon-exon junction.
- [ ] **50–55 nt rule:** A PTC is recognized if a downstream EJC is **≥ 50–55 nt** away.
- [ ] **NMD core proteins:** UPF1, UPF2, UPF3 (named from yeast Up-Frameshift mutants).
- [ ] **SMG1** phosphorylates UPF1; **PP2A** dephosphorylates it.
- [ ] **SMG6** is the endonuclease that cleaves the mRNA near the PTC.
- [ ] **Ski complex (Ski2-Ski3-Ski8) + Ski7** drive NSD via exosome recruitment.
- [ ] **Dom34 (Pelota) + Hbs1** drive NGD by splitting stalled ribosomes.
- [ ] **P-bodies** = sites of 5′→3′ decay and mRNA storage; contain DCP1/DCP2, XRN1, GW182.
- [ ] **Stress granules** = stalled 48S pre-initiation complexes under stress.

### C. Cross-Cutting Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] **Directionality** in mRNA export comes from **Dbp5 ATPase**, not from the Ran-GTP gradient (which is reserved for protein/snRNA export).
- [ ] **Quality control (NMD/NSD/NGD) and translation are physically linked** — the ribosome itself is the sensor in all three pathways.
- [ ] **Evolutionary conservation:** All three surveillance pathways are found in **yeast, plants, and mammals**, though with some component variations.
- [ ] **Therapeutic relevance:** ~11% of all known human genetic diseases are caused by PTCs; NMD modulation is an active drug-discovery area.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for Export Order:** **"C**ap, **S**plice, **P**oly-A" → "**T**REX, **U**AP56, **A**LY, **T**AP" → "**D**bp5 release."

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When asked to "describe" a pathway, follow this order — **(i) Trigger, (ii) Sensor proteins, (iii) Mechanism (with a labeled diagram), (iv) Outcome (decay product)**, and **(v) Biological significance.** Examiners give full marks only when all five elements are present.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RNA Interference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RNA Interference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RNA Interference (RNAi)** is a conserved, evolutionarily ancient eukaryotic post-transcriptional gene-silencing mechanism in which double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) molecules trigger the sequence-specific degradation of homologous messenger RNA (mRNA), thereby inhibiting translation and silencing gene expression.

The phenomenon was first conclusively demonstrated by **Andrew Fire and Craig C. Mello (1998)** in *Caenorhabditis elegans*, an achievement that earned them the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2006**. RNAi operates through two principal endogenous and exogenous small RNA effectors:

- **siRNA (Small Interfering RNA):** 21–23 nucleotide (nt) duplexes derived from perfectly base-paired exogenous dsRNA (viral, transposon, or experimentally introduced). Triggers catalytic cleavage of the target mRNA.
- **miRNA (microRNA):** 20–24 nt single-stranded RNAs derived from endogenous, imperfectly paired hairpin precursors (pre-miRNA). Typically mediate translational repression and/or mRNA destabilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students must distinguish between the **siRNA pathway** (perfect complementarity → cleavage) and the **miRNA pathway** (imperfect complementarity → translational repression), and explain the role of **Dicer**, **RISC**, and **Argonaute** proteins in silencing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Snipper & Search-Destroy Drone"

Imagine a heavily guarded library (the cell) where a single forbidden book (a specific mRNA) is producing dangerous instructions (a toxic or unwanted protein). The cell cannot simply destroy the book because it is needed transiently for other purposes, and it cannot destroy the entire library section.

The solution is to deploy a **search-and-destroy micro-drone**:
1. The drone is manufactured from the *exact same page sequence* as the forbidden book (this is the **dsRNA trigger**).
2. The drone is then **cut and pre-loaded** by a precision scissors machine (**Dicer** + **TRBP/PACT**).
3. The pre-loaded drone is delivered to a guided missile system (**RISC** with **Argonaute 2**), which discards one half (the passenger strand) and keeps the guide strand.
4. The missile **scans every mRNA in the cytoplasm** and **cleaves only the one whose sequence matches the guide** (slicer activity of Ago2).

This is RNAi in plain language — a programmable, sequence-specific, post-transcriptional silencer.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Benchmarks (Examinable Constants)

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Typical siRNA length | **21–23 nt** | Length required for optimal RISC loading |
| Typical miRNA length | **20–24 nt** | Endogenous regulatory size |
| Dicer cleavage cycle | 1 dsRNA $\rightarrow$ many siRNAs | **Amplification principle** |
| ATP requirement | Yes (for Dicer & helicase activity) | Energy-coupled unwinding |
| Minimal dsRNA trigger | **≥ 30 bp** (in mammals) | Below this → IFN response avoided |
| RNAi discovery | **1998** (Fire & Mello) | Nobel 2006 |

> [!TIP]
> In **mammalian cells** long dsRNA ($>$ 30 bp) activates the **interferon (IFN) response** and triggers **PKR-mediated translational shutdown**. This is why synthetic **21–23 nt siRNA duplexes** are used in mammalian RNAi experiments — short enough to bypass IFN, long enough to trigger RISC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Map

## 2.1 The Central Dogma Disruption — Why RNAi Matters

The classical **Central Dogma of Molecular Biology** (DNA $\rightarrow$ mRNA $\rightarrow$ Protein) describes a one-way information flow. RNAi adds a **regulatory retrograde arrow** from mRNA back to itself, providing the cell with a tunable rheostat for gene expression. This explains why $\sim$**30 % of all human genes** are estimated to be regulated by miRNAs alone, and why a single miRNA can target **hundreds** of mRNAs (pleiotropic regulation).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Mechanistic Logic (siRNA Pathway)

The siRNA pathway proceeds through five canonical steps:

1. **Trigger Introduction / Generation** — Exogenous dsRNA (viral replication intermediate, transposon transcript, experimentally transfected dsRNA, or hairpin construct) enters the cytoplasm.

2. **Dicer Processing** — The RNase III endonuclease **Dicer** (in humans: a $\sim$**200 kDa** multidomain protein) cleaves the dsRNA into **21–23 nt** duplexes with **2-nt 3′ overhangs** and a **5′-phosphate**. Domains used: DEAD-box helicase, PAZ (binds 3′ overhang), two RNase III catalytic centers, and dsRBD.

3. **RISC Loading** — The siRNA duplex is loaded onto the **RISC Loading Complex (RLC)**, composed of Dicer, **TRBP** (HIV-1 TAR RNA-binding protein) and **Argonaute 2 (Ago2)**. This is **ATP-dependent**.

4. **Strand Selection & Unwinding** — The **passenger (sense) strand** is discarded. Selection is governed by the **5′-end rule** — the strand whose 5′ end is in the less thermodynamically stable (A/U-rich) end of the duplex is retained as the **guide strand**.

5. **Target Recognition & Catalytic Cleavage** — The mature **RISC** (Argonaute + guide strand) scans cytoplasmic mRNAs via Watson-Crick base pairing. Ago2's **MID + PIWI domain** forms the **slicer catalytic center** (Asp-Asp-His tetrad). Perfect or near-perfect complementarity → endonucleolytic cleavage between nucleotides **10 and 11** of the guide, measured from the 5′ end.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Mechanistic Logic (miRNA Pathway)

The miRNA pathway differs principally in **biogenesis origin** and **pairing tolerance**:

1. **Transcription** — Primary miRNA (**pri-miRNA**) transcribed by **RNA Polymerase II** from intronic or intergenic loci; capped, polyadenylated.
2. **Nuclear Processing** — The **Microprocessor complex** (Drosha + DGCR8) cleaves pri-miRNA $\rightarrow$ **pre-miRNA** ($\sim$70 nt hairpin with 2-nt 3′ overhang).
3. **Cytoplasmic Export** — **Exportin-5 / Ran-GTP** translocates pre-miRNA to the cytoplasm.
4. **Dicer Cleavage** — Dicer (with **TRBP**) trims pre-miRNA $\rightarrow$ **miRNA duplex** ($\sim$22 nt).
5. **RISC Loading & Target Repression** — Loaded onto **Ago1, Ago2, Ago3, or Ago4**. Unlike siRNA, miRNAs tolerate **bulges and mismatches**, especially in the central region. Binding to 3′ UTR of target mRNA via the **seed region (nt 2–8 of the miRNA)** recruits **GW182 (TNRC6)**, which recruits **CCR4-NOT** and **DEAD-box helicase DDX6** to deadenylate and repress translation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SiRNA vs miRNA — The Examiner's Favourite Comparison**

| Feature | siRNA | miRNA |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Exogenous / long dsRNA / transposon | Endogenous genomic loci |
| Precursor | Perfect dsRNA | Imperfect hairpin (pre-miRNA) |
| Biogenesis enzyme | Dicer only | Drosha + Dicer |
| Length | 21–23 nt | 20–24 nt |
| Complementarity to target | Perfect / near-perfect | Imperfect (seed match essential) |
| Mode of action | Endonucleolytic cleavage (Ago2 slicer) | Translational repression + deadenylation |
| Number of targets | One specific mRNA | Multiple mRNAs (pleiotropic) |
| Amplification | None (mammals) / RdRP (plants, *C. elegans*) | Tuned by expression level |
| Physiological role | Antiviral defense, transposon silencing | Developmental timing, differentiation, metabolism |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Constants (LaTeX-Clean)

> [!NOTE]
> Biology does not have "formulas" in the engineering sense, but the following are the **key quantitative relationships** examiners love to test.

$$
N_{\text{siRNA}} \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{L_{\text{dsRNA}} - 2}{21} \right\rfloor
$$

where $L_{\text{dsRNA}}$ is the length of the precursor dsRNA. This gives the number of siRNA products per dsRNA substrate (e.g., a 500 bp dsRNA yields $\sim$**23 siRNAs**).

$$
\Delta G_{\text{unwinding}} \;=\; RT \ln K_{\text{eq}} \quad ; \quad K_{\text{eq}} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{[\text{GC}]_{\text{5'-end}}}
$$

The passenger strand is discarded because its 5′ end has a higher GC content (thermodynamically more stable). The strand with the **A/U-rich 5′ end** is preferentially retained as the guide.

$$
\text{Seed match} : \text{miRNA nt } 2 \text{ to } 8 \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; 3' \text{ UTR of target mRNA}
$$

The **seed region** is the **non-negotiable** determinant of miRNA target recognition.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility

- **Functional Genomics:** Genome-wide **siRNA / shRNA libraries** allow systematic knock-down of every gene in the human genome to phenotype loss-of-function.
- **Therapeutics:** FDA-approved **Patisiran (Onpattro®, 2018)** — the first RNAi therapeutic, treats hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis (hATTR) by silencing the *TTR* gene in the liver using a lipid-nanoparticle (LNP) delivery system. Followed by **Givosiran, Lumasiran, Inclisiran**.
- **Agriculture:** **Host-Induced Gene Silencing (HIGS)** — transgenic plants expressing hairpin RNAs targeting pest or pathogen genes confer resistance (e.g., virus-resistant papaya, *Plum pox virus*-resistant plum).
- **CRISPR Adjunct:** RNAi provides **transient, reversible, dosage-tunable** knock-down — complementing CRISPR's permanent knock-out.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Detailed Mechanistic Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** In Part B questions, students are expected to draw a **labelled flow diagram** of the siRNA or miRNA pathway and **write at least 4–5 sentences per step**. Skipping arrows or omitting enzyme names attracts heavy mark deductions.

## 3.1 Exhaustive Mechanism — siRNA Pathway (Mammalian)

### Step 1 — Entry of the Trigger

Double-stranded RNA enters the cytoplasm via:
- **Viral infection** (replicative intermediates of RNA viruses).
- **Experimental transfection** (lipofection, electroporation, or LNP delivery of synthetic 21–23 nt siRNA duplexes).
- **Transgenic expression** of short hairpin RNA (shRNA) from a Pol III (H1 or U6) promoter.
- **Transposon transcription** yielding bidirectional transcripts that anneal.

> Length rule (mammals): dsRNA $\geq$ 30 bp $\rightarrow$ **dsRNA-activated protein kinase (PKR)** $\rightarrow$ eIF2α phosphorylation $\rightarrow$ global translation shutdown + IFN response. To bypass, use **21–23 nt siRNA** or longer hairpins processed by Dicer.

### Step 2 — Dicer Recognition and Cleavage

$$
\text{dsRNA} \;\xrightarrow[\text{ATP}]{\text{Dicer}} \;\text{siRNA duplex (21–23 nt, 2-nt 3' overhang, 5'-P)}
$$

- **Domain architecture of human Dicer 1** (219 kDa, 1925 aa):
  - **DEAD-box helicase** (ATP-driven translocation along dsRNA).
  - **DUF283** (unknown function, mediates partner binding).
  - **PAZ domain** — anchors the 3′ overhang of the dsRNA and acts as a molecular ruler, positioning the active site exactly **21–23 nt** from the terminus.
  - **Two RNase IIIa and IIIb domains** — intramolecular dimer forming the catalytic core.
  - **dsRBD** (double-stranded RNA binding domain) — non-sequence-specific dsRNA binding.

- The two RNase III active sites each cleave one strand, generating the characteristic **2-nt 3′ hydroxyl overhangs** and **5′-phosphate / 3′-OH termini** (a hallmark of RNase III enzymes).

### Step 3 — RISC Loading

$$
\text{siRNA duplex} \;+\; \text{Dicer} \;+\; \text{TRBP} \;+\; \text{Ago2} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RLC (RISC Loading Complex)}
$$

The **RLC** assembles the siRNA onto Argonaute. **TRBP** stabilises the complex and senses dsRNA end geometry. **Hsc70/Hsp90** chaperones assist in opening Ago2's central pocket (the "open-lid" conformation).

### Step 4 — Strand Asymmetry and Unwinding

- The duplex is unwound by the **Mid domain of Argonaute** acting in concert with a yet-debated helicase (DDX5/DDX17 or Hsc70).
- **Strand selection rule** — thermodynamic asymmetry: the strand whose **5′ end is in the A/U-rich (less stable) half** of the duplex becomes the **guide**. The other strand (passenger) is ejected.
- If the passenger strand is fully complementary to the guide, **Ago2's PIWI domain** cleaves it between nt 10 and 11 (the same "slicer" reaction as on a target mRNA). The cleaved passenger is then degraded by **C3PO** (component 3 promoter of RISC, an endoribonuclease) and the **exosome**.

### Step 5 — Target Recognition and Slicer Activity

The mature **RISC** — minimally **Ago2 + guide siRNA** — scans cytoplasmic mRNAs. The **PAZ domain** of Ago2 anchors the 3′ end of the guide; the **MID domain** anchors the 5′ phosphate; the **PIWI domain** houses the catalytic Asp-Asp-His tetrad coordinated to **Mg²⁺**.

$$
\text{PIWI}_{\text{Ago2}} \;:\; \text{D760, D845, H807 (human Ago2)} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Mg}^{2+} \text{-dependent catalysis}
$$

When Watson-Crick base pairing extends across the guide (full or near-full), Ago2 cleaves the target mRNA between nt **10 and 11** (counted from the 5′ of the guide). The cleaved fragments are then degraded by:

$$
\text{5' fragment} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Xrn1 (5'→3' exonuclease)}}\; \text{degraded}
$$

$$
\text{3' fragment} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Exosome (3'→5' exosome complex)}}\; \text{degraded}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Mechanism — miRNA Pathway

### Step 1 — pri-miRNA Transcription

RNA Pol II produces a capped, polyadenylated **pri-miRNA** ($\sim$ 80 nt hairpin plus flanking single-stranded segments) from intronic (mirtrons in some cases) or intergenic loci.

### Step 2 — Nuclear Microprocessor Activity

$$
\text{pri-miRNA} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Drosha + DGCR8}}\; \text{pre-miRNA} \;\;(\sim 70 \text{ nt hairpin, 2-nt 3' overhang})
$$

- **Drosha** — a **RNase III** endonuclease, partnered with **DGCR8** (DiGeorge Critical Region 8, also called Pasha) which recognizes the ssRNA–dsRNA junction (N6-methyladenine-modified pri-miRNAs are recognized via the **mhDAZ** domain of DGCR8).
- Result: **pre-miRNA** with the canonical 2-nt 3′ overhang, the "licence" for export.

### Step 3 — Nuclear Export

$$
\text{pre-miRNA} \;+\; \text{Exportin-5} \;+\; \text{Ran-GTP} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{cytoplasm}
$$

Exportin-5 recognises the **2-nt 3′ overhang + stem length ≥ 16 bp**; GTP hydrolysis releases pre-miRNA in the cytoplasm.

### Step 4 — Cytoplasmic Dicer Processing

Same as siRNA — Dicer (with TRBP) trims the pre-miRNA loop $\rightarrow$ $\sim$ 22 nt miRNA duplex.

### Step 5 — RISC Loading and Target Repression

- Loaded onto **Ago1, Ago2, Ago3, or Ago4**. **Ago2 is the only catalytically active slicer in humans**; the others function as translational repressors.
- **miRNA binds 3′ UTR** of target mRNA. Critical binding: **seed region (nt 2–8)**. Perfect seed match $\pm$ auxiliary complementarity (nt 13–16) gives the highest affinity.
- Recruitment of **GW182 (TNRC6A/B/C)** — scaffolds the repression machinery:
  - **GW182 $\rightarrow$ PAN2–PAN3 deadenylase complex** (initial deadenylation).
  - **GW182 $\rightarrow$ CCR4–NOT complex** (further deadenylation $\rightarrow$ translational repression).
  - **GW182 $\rightarrow$ DDX6** $\rightarrow$ P-body sequestration; translational repression.
  - **GW182 $\rightarrow$ eIF4A sequestration**, blocking 43S pre-initiation complex assembly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Many textbooks incorrectly state that miRNAs *never* cause cleavage. In mammals, if an miRNA has **near-perfect complementarity** to a target (rare, but engineered miRNA-mimics can do this), **Ago2 slicer activity can be triggered**. Conversely, in plants, most miRNAs are perfectly complementary and *do* trigger slicing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Distinguishing Pathways in the Exam

| Checkpoint | siRNA | miRNA |
|---|---|---|
| Precursor symmetry | Symmetric (perfect) dsRNA | Asymmetric hairpin |
| Initial enzyme | Dicer | Drosha $\rightarrow$ Dicer |
| Exportin used | None (cytoplasmic) | **Exportin-5** |
| Number of targets per RNA | One | Hundreds |
| Cleavage site on target | Between nt 10–11 of guide | Variable, only if perfectly matched |
| Amplification in plants | **RDR6 + SGS3** $\rightarrow$ secondary siRNA (transitivity) | None |
| Functional homolog in *C. elegans* | **Eri-1**, **Rrf-1** (RdRPs) | Egl, Lsy miRNAs |
| Therapeutic flagship | **Patisiran (TTR)** | **CDR132L, MRG-229** (in trials) |

## 3.4 Off-Target Effects and Saturation — A Critical Concern

Two clinical/experimental pitfalls must be stated for full marks:

1. **Off-target silencing** — siRNA guide strands can function like miRNAs and repress unintended mRNAs sharing partial seed complementarity. Mitigation: **BLAST the guide against the transcriptome**; use **seed-matched 8-mers** as exclusion criteria.
2. **RISC saturation** — Excess transfected siRNA or shRNA out-competes endogenous miRNAs for Ago loading, perturbing natural miRNA networks and producing **hepatotoxicity** (this halted several first-generation shRNA clinical trials). Mitigation: dose titration; use **modified nucleotides (2′-O-Me, 2′-F)** to enhance stability without saturation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are engineered to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 syntax safeguards: every node ID is alphanumeric, no reserved keywords are used as node names, and all node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without internal formatting tags.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — siRNA Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["EXOGENOUS OR VIRAL DOUBLE STRANDED RNA ENTERS CYTOPLASM"]
    A2["DICER BINDS DSRNA TERMINUS VIA PAZ DOMAIN"]
    A3["DICER RNASE III DOMAINS CLEAVE INTO 21 TO 23 NT DUPLEX"]
    A4["SIRNA DUPLEX LOADED ONTO RISC LOADING COMPLEX DICER TRBP AGO2"]
    A5["ATP DEPENDENT STRAND UNWINDING"]
    A6["5 PRIME END RULE GUIDE STRAND SELECTION A OR U RICH 5 PRIME"]
    A7["PASSENGER STRAND EJECTION AND DEGRADATION BY C3PO"]
    A8["MATURE RISC ASSEMBLED AGO2 PLUS GUIDE STRAND"]
    A9["RISC SCANS CYTOPLASMIC MRNAS FOR COMPLEMENTARITY"]
    A10["AGO2 SLICER CLEAVAGE BETWEEN NT 10 AND 11 OF GUIDE"]
    A11["MRNA FRAGMENTS DEGRADED BY XRN1 AND EXOSOME"]
    A12["GENE EXPRESSION SILENCED"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10 --> A11 --> A12

    B1["OFF TARGET SEED MATCH REPRESSION"] -.-> A9
    B2["RISC SATURATION COMPETES WITH ENDOGENOUS MIRNA"] -.-> A8
    B3["PKR AND IFN RESPONSE IF DSRNA LONGER THAN 30 BP"] -.-> A1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — miRNA Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["PRIMARY MIRNA TRANSCRIBED BY RNA POLYMERASE II"]
    M2["PRI MIRNA CAPPED POLYADENYLATED 80 NT HAIRPIN PLUS FLANKS"]
    M3["MICROPROCESSOR COMPLEX DROSHAA PLUS DGCR8 IN NUCLEUS"]
    M4["PRE MIRNA 70 NT HAIRPIN WITH 2 NT 3 PRIME OVERHANG"]
    M5["EXPORTIN 5 PLUS RAN GTP TRANSPORTS PRE MIRNA TO CYTOPLASM"]
    M6["DICER PLUS TRBP TRIMS LOOP GENERATES 22 NT MIRNA DUPLEX"]
    M7["MIRNA DUPLEX LOADED ONTO AGO1 AGO2 AGO3 OR AGO4"]
    M8["STRAND SELECTION GUIDE STRAND RETAINED PASSENGER EJECTED"]
    M9["MIRISC SCANS 3 PRIME UTR OF TARGET MRNA"]
    M10["SEED REGION NT 2 TO 8 BINDS TARGET WITH IMPERFECT PAIRING"]
    M11["GW182 TNRC6 RECRUITED TO COMPLEX"]
    M12["CCR4 NOT DEADENYLASE AND DDX6 RECRUITED"]
    M13["P BODY SEQUESTRATION AND TRANSLATIONAL REPRESSION"]
    M14["MRNA DESTABILISED AND SILENCED"]

    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6 --> M7 --> M8 --> M9 --> M10 --> M11 --> M12 --> M13 --> M14
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — siRNA vs miRNA Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q["TRIGGER MOLECULE DETECTED"] --> Q1{"PERFECT DUPLEX EXOGENOUS"}
    Q --> Q2{"IMPERFECT HAIRPIN ENDOGENOUS"}

    Q1 --> R1["DICER ONLY PROCESSING"]
    R1 --> R2["SIRNA PATHWAY"]
    R2 --> R3["AGO2 SLICER CLEAVAGE OF SINGLE TARGET"]

    Q2 --> S1["DROSHA PLUS DGCR8 NUCLEAR"]
    S1 --> S2["DICER CYTOPLASMIC"]
    S2 --> S3["MIRNA PATHWAY"]
    S3 --> S4["MULTIPLE TARGETS TRANSLATIONAL REPRESSION"]
```

## 4.4 Argonaute Domain Architecture (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["N TERMINUS OF ARGONAUTE"] --> N2["N DOMAIN"]
    N2 --> N3["MID DOMAIN ANCHORS 5 PRIME PHOSPHATE OF GUIDE"]
    N3 --> N4["PAZ DOMAIN ANCHORS 3 PRIME END OF GUIDE"]
    N4 --> N5["PIWI DOMAIN SLICER CATALYTIC CENTER ASP ASP HIS PLUS MG 2 PLUS"]
```

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU board exam, even a simple hand-drawn block diagram with the **five labelled arrows** of the siRNA pathway and the **two extra nuclear steps** of the miRNA pathway (Drosha, Exportin-5) is worth at least 4–5 marks out of 14.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define RNA interference. Name the scientists who discovered it and the year of the discovery.

**Model Answer:**

> **RNA interference (RNAi)** is a conserved, post-transcriptional gene-silencing mechanism in which double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) triggers the sequence-specific degradation of homologous mRNA, leading to silencing of gene expression.
>
> It was discovered by **Andrew Fire** and **Craig C. Mello** in **1998** in the nematode *Caenorhabditis elegans* (work published in *Nature*, 1998). They were awarded the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2006** for this discovery.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for discovery, 1 for Nobel context.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` Distinguish between siRNA and miRNA in four points.

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | siRNA | miRNA |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Origin | Exogenous / viral / dsRNA | Endogenous genomic loci |
| 2 | Complementarity | Perfect / near-perfect to target | Imperfect (seed match only) |
| 3 | Function | Endonucleolytic cleavage of mRNA | Translational repression, deadenylation |
| 4 | Targets | Single specific mRNA | Multiple mRNAs (pleiotropic) |

*(3 marks: 0.75 each well-articulated point.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the mechanism of the **siRNA-mediated RNA interference pathway** in mammals. Mention the role of Dicer, RISC, and Argonaute 2. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the biogenesis and mechanism of action of **siRNA and miRNA**. Add a note on the **therapeutic application of RNAi**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — siRNA Mechanism

1. **Trigger entry** (1 mark): Long dsRNA or experimentally introduced 21–23 nt siRNA duplex enters the cytoplasm. Long dsRNA in mammals is cleaved by Dicer to avoid the IFN response.
2. **Dicer processing** (2 marks): Dicer (RNase III endonuclease) cleaves dsRNA into 21–23 nt duplexes with 2-nt 3′ overhangs and 5′-phosphate. Dicer has a PAZ domain that measures length, two RNase III catalytic centres that cleave, and a helicase domain for translocation.
3. **RISC loading** (2 marks): The siRNA duplex is loaded onto the **RISC Loading Complex (RLC)** consisting of Dicer, TRBP, and **Argonaute 2 (Ago2)**. ATP is required.
4. **Strand selection & activation** (1 mark): The passenger (sense) strand is removed by Ago2's slicer activity or by C3PO-mediated degradation. The strand with the A/U-rich 5′ end becomes the guide.
5. **Target cleavage** (1 mark): The mature RISC (Ago2 + guide) binds perfectly matched mRNA; Ago2's PIWI domain cleaves between nt 10 and 11 of the guide, leading to mRNA degradation by Xrn1 and the exosome.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — siRNA vs miRNA + Therapeutic Application

- **Biogenesis** (2 marks): siRNA is processed by Dicer from perfect dsRNA. miRNA is transcribed by Pol II as pri-miRNA, cleaved in the nucleus by Drosha + DGCR8 to pre-miRNA, exported by Exportin-5, then trimmed in the cytoplasm by Dicer.
- **Mechanism** (2 marks): siRNA binds its target with perfect complementarity and Ago2 cleaves the mRNA. miRNA binds 3′ UTR of mRNA with imperfect complementarity, requiring only the seed region (nt 2–8); recruits GW182 → CCR4-NOT → deadenylation and translational repression.
- **Number of targets** (1 mark): siRNA has a single target; miRNA has multiple targets.
- **Therapeutic application** (2 marks): Mention **Patisiran (Onpattro®, 2018)**, the first FDA-approved RNAi drug, used for hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis (hATTR). Delivered via lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) targeting hepatocytes; silences the *TTR* gene in the liver. Mention other approved drugs: **Givosiran, Lumasiran, Inclisiran**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the **biogenesis and mechanism of action of miRNA** in mammalian cells. Highlight the roles of **Drosha, Exportin-5, Dicer, and RISC**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a note on the **physiological significance of RNA interference** and the **off-target effects** that complicate its therapeutic use. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — miRNA Biogenesis and Mechanism

1. **Transcription** (1 mark): RNA Pol II transcribes **pri-miRNA** (capped, polyadenylated, $\sim$80 nt hairpin with flanking regions).
2. **Nuclear processing** (1.5 marks): The **Microprocessor complex** (Drosha + DGCR8) cleaves pri-miRNA $\rightarrow$ **pre-miRNA** ($\sim$70 nt hairpin with 2-nt 3′ overhang).
3. **Export** (1 mark): **Exportin-5** with **Ran-GTP** exports pre-miRNA to the cytoplasm; the 2-nt 3′ overhang is the recognition motif.
4. **Cytoplasmic processing** (1 mark): **Dicer** + **TRBP** trim the loop $\rightarrow$ $\sim$22 nt miRNA duplex.
5. **RISC loading & target repression** (2.5 marks): The mature **miRISC** (Ago1/2/3/4 + guide miRNA) binds 3′ UTR of target mRNA via the seed region (nt 2–8). **GW182** is recruited, which in turn recruits **CCR4-NOT** (deadenylation) and **DDX6** (P-body sequestration, translational repression). mRNA is deadenylated, translationally silenced, and sequestered in P-bodies.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Physiological Significance & Off-Target Effects

- **Physiological significance** (3.5 marks):
  - **Antiviral defence** — first discovered as plant antiviral mechanism; siRNA degrades viral RNA.
  - **Transposon silencing** — piRNA pathway in the germline silences mobile genetic elements.
  - **Developmental timing** — *C. elegans lin-4* and *let-7* miRNAs control larval stage transitions.
  - **Cell differentiation and metabolism** — miR-122 regulates liver metabolism; miR-1 regulates muscle differentiation.
  - **Genome-wide regulation** — $\sim$30% of human genes are estimated to be miRNA-regulated.
  - **Epigenetic feedback** — RNAi can direct heterochromatin formation (fission yeast, plants).

- **Off-target effects** (3.5 marks):
  - **Seed-mediated off-targeting** — siRNA guide strands can bind 3′ UTRs of unintended mRNAs sharing partial seed complementarity, acting like miRNAs.
  - **RISC saturation** — Excess shRNA/siRNA out-competes endogenous miRNAs for Ago, perturbing natural miRNA networks and causing hepatotoxicity (noted in first-generation shRNA trials).
  - **Innate immune activation** — Longer dsRNA triggers PKR and IFN response; even shorter siRNA can activate Toll-like receptors (TLR3, TLR7/8) depending on the delivery vehicle and sequence motifs (e.g., GU-rich, 5′-UGUGU-3′).
  - **Mitigations** — chemical modifications (2′-O-Me, 2′-F, phosphorothioate), seed-region BLAST, dose titration, GalNAc-conjugation for hepatocyte targeting.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in RNA Interference questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the order of Drosha vs Dicer** in the miRNA pathway. **Mnemonic:** "Drosha = **N**ucleus (alphabetically earlier than Dicer's **C**ytoplasm)." Drosha acts FIRST in the nucleus, then pre-miRNA is exported, THEN Dicer acts in the cytoplasm.
> 2. **Forgetting Exportin-5** as the transporter of pre-miRNA. Many students skip this step entirely.
> 3. **Stating "miRNAs never cause cleavage"** — this is *almost* true in mammals but **false in plants** and **false in engineered miRNA-mimic designs**. Examiners specifically test this nuance.
> 4. **Drawing the diagram without labelling the enzymes on the arrows**. A flow diagram with bare arrows and no enzyme names = **2–3 marks deducted**.
> 5. **Mixing up passenger and guide strands**. The guide is the one retained in RISC; it is the **antisense / complementary** strand to the target mRNA.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Nobel Prize / Fire and Mello discovery** when the question asks "what is RNAi." Contextual history is rewarded.
> 7. **Owing the answer in essay form without a diagram**. Always include a labelled flow diagram in 14-mark questions — it typically carries 3–4 marks on its own.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **RNAi = post-transcriptional gene silencing** triggered by dsRNA; discovered by **Fire & Mello (1998)**, Nobel 2006.
- **siRNA** = 21–23 nt, perfect complementarity, Ago2 slicer cleavage, single target, exogenous origin.
- **miRNA** = 20–24 nt, imperfect (seed 2–8), translational repression + deadenylation, multiple targets, endogenous origin.
- **Pathway order (siRNA):** dsRNA $\rightarrow$ **Dicer** $\rightarrow$ siRNA duplex $\rightarrow$ **RISC loading (Dicer/TRBP/Ago2)** $\rightarrow$ **strand selection (5′-A/U rule)** $\rightarrow$ **RISC + guide** $\rightarrow$ **Ago2 cleavage (nt 10–11)** $\rightarrow$ mRNA degraded by **Xrn1** and **exosome**.
- **Pathway order (miRNA):** pri-miRNA (Pol II) $\rightarrow$ **Drosha + DGCR8** (nucleus) $\rightarrow$ pre-miRNA $\rightarrow$ **Exportin-5 + Ran-GTP** $\rightarrow$ **Dicer + TRBP** (cytoplasm) $\rightarrow$ miRNA duplex $\rightarrow$ **miRISC (Ago1/2/3/4)** $\rightarrow$ **seed match to 3′ UTR** $\rightarrow$ **GW182 / CCR4-NOT / DDX6** $\rightarrow$ **P-body, deadenylation, translational repression**.
- **Argonaute architecture:** N, MID (5′-P binding), PAZ (3′-end binding), PIWI (slicer, Asp-Asp-His + Mg²⁺).
- **Dicer architecture:** DEAD-box helicase, DUF283, PAZ, two RNase III, dsRBD; yields 21–23 nt duplexes with 2-nt 3′ overhangs and 5′-P.
- **Mammalian length rule:** dsRNA $\geq$ **30 bp** triggers **PKR / IFN response**; use 21–23 nt siRNA to bypass.
- **First FDA-approved RNAi drug:** **Patisiran (2018)** for hATTR amyloidosis; LNP delivery; targets *TTR* in hepatocytes.
- **Other approved RNAi drugs:** Givosiran (AHP), Lumasiran (PH1), Inclisiran (hypercholesterolemia — GalNAc-conjugated, targets *PCSK9*).
- **Off-target pitfalls:** seed-mediated repression, RISC saturation, innate immune activation.
- **Mitigations:** chemical modifications (2′-O-Me, 2′-F), seed BLAST, GalNAc conjugation, dose titration.
- **Key co-factors:** TRBP, PACT, GW182 (TNRC6), CCR4-NOT, DDX6, C3PO, Xrn1, exosome.
- **Plant/C. elegans special feature:** **RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RDR)** enables **transitive siRNA amplification** — absent in mammals.
- **Comparative number:** $\sim$**30% of human genes** are regulated by miRNAs; a single miRNA can target **hundreds of mRNAs**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam mnemonic — "DDEPA" for miRNA pathway:**
> **D**rosha $\rightarrow$ **D**icer $\rightarrow$ **E**xportin-5 (between them) $\rightarrow$ **P**rocessing in cytoplasm $\rightarrow$ **A**go-RISC repression.
> (Technically Exportin-5 acts between Drosha and Dicer — remember it slots *between* the two D's.)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MicroRNA and Regulation of mRNA Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT402 - MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MicroRNA and Regulation of mRNA Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 MicroRNA (miRNA) – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MicroRNAs (miRNAs)** are a class of small, evolutionarily conserved, single-stranded **non-coding RNA (ncRNA) molecules** approximately **~22 nucleotides (nt) in length** that function as post-transcriptional regulators of gene expression. They mediate their effects by base-pairing (often with partial complementarity in animals) to the 3′ untranslated region (3′ UTR) of target messenger RNAs (mRNAs), leading to translational repression and/or mRNA degradation.

miRNAs are encoded in the genome (often in introns of protein-coding genes or as independent transcription units) and are transcribed by **RNA Polymerase II** as long primary transcripts called **pri-miRNAs**, which are processed stepwise into mature, functional miRNAs.

## 1.2 mRNA Stability – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **mRNA stability** (also called mRNA half-life, $t_{1/2}$) refers to the **average lifetime of a messenger RNA molecule** in the cell from the moment it is transcribed until it is degraded. It is a critical determinant of gene expression because the longer an mRNA persists, the more protein it can produce per transcript.

The decay of most eukaryotic mRNAs begins with **deadenylation** (shortening of the 3′ poly-A tail), followed by **decapping** (removal of the 5′ 7-methylguanosine cap) and rapid **5′→3′ exonucleolytic degradation** by XRN1, or alternatively **3′→5′ exonucleolytic degradation** by the exosome complex.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Library Book System

Imagine the cell as a **library** and mRNAs as **recipe books** that can be photocopied to make proteins (the "meals"):

- **A regular mRNA** is a recipe book that circulates freely, gets copied many times, then is eventually returned and shredded after a set period (this is its **stability** or *half-life*).
- **A microRNA (miRNA)** is a **librarian's red marker stamp** that marks certain recipe books as "do not photocopy" — it does not destroy the book immediately, but it stops the kitchen (ribosome) from reading it efficiently, and eventually routes the marked book for shredding.
- **RNA-binding proteins (RBPs)** that bind the 3′ UTR are like **bookmark stickers** — some are "**stability bookmarks**" (HuR) that protect the book from shredding, while others are "**disposal tags**" (AUF1, TTP, KSRP) that send it to the shredder faster.
- **AU-rich elements (AREs)** in the 3′ UTR are the **"tear-here" perforated edges** on the recipe book cover — proteins that recognize these tags either stabilize or destabilize the mRNA.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The 3′ UTR is the *master regulatory hub* of mRNA fate. It is the docking station for miRNAs, RBPs, and destabilizing elements — collectively called **cis-regulatory elements** in the RNA.

## 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants in the Field

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Mature miRNA length | **~21–23 nt (avg. 22 nt)** | Final functional size after Dicer cleavage |
| Pre-miRNA length | **~70–90 nt** | Stem-loop hairpin exported from nucleus |
| Pri-miRNA length | **Several hundred nt to >1 kb** | Initial Pol II transcript |
| Average eukaryotic mRNA half-life | **~10 minutes to >24 hours** (median ~10 h in mammals) | Determines protein output per mRNA |
| Typical ARE consensus | **AUUUA pentamer** (often in U-rich context) | Binding site for ARE-BPs |
| Seed region of miRNA | **nt 2–8 from 5′ end** | Critical for target recognition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biogenesis of miRNAs – The Canonical Pathway

The maturation of a miRNA is a multi-step, compartmentalized process. The canonical pathway proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Nuclear Transcription
- The **pri-miRNA** is transcribed by **RNA Polymerase II** in the nucleus.
- It contains a local **stem-loop (hairpin) structure** of ~33 bp flanked by single-stranded segments.
- The pri-miRNA is **5′ capped** (7-methylguanosine) and **3′ polyadenylated**, identical to a typical mRNA.

### Step 2: Microprocessor Cleavage (Nuclear)
- The **Microprocessor complex** (consisting of the RNase III enzyme **Drosha** + its cofactor **DGCR8/Pasha**) recognizes the stem-loop.
- Drosha cleaves the pri-miRNA ~11 nt from the base of the hairpin, releasing a **pre-miRNA** (~70 nt hairpin with a 2-nt 3′ overhang).

### Step 3: Nuclear Export
- The pre-miRNA is exported from the nucleus to the cytoplasm by **Exportin-5 (XPO5)** in a Ran-GTP-dependent manner.
- The 2-nt 3′ overhang and the double-stranded stem are recognized by Exportin-5.

### Step 4: Cytoplasmic Processing by Dicer
- In the cytoplasm, the **RNase III enzyme Dicer**, often with the cofactor **TRBP**, recognizes the pre-miRNA.
- Dicer cleaves ~22 nt from the terminal loop, producing a **miRNA duplex** (~22 nt) with 2-nt 3′ overhangs on both ends.

### Step 5: Strand Selection and RISC Loading
- The miRNA duplex is loaded onto **Argonaute (Ago) proteins** by the **RISC (RNA-Induced Silencing Complex) Loading Complex (RLC)**.
- The strand with the **less stable 5′ base pairing** (the **guide strand / miRNA**) is retained; the other (**passenger strand / miRNA\***) is discarded (or sometimes functional in the opposite direction).
- The mature **miRISC** contains a single-stranded miRNA loaded into Ago, ready to scan target mRNAs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two strands?** Thermodynamic asymmetry at the 5′ ends of the duplex dictates which strand is loaded — this is the **"asymmetric rule"** of miRNA strand selection.

## 2.2 Mechanism of miRNA-Mediated Gene Silencing

Once loaded into Ago, the miRISC performs its regulatory function via three main mechanisms:

### Mechanism A: Translational Repression
- miRISC inhibits the **initiation step** of translation (the most common mode in animals).
- It competes with **eIF4E** for the 5′ cap by recruiting **eIF6**, preventing 60S ribosomal subunit joining.
- It may also recruit the **CCR4-NOT deadenylase complex** to shorten the poly-A tail, which in turn prevents the closed-loop structure (cap–poly(A)–PABP) needed for efficient translation.

### Mechanism B: mRNA Deadenylation and Decapping
- miRISC recruits the **GW182 (TNRC6) scaffold protein**, which in turn recruits the **CCR4-NOT deadenylase** and the **DCP1/DCP2 decapping complex**.
- Deadenylation → decapping → **5′→3′ exonucleolytic degradation by XRN1**.

### Mechanism C: mRNA Target Cleavage (rare in animals, common in plants)
- In animals, miRNAs bind with **partial complementarity** (seed match at positions 2–8).
- In plants, miRNAs bind with **near-perfect complementarity**, which activates the **endo-nucleolytic ("slicer") activity of Ago2**, leading to direct mRNA cleavage.

## 2.3 Regulation of mRNA Stability – Cis and Trans Elements

### A. AU-Rich Elements (AREs) – Cis-acting Destabilizers
- Found in the 3′ UTR of many short-lived mRNAs (e.g., cytokines, oncogenes, growth factors).
- Multiple copies of the **AUUUA pentamer** in a U-rich context.
- AREs recruit **destabilizing RNA-binding proteins (ARE-BPs)** that promote rapid deadenylation and decay.

### B. Major mRNA Destabilizing Proteins

| Protein | Full Name | Mode of Action |
|---|---|---|
| **TTP (Tristetraprolin)** | Zinc finger protein ZFP36 | Recruits deadenylase complex → rapid mRNA decay |
| **AUF1 (HNRNPD)** | AU-rich element RNA-binding protein 1 | Recruits exosome and/or PARN deadenylase |
| **KSRP (KHSRP)** | KH-type splicing regulatory protein | Recruits PARN, Drosha, and the exosome |
| **TIA-1 / TIAR** | T-cell-restricted intracellular antigen-1 | Sequesters mRNA in **stress granules** → translational silencing |
| **BRF1 / BRF2** | Butyrate response factors 1/2 | Recruit deadenylases, similar to TTP |

### C. Major mRNA Stabilizing Proteins

| Protein | Mode of Action |
|---|---|
| **HuR (ELAVL1)** | Binds AREs and **blocks** destabilizing protein access; also promotes nuclear export |
| **PCBP2** | Poly(C)-binding protein — stabilizes specific mRNAs |
| **PABP (Poly-A Binding Protein)** | Binds poly-A tail, interacts with eIF4G at the cap → circularizes mRNA, **protects from exonucleases** |
| **CELF1 (CUGBP1)** | Stabilizes specific mRNAs in muscle and embryonic tissues |

## 2.4 The 5′→3′ and 3′→5′ Decay Pathways

### A. 5′→3′ Decay (Major Pathway)
1. **Deadenylation** (CCR4-NOT, PARN) → poly-A tail shortens.
2. **Decapping** (DCP1/DCP2 + enhancers) → 5′ cap removed.
3. **5′→3′ exonucleolysis** by **XRN1**.

### B. 3′→5′ Decay (Exosome Pathway)
- After deadenylation, the **RNA exosome complex** (a multi-subunit 3′→5′ exonuclease) degrades the mRNA from the 3′ end.
- The **SUPV3L1/DIS3** catalytic subunit performs the actual hydrolysis.

## 2.5 P-Bodies and Stress Granules – Cellular Compartments of mRNA Regulation

- **Processing bodies (P-bodies)**: Cytoplasmic foci containing **decapping enzymes, deadenylases, XRN1, Ago2, GW182, miRNAs**. They are sites of mRNA storage, decay, and miRNA-mediated silencing.
- **Stress granules**: Form under stress (e.g., oxidative stress, heat shock); contain **TIA-1, TIAR, G3BP1, stalled translation initiation complexes**. mRNAs are sorted here for storage or re-initiation.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Units / Description |
|---|---|---|
| mRNA half-life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{decay}}$ | hours or minutes; $k_{decay}$ is the decay constant |
| Protein output (steady-state) | $P_{ss} = \dfrac{k_{translation} \cdot [mRNA]}{k_{protein\ decay}}$ | Arbitrary units |
| Fractional miRNA seed match | Wobble base-pairing at position 1, 2–8 seed | $G:U$ allowed at pos 1–2; seed: pos 2–8 |
| Deadenylation rate | $v = k_{deadenylase} \cdot [PABP-free\ polyA]$ | Bases/minute |
| Target repression | Fold-change = $\dfrac{1}{1 + (miRNA/M)^n}$ (Hill-like) | $M$ = EC50; $n$ = Hill coefficient |
| Pri-miRNA → pre-miRNA cut | Drosha cuts ~11 bp from stem–ss junction | Releases ~70 nt hairpin |
| Pre-miRNA → mature miRNA | Dicer cuts ~22 nt from terminal loop | Produces ~22 nt duplex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering / Real-world utility:** miRNA-based therapeutics (e.g., *miravirsen* for HCV targeting miR-122; *patisiran* as the first FDA-approved siRNA drug, 2018) and **mRNA stability engineering** (used in **mRNA vaccine design** — Pfizer-BioNTech & Moderna COVID-19 vaccines use modified nucleosides like N1-methylpseudouridine and codon optimization to extend mRNA half-life) — both rely on the principles discussed in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: mRNA Half-Life from Decay Kinetics

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** The decay of an mRNA population in the absence of new transcription follows **first-order kinetics**. We derive the relationship between half-life and the decay constant $k_{decay}$, which is central to quantitative gene-expression analysis.

### Step 1: Define the differential equation for first-order decay
For an mRNA population $M(t)$ at time $t$, the rate of disappearance is proportional to its concentration:

$$
\frac{dM(t)}{dt} = -k_{decay} \cdot M(t)
$$

where $k_{decay}$ has units of **time$^{-1}$** (e.g., min$^{-1}$ or h$^{-1}$).

### Step 2: Solve the separable ODE
Separate variables:

$$
\frac{dM}{M} = -k_{decay} \, dt
$$

### Step 3: Integrate both sides from $M_0$ (at $t=0$) to $M(t)$ (at time $t$)

$$
\int_{M_0}^{M(t)} \frac{dM}{M} = -k_{decay} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integrals

$$
\ln M(t) - \ln M_0 = -k_{decay} \cdot t
$$

$$
\ln\left( \frac{M(t)}{M_0} \right) = -k_{decay} \cdot t
$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate to obtain the explicit solution

$$
\frac{M(t)}{M_0} = e^{-k_{decay} \cdot t}
$$

$$
\boxed{M(t) = M_0 \cdot e^{-k_{decay} \cdot t}}
$$

### Step 6: Define half-life as the time when $M(t) = M_0/2$

$$
\frac{M_0}{2} = M_0 \cdot e^{-k_{decay} \cdot t_{1/2}}
$$

### Step 7: Cancel $M_0$ and solve

$$
\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k_{decay} \cdot t_{1/2}}
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k_{decay} \cdot t_{1/2}
$$

$$
-\ln 2 = -k_{decay} \cdot t_{1/2}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{decay}} \approx \frac{0.693}{k_{decay}}}
$$

### Step 8: Worked Numerical Example
Suppose a cytokine mRNA (e.g., TNF-α) has $k_{decay} = 0.1\ \text{min}^{-1}$ under resting conditions.

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.1} = 6.93\ \text{min}
$$

So ~93% of the mRNA is gone in 30 minutes. In contrast, a stable mRNA like β-globin with $k_{decay} = 0.001\ \text{min}^{-1}$ would have:

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.001} = 693\ \text{min} \approx 11.55\ \text{hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 100-fold difference in $k_{decay}$ produces a 100-fold difference in half-life and a corresponding 100-fold difference in protein output per transcript — a powerful amplification of gene expression control.

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State mRNA Level and Protein Output

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A cell's mRNA is a balance between synthesis (transcription) and degradation. We derive the steady-state mRNA concentration and the steady-state protein output.

### Step 1: Mass balance for mRNA
Let $k_{syn}$ = rate of transcription (mRNA/min) and $k_{decay}$ = first-order decay rate.

$$
\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_{syn} - k_{decay} \cdot [M]
$$

### Step 2: At steady state, $d[M]/dt = 0$

$$
0 = k_{syn} - k_{decay} \cdot [M]_{ss}
$$

$$
\boxed{[M]_{ss} = \frac{k_{syn}}{k_{decay}}}
$$

### Step 3: Steady-state protein concentration
Similarly, for protein (translation rate $k_{tl}$ per mRNA, and protein decay $k_{P}$):

$$
[P]_{ss} = \frac{k_{tl} \cdot [M]_{ss}}{k_{P}} = \frac{k_{tl} \cdot k_{syn}}{k_{P} \cdot k_{decay}}
$$

### Step 4: Worked Example – miRNA Effect
Consider a target mRNA with $k_{syn} = 100\ \text{molecules/min}$ and $k_{decay} = 0.01\ \text{min}^{-1}$ (no miRNA). Then:

$$
[M]_{ss} = \frac{100}{0.01} = 10{,}000\ \text{molecules}
$$

If a miRNA RISC complex is recruited and accelerates deadenylation such that $k_{decay}$ becomes $0.1\ \text{min}^{-1}$ (10× faster), then:

$$
[M]_{ss} = \frac{100}{0.1} = 1{,}000\ \text{molecules}
$$

This is a **10-fold reduction in mRNA abundance**, demonstrating how miRNAs quantitatively repress gene expression.

## 3.3 Worked Example – Quantifying miRNA Targeting by Seed Complementarity

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A researcher is testing whether **miR-21** (a well-known oncomiR) targets the 3′ UTR of **PTEN** (a tumor suppressor mRNA). They need to identify a canonical seed-match site.

### Step 1: Identify the mature miR-21 sequence
- Mature miR-21 (human, miRBase): **5′–UAGCUUAUCAGACUGAUGUUGA–3′** (22 nt).

### Step 2: Extract the seed region (positions 2–8 from 5′ end)
- Seed of miR-21: **5′–AGCUUAU–3′** (7 nt).

### Step 3: Predict the target site on the mRNA
- The target mRNA must contain a sequence **complementary and antiparallel** to the seed, i.e., on the mRNA: **5′–AUAAGCU–3′** (Watson–Crick base pairing: A:U, G:C, etc., with G:U wobble allowed at positions 1–2).

### Step 4: Verify match context
- A 7-mer match of **AUAAGCU** in the PTEN 3′ UTR is a strong canonical site. The mammalian PTEN 3′ UTR is ~3,070 nt long and contains a verified miR-21 site.

### Step 5: Predict biological outcome
- miR-21 binding → recruitment of GW182 → deadenylation and translational repression → **reduced PTEN protein** → activation of PI3K/AKT pathway → pro-survival signaling (a hallmark of many cancers).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Distinguishing Features at a Glance

| Feature | Plant miRNAs | Animal miRNAs |
|---|---|---|
| Target complementarity | Near-perfect | Partial (seed match) |
| Endonuclease ("slicer") activity | Yes (Ago-mediated cleavage) | Rare (Ago2 only) |
| Genomic location | Mostly intergenic | Mostly intronic |
| Number of targets per miRNA | Few (high specificity) | Hundreds (one miRNA can repress >100 mRNAs) |
| Predominant mechanism | mRNA cleavage | Translational repression + deadenylation |
| Target sites | Coding region + 3′ UTR | Almost exclusively 3′ UTR |
| 3′ complementarity (compensatory) | Required | Not required |
| Example | miR-172 (Arabidopsis) | miR-21 (Homo sapiens) |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of miRNA Action vs. siRNA Action

| Feature | miRNA | siRNA |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Endogenous (genome-encoded) | Exogenous (dsRNA, transposons, viruses, lab-introduced) |
| Precursor | Imperfect stem-loop (pre-miRNA) | Perfect long dsRNA |
| Dicer dependence | Yes | Yes |
| Strand selection | Thermodynamic asymmetry | Both strands can be functional |
| Ago protein loaded | Mainly Ago1/2/3 in mammals | Mainly Ago2 |
| Target complementarity | Partial (animals) | Perfect |
| Outcome | Repression/cleavage (species-dependent) | Robust cleavage |
| Therapeutic use | Indirect (target miRNA) | Direct gene knockdown (patisiran, givosiran) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 – Canonical miRNA Biogenesis Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DNA Genome - MIR gene] --> B[RNA Pol II Transcription]
    B --> C[Pri-miRNA - capped and polyadenylated hairpin]
    C --> D[Nuclear Microprocessor Complex]
    D --> D1[Drosha RNase III]
    D --> D2[DGCR8 cofactor]
    D1 --> E[Pre-miRNA - 70 nt hairpin with 2 nt 3' overhang]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Exportin-5 + Ran-GTP]
    F --> G[Cytoplasmic Dicer Processing]
    G --> G1[Dicer RNase III]
    G --> G2[TRBP cofactor]
    G1 --> H[miRNA Duplex - 22 nt with 2 nt 3' overhangs]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[RISC Loading Complex]
    I --> J[Argonaute Protein Binding]
    J --> K[Guide strand retained - mature miRISC]
    J --> L[Passenger strand miRNA asterisk - discarded or complementary function]
    K --> M[Target mRNA Recognition via seed 2 to 8 nt]
    M --> N[Gene Silencing - translation repression and deadenylation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 – mRNA Stability Regulatory Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Nucleus
        DNA[DNA - Gene]
        Pol[RNA Pol II]
        Pre-mRNA[Pre-mRNA]
        Mature-mRNA-Nuc[Mature mRNA in nucleus]
        DNA --> Pol --> Pre-mRNA
        Pre-mRNA --> Splicing[Splicing and 3' end processing]
        Splicing --> Mature-mRNA-Nuc
    end

    Mature-mRNA-Nuc --> Export[Nuclear Export - NXF1 TAP]

    subgraph Cytoplasm
        mRNA-Cyt[Translating mRNA]
        5Cap[5' m7G Cap]
        3UTR[3' UTR with AREs and miRNA sites]
        3PolyA[3' Poly-A Tail]

        mRNA-Cyt --- 5Cap
        mRNA-Cyt --- 3UTR
        mRNA-Cyt --- 3PolyA

        HuR-Protein[HuR - stabilizing RBP]
        AUF1-Protein[AUF1 - destabilizing RBP]
        TTP-Protein[TTP - destabilizing RBP]
        miRISC[miRISC - Ago plus miRNA]

        HuR-Protein -. binds 3' UTR .-> 3UTR
        AUF1-Protein -. binds AREs .-> 3UTR
        TTP-Protein -. binds AREs .-> 3UTR
        miRISC -. binds seed match .-> 3UTR
    end

    Export --> mRNA-Cyt

    mRNA-Cyt -->|Translational repression| Silent-mRNA[Silenced mRNA]
    Silent-mRNA --> P-body[P-body storage and decay]
    P-body --> Deadenylase[CCR4-NOT and PARN deadenylases]
    Deadenylase --> Decapping-enzymes[DCP1 DCP2 decapping complex]
    Decapping-enzymes --> XRN1[XRN1 5' to 3' exonuclease]
    XRN1 --> Fragments[Nucleotide monophosphates - recycled]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 – Decision Tree: miRNA vs. siRNA Functional Outcome

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[dsRNA or hairpin precursor detected in cytoplasm] --> DicerProcess[Dicer and TRBP cleavage]
    DicerProcess --> Duplex[22 nt duplex generated]
    Duplex --> Loading[Ago protein loading]
    Loading --> StrandSel{Perfect or imperfect duplex?}

    StrandSel -->|Perfect dsRNA| siRNA[siRNA pathway - Ago2 slicer active]
    StrandSel -->|Imperfect hairpin| miRNA[miRNA pathway - Ago1 dominant]

    siRNA --> PerfectMatch[Perfect complementarity to target]
    PerfectMatch --> Slicer[Ago2 endonuclease cleavage]
    Slicer --> Result1[Robust mRNA degradation]

    miRNA --> SeedMatch[Seed 2 to 8 nt partial match to 3' UTR]
    SeedMatch --> GW182Recruit[GW182 TNRC6 scaffold recruitment]
    GW182Recruit --> DeadPathway[CCR4-NOT deadenylase and DCP1 DCP2 decapping]
    GW182Recruit --> TransRepress[Translational inhibition via 5' cap and 60S blocking]
    DeadPathway --> Result2[mRNA destabilization and P-body localization]
    TransRepress --> Result2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 – Block Architecture of the miRISC Effector Complex

flowchart TB
    subgraph miRISC_Effector
        A[Argonaute Protein Ago]
        B[miRNA guide strand - 22 nt]

        F[XRN1 5' to 3' Exonuclease]
        G[eIF6 - blocks 60S joining]
    end

    A --- B

    A -. recognizes seed match .-> Target_mRNA[Target mRNA 3' UTR]
    Target_mRNA --> PolyA[Poly-A tail shortening by D]
    PolyA --> Cap_removal[Cap removal by E]
    Cap_removal --> XRN1_deg[Degradation by F]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define microRNA. Mention its length and the enzyme that processes the precursor.
**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A microRNA (miRNA) is a small, single-stranded, non-coding RNA of approximately **~22 nucleotides (nt)** in length that regulates gene expression post-transcriptionally. It is transcribed by **RNA Polymerase II** as a **pri-miRNA**, which is processed in the nucleus by the **Drosha–DGCR8 Microprocessor** complex into a **pre-miRNA (~70 nt hairpin)**, and then in the cytoplasm by **Dicer** (with TRBP) into a mature ~22 nt miRNA duplex. One strand (the guide) is loaded into **Argonaute (Ago)** to form the **miRISC** effector complex, which targets the 3′ UTR of specific mRNAs to induce translational repression and/or mRNA degradation.

### Question 2: What are AU-rich elements (AREs)? Name two proteins that bind to them and state their role.
**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **AU-rich elements (AREs)** are **cis-acting regulatory sequences** found in the 3′ UTRs of many short-lived mRNAs (e.g., cytokines, proto-oncogenes). They are characterized by multiple copies of the **AUUUA pentamer** within a U-rich context. AREs recruit **RNA-binding proteins (ARE-BPs)**. **Stabilizing proteins** (e.g., **HuR**) bind AREs to protect mRNAs from decay, while **destabilizing proteins** (e.g., **TTP, AUF1, KSRP**) bind to promote rapid deadenylation and exonucleolytic degradation. The balance of these opposing activities determines the cytoplasmic half-life of the mRNA.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
> (a) Describe the canonical biogenesis pathway of microRNAs in animals, naming the key enzymes and the cellular compartments involved. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Explain the mechanisms by which mature miRISC complexes regulate the stability and translation of target mRNAs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Nuclear step — Pri-miRNA synthesis:** The miRNA-encoding gene (MIR) is transcribed by **RNA Polymerase II** in the nucleus to yield a long primary transcript called the **pri-miRNA**, which contains a local stem–loop hairpin and is **5′ capped** and **3′ polyadenylated**. (1 mark)

2. **Microprocessor cleavage — Pri- to Pre-miRNA:** In the nucleus, the **Microprocessor complex** (composed of the **RNase III enzyme Drosha** and its double-stranded-RNA-binding cofactor **DGCR8/Pasha**) recognizes the stem–ss junction and cleaves ~11 bp from the base of the hairpin, releasing a **pre-miRNA** of ~70 nt with a characteristic **2-nt 3′ overhang**. (2 marks)

3. **Nuclear export:** The pre-miRNA is recognized by **Exportin-5 (XPO5)** in cooperation with **Ran-GTP**, and is exported through the nuclear pore to the cytoplasm. The 2-nt 3′ overhang is a key recognition feature. (1 mark)

4. **Cytoplasmic processing by Dicer:** In the cytoplasm, the **RNase III enzyme Dicer** (with cofactor **TRBP/PACT**) cleaves the pre-miRNA ~22 nt from the terminal loop, yielding a **miRNA duplex** (~22 nt) with 2-nt 3′ overhangs at both ends. (2 marks)

5. **RISC loading and strand selection:** The duplex is loaded onto an **Argonaute (Ago) protein** by the RISC Loading Complex. The strand whose 5′ end has **less thermodynamic stability** is retained as the **guide (mature) miRNA**, while the other strand (the **passenger / miRNA\***) is unwound and discarded (or sometimes functional). The mature complex is called **miRISC**. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **mRNA target recognition:** The miRISC scans the 3′ UTRs of potential target mRNAs. Recognition is mediated primarily by the **seed region (positions 2–8 from the 5′ end of the miRNA)** which forms Watson–Crick base pairs with a complementary site in the 3′ UTR. Additional compensatory pairing at the 3′ end may enhance targeting. (2 marks)

2. **Translational repression:** In animal cells, miRISC (via Ago and the **GW182/TNRC6** scaffold) inhibits translation at the **initiation step** by: (i) recruiting **eIF6** to prevent 60S ribosomal subunit joining; (ii) interfering with the **5′ cap recognition by eIF4E**; and (iii) recruiting the **CCR4-NOT deadenylase complex** which disrupts the closed-loop mRNA structure (PABP–eIF4G interaction). (2 marks)

3. **mRNA destabilization (deadenylation and decay):** GW182 recruits the **CCR4–NOT** and **PARN** deadenylases, which shorten the poly-A tail. This is followed by recruitment of the **DCP1–DCP2 decapping complex** (with enhancers such as Edc3, Pat1, Lsm1-7), removing the 5′ cap. The deadenylated, decapped mRNA is then degraded by the **5′→3′ exonuclease XRN1** (or alternatively by the **3′→5′ exosome**). (2 marks)

4. **P-body localization:** Silenced mRNAs accumulate in cytoplasmic **processing bodies (P-bodies)**, which are foci rich in decapping enzymes, deadenylases, XRN1, Ago, and GW182. P-bodies serve as sites of mRNA storage, decay, and miRNA-mediated silencing. (1 mark)

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
> (a) Discuss the role of the 3′ UTR in regulating mRNA stability, with emphasis on **AU-rich elements (AREs)** and their associated RNA-binding proteins. Give two examples each of stabilizing and destabilizing proteins. **(7 marks)**
> (b) With suitable examples, compare the mechanisms of mRNA degradation via the **5′→3′** and **3′→5′** pathways. How does the **miRNA** pathway connect to these decay pathways? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. The **3′ UTR** is a major hub of post-transcriptional regulation. It contains cis-elements that recruit trans-acting RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) and miRNAs, which collectively determine the cytoplasmic half-life of the mRNA. (1 mark)

2. **AU-rich elements (AREs)** are the most extensively studied cis-elements in 3′ UTRs. They consist of one or more copies of the **AUUUA pentamer** embedded in a U-rich context. They are present in mRNAs encoding cytokines (TNF-α, IL-2, IL-6), proto-oncogenes (c-fos, c-myc), and growth factors. (2 marks)

3. **Destabilizing ARE-binding proteins** promote rapid mRNA decay:
   - **TTP (Tristetraprolin / ZFP36):** Recruits the **CCR4–NOT deadenylase complex** to ARE-containing mRNAs. Knockout of TTP in mice causes a severe inflammatory syndrome due to TNF-α mRNA stabilization.
   - **AUF1 (HNRNPD):** Recruits the exosome and/or PARN deadenylase to destabilize target mRNAs.
   - **KSRP (KHSRP):** Recruits PARN and the exosome; also interacts with Drosha.
   - **BRF1/BRF2:** Functionally similar to TTP. (2 marks)

4. **Stabilizing ARE-binding proteins** protect mRNAs from decay:
   - **HuR (ELAVL1):** The best-characterized stabilizing RBP. Binds AREs in the nucleus and accompanies the mRNA to the cytoplasm. HuR **competitively excludes** destabilizing proteins like TTP, and promotes translation. (Example: HuR stabilizes VEGF, cyclin-A, and p21 mRNAs.)
   - **PCBP2:** Poly(C)-binding protein 2 — stabilizes specific mRNAs.
   - **CELF1 (CUGBP1):** Stabilizes mRNAs in muscle and embryonic tissues. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **5′→3′ decay pathway (major pathway):** Begins with **deadenylation** of the poly-A tail by enzymes such as **CCR4-NOT** or **PARN**. The deadenylated mRNA is recognized by the **Lsm1-7/Pat1** complex, which activates the **DCP1–DCP2 decapping** machinery (assisted by enhancers like **Edc3, Dhh1, Edc4**). The exposed 5′ monophosphate is then processively degraded by the **5′→3′ exonuclease XRN1**. (3 marks)

2. **3′→5′ decay pathway (exosome pathway):** After deadenylation, the mRNA is degraded from the 3′ end by the **RNA exosome** — a multi-subunit complex of 3′→5′ exonucleases. The catalytic activity resides in the **DIS3 (SUPV3L1)** subunit, with **RRP6** as an additional nuclear exosome component. The exosome also cooperates with the **SKI complex** for cytoplasmic mRNA surveillance. (2 marks)

3. **Connection to the miRNA pathway:** The miRNA pathway converges on these decay mechanisms through the **GW182 (TNRC6)** scaffold. GW182 bound to Ago recruits the **CCR4–NOT deadenylase** (initiating 5′→3′ decay) and the **DCP1–DCP2 decapping complex**. Deadenylated, decapped mRNAs are degraded by XRN1. Thus, miRNA-mediated silencing mechanistically utilizes the **same deadenylation → decapping → 5′→3′ exonucleolysis** pipeline as bulk mRNA decay. In the **3′→5′ direction**, GW182 and miRISC can also feed target mRNAs into the exosome pathway. (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing Drosha and Dicer:** Drosha acts in the **nucleus** on **pri-miRNA**; Dicer acts in the **cytoplasm** on **pre-miRNA**. Swapping these will cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **miRNA vs. siRNA complementarity:** Students often state that "miRNAs cleave their targets like siRNAs." In **animals**, miRNAs bind with **partial complementarity** (seed match) and rarely cleave; cleavage is mainly a **plant** or **siRNA** phenomenon.
> 3. **3′ UTR vs. 5′ UTR:** The miRNA seed-match site is in the **3′ UTR** in animals. Mentioning 5′ UTR or coding region (without qualification) is a common error.
> 4. **Stabilizers vs. destabilizers:** Mixing up HuR (stabilizer) and TTP (destabilizer) is a high-frequency error. Remember: **HuR = protective, TTP = destructive.**
> 5. **Poly-A tail function:** Many students forget that the poly-A tail is required for **translation initiation** (via PABP–eIF4G circularization) — not just mRNA stability.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 4: MicroRNA and Regulation of mRNA Stability**

- [ ] **miRNA** = ~22 nt single-stranded non-coding RNA, transcribed by **Pol II**, processed by **Drosha** (nucleus) and **Dicer** (cytoplasm).
- [ ] **Canonical biogenesis:** Pri-miRNA → Pre-miRNA (Drosha/DGCR8) → miRNA duplex (Dicer/TRBP) → mature miRISC (Ago-loaded).
- [ ] **Exportin-5** + **Ran-GTP** exports pre-miRNA to the cytoplasm.
- [ ] **Strand selection** is governed by the **thermodynamic asymmetry** of the duplex (5′ end of the guide is less stably paired).
- [ ] **miRISC** = **Ago + mature miRNA + GW182 (TNRC6)** — the effector complex.
- [ ] **Seed region** = positions **2–8 from the 5′ end** of the miRNA; binds complementary site in the **3′ UTR** of the target mRNA.
- [ ] In **animals**, miRNA-target pairing is **partial** → translational repression + deadenylation, NOT cleavage.
- [ ] In **plants**, miRNA-target pairing is **near-perfect** → Ago slicer cleavage of target.
- [ ] **Mechanisms of silencing:** (1) **Translational inhibition** (cap/60S/eIF6); (2) **Deadenylation** (CCR4–NOT/PARN); (3) **Decapping** (DCP1/DCP2); (4) **5′→3′ decay** (XRN1); (5) **3′→5′ decay** (exosome/DIS3).
- [ ] **GW182 (TNRC6)** is the master scaffold that bridges Ago to the deadenylation and decapping machinery.
- [ ] **P-bodies** = cytoplasmic foci for storage and decay; contain Ago, GW182, decapping enzymes, XRN1.
- [ ] **AU-rich elements (AREs)** = AUUUA pentamers in 3′ UTRs of short-lived mRNAs (cytokines, oncogenes).
- [ ] **Stabilizing RBPs:** **HuR, PCBP2, CELF1** (HuR is the prototype).
- [ ] **Destabilizing RBPs:** **TTP, AUF1, KSRP, BRF1/2** (TTP is the prototype).
- [ ] **mRNA half-life formula:** $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{decay}} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{k_{decay}}$.
- [ ] **Steady-state mRNA:** $[M]_{ss} = \dfrac{k_{syn}}{k_{decay}}$ — decreasing $k_{decay}$ (faster decay) → lower mRNA steady state.
- [ ] **Therapeutic relevance:** mRNA stability engineering underlies **mRNA vaccine design** (Pfizer-BioNTech, Moderna COVID-19 vaccines); miRNAs are therapeutic targets (**miravirsen** for HCV) and siRNA drugs (**patisiran**, **givosiran**) are FDA-approved.
- [ ] **siRNA vs. miRNA:** siRNA = exogenous, perfect complementarity, Ago2 slicer; miRNA = endogenous, partial complementarity, Ago1-dominant.
- [ ] **miR-21** targeting **PTEN** is a clinically important example in oncology.
- [ ] **Decapping complex = DCP1 + DCP2**; **5′→3′ exonuclease = XRN1**; **3′→5′ exonuclease = exosome (DIS3)**; **Deadenylases = CCR4–NOT, PARN**.
- [ ] **Stress granules** (TIA-1, TIAR) vs. **P-bodies** (decapping, decay) — both are cytoplasmic RNA granules, but with different compositions and functions.
